meta.title: Hadji Murat
meta.expansion_note: Novel -> feature film: Compression needed. Cut subplots, merge characters, tighten.

style_manifest.tone: tragic and sober, epic distance with intimate moments; violence without glorification; moral ambivalence toward empire and resistance

Hadji Murad.physical_anchor: Tall, broad-shouldered Avar mountaineer of powerful but lean build, a shaven head under a tall sheepskin papakha and a close-trimmed reddish-black beard framing a slightly pock-marked face with quick, light-colored eyes; carries himself erect in a long cherkeska, moves with a pronounced limp from an old wound, and habitually rests a hand on the hilt of his kinjal or draws his palms down his beard after speaking.
Hadji Murad.vocal_quality: Low, dry baritone with a guttural Avar-Turkic accent, measured tempo and long pauses, formal in register and almost never raised — authority carried by stillness rather than volume.
Hadji Murad.social_presence: Enters a room quietly and immediately becomes its center of gravity: other men stand, voices drop, and even Russian officers feel themselves watched by a calm, unblinking warrior whose courtesy is exact and whose reputation does the intimidating for him.
Hadji Murad.historical_reference: Grounded in contemporary 1851–1852 Russian sources: the portrait and description by G. G. Gagarin, the watercolor sketches and notes of officers at Tiflis and Nukha, and the memoir accounts of A. I. Poltoratsky and M. T. Loris-Melikov (who interviewed him during his stay with the Russians). These sources consistently record a tall, powerfully built Avar of about forty-seven, a shaven head worn under a papakha, a short reddish beard, a slightly pock-marked face with pale, alert eyes, a marked limp from a wound sustained leaping from a cliff during his escape from Akhulgo, and a reserved, ceremoniously polite bearing.

Shamil.physical_anchor: Tall, lean, and upright for his age, with broad shoulders narrowing to a spare frame conditioned by decades of mountain warfare; a long reddish-auburn beard tinged with grey frames a pale, high-cheekboned face marked by a slight limp from old battle wounds. His posture is vertical and unmoving — hands folded before him or resting on the hilt of a shashka — and his habitual gesture is a slow, deliberate stroking of the beard before he speaks.
Shamil.vocal_quality: Low-pitched, resonant, and unhurried, a dry scholar's baritone that carries without volume and lands each syllable like a hammer tapping glass.
Shamil.social_presence: He enters a room and the room reorganizes around him — every other man stands, bows, or falls silent before he has spoken; his authority is sacred and absolute, and his stillness reads as menace held in reserve.
Shamil.historical_reference: Contemporary Russian, British and German accounts (A. Runovsky's diaries as Shamil's captor-companion 1859-1862; John Milton Mackie's 'Life of Schamyl' 1856; Moritz Wagner's travel reports; the few surviving portraits by Gorschelt and the 1859 Timm lithograph made after his surrender) describe him as tall, slender but powerfully built, fair-skinned for a Caucasian highlander, with grey-blue or hazel eyes, a long beard dyed with henna to a reddish hue, a prominent aquiline nose, and a grave, almost immobile face. Sources stress his erect bearing, slow measured movements, a limp from a bayonet wound at Akhulgo (1839), invariable dress of a white turban wound over a tall lambskin cap and a plain dark cherkeska, and a reputation for speaking little, never laughing, and never raising his voice.

Sado.physical_anchor: Weathered, compact mountain build — sinewy rather than broad, with sun-darkened skin, a close-cropped grey-shot beard, and deep-set eyes that hold a steady, unblinking regard. Moves with slow economy; habitually rests a calloused hand flat on his chest or on a guest's shoulder when speaking a word that matters.
Sado.vocal_quality: Low, even baritone with a dry, unhurried tempo and a resonant chest register that never rises, shaped by Quranic cadence and long silences.
Sado.social_presence: Occupies a room with quiet moral gravity — the still point others turn toward, radiating warmth to the guest at his hearth and an iron, unraised authority to anyone who would violate it.

Eldar.physical_anchor: Tall, broad-shouldered young highlander with the heavy, unhurried frame of a wrestler-trained warrior; a dark-browed, clean-jawed face kept low, eyes habitually dropped toward his leader's hands, one palm resting constantly on the hilt of his dagger.
Eldar.vocal_quality: Low chest-register baritone, slow-tempoed and physically grounded, clipped at the ends of phrases as if each word has been weighed before release.
Eldar.social_presence: Occupies a room as a silent, watchful mass just behind his master — invisible until his leader is threatened, at which point his stillness reads as pure, contained menace.

Narrator (Authorial I).physical_anchor: Voice-only, disembodied presence — no body on screen, no face, no gesture. If anchored at all, the listener imagines a figure standing alone at the edge of a summer field, hat in hand, a single plucked thistle dropped at his boots.
Narrator (Authorial I).vocal_quality: Warm, slightly weathered baritone with unhurried, periodic cadence and sudden rhetorical lifts; educated 19th-century Russian gentry register, reverent and morally weighted rather than sentimental.
Narrator (Authorial I).social_presence: Occupies the room as a quiet elder chronicler — no menace, no performance; authority comes from stillness, long looking, and the sense that he has walked the ground he describes.

Sado's Son.physical_anchor: Wiry, half-grown mountain boy on the cusp of a man's frame — shoulders widening faster than his chest has filled, wrists and hands already too big for his sleeves, cheeks still soft under the first dark down of a moustache. Habitually stands a half-step nearer to whichever man is speaking than a child should, hands hovering at his belt as if a dagger he does not yet own were there.
Sado's Son.vocal_quality: Bright, breath-forward adolescent tenor in the cracked zone between boy and man, tempo quick and clipped under awe, dropping to a hushed, respectful murmur around elders.
Sado's Son.social_presence: Occupies a room by orbiting its centre rather than holding it — a watchful, held-breath presence at thresholds and doorways, unnoticed until his wide, memorising eyes give him away.

Sado's Wife.physical_anchor: Small, wiry frame hardened by decades of household labor, with weathered hands that move with practiced economy and a back already set into a permanent slight stoop from years of bending to hearth, cushion, and bread-board. Defining feature: the deep vertical line between her brows that never softens, and the habitual gesture of folding her hands tight at her waist whenever a man enters the room.
Sado's Wife.vocal_quality: Low, even alto that never rises in pitch, measured and almost liturgical in tempo, with the dry warmth of a voice shaped by woodsmoke and set phrases rather than conversation.
Sado's Wife.social_presence: Occupies a room through near-invisibility — she enters bent nearly double and eyes lowered, yet her silent rearrangement of cushions, bread, and water is the gravitational center that holds the ritual of male hospitality in place.

Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).physical_anchor: Wiry, weather-hardened mountain build — lean shoulders, long sinewed forearms, and the bow-legged balance of a man raised on horseback; habitually stands a half-step behind and to the left of his master with one hand already near the bridle or the hilt, eyes tracking hands and thresholds rather than faces.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).vocal_quality: Low, dry, slightly rasped chest-voice with clipped consonants and measured tempo, dropping into a softer deferential register only when addressing his agha.
Hadji Murad's Murid (unnamed, active).social_presence: Occupies a room as a watchful absence — he reduces himself to furniture until the instant a threat appears, then becomes the one body between his master and the door.

Old man on the saklya roof.physical_anchor: Wiry, small-framed Caucasian elder with a sun-cured, deeply lined face half-hidden in a grey beard, stooped shoulders wrapped in a worn sheepskin over a faded beshmet. A habitual gesture of laying one hand flat on his chest before speaking, and a slow, careful tread as if every footstep were measured against a sleeping household.
Old man on the saklya roof.vocal_quality: Low, dry, gravel-soft baritone pitched just above a whisper, with a slow measured tempo and long pauses between short finished sentences.
Old man on the saklya roof.social_presence: Occupies a room through stillness rather than authority — a quiet, watchful warmth that makes guests lower their own voices, the kind of invisibility that hides a householder who has already decided what he will and will not say.

Silence and sound as dramatic gauge — crowing cocks, running water, owl mark the transition from human world to wilderness
Simon/Meller quarrel dismissed — institutional loyalty over justice

Social hierarchy encoded in seating, arm-offering, and who speaks first
Social performance as power management — polite society as battlefield
Social performance as power — Marya Vasilevna wields beauty as influence
Social performance over truth: the general is silenced not for lying but for inconveniencing the host

Songs as imperial self-anesthesia — the marching music literally drowns out what just happened

Spectacle of the enemy becoming the guest — ambiguity of hospitality and conquest
Spiritual resignation in the face of death — prayer as HM's final autonomous act
Stars as navigation and time — Hadji Murad reads the Pleiades; nature as the only neutral witness to his flight

Submission as political strategy — surrender is tactical, not defeat

Survival as legend-building — the body itself becomes a political statement

Survival through recklessness: Khan Mahoma's gambling ethos mirrors Hadji Murad's own geopolitical gamble
Suspicion as the grammar of empire — Loris-Melikov reads Gamzalo's loyalty as proof of treachery
Sworn brotherhood (Khanefi–HM) as alternative kinship structure — loyalty by choice against the blood-loyalty theme of the song
Systemic corruption normalized — stealing is assumed universal; punishment is mechanical performance, not reform
Terror as social currency: the Uhlan's paralysis mirrors the submission of everyone around Nicholas
The 'kindly childlike expression' on the dead face: innocence persisting through violence
The Caucasus war is a abstraction to St. Petersburg; Hadji Murad is a chess piece, not a man
The Russian garrison's immediate judgment of Hadji Murad after his departure — rogues vs. admirable men — reflects the novel's anti-imperial irony: the 'enemy' is more courteous than the officers
The Russian gaze — colonizer reading the colonized, always uncertain, always wrong in some way
The absent protagonist felt through his family's suffering — HM's defection has direct consequence here
The absent soldier (Peter/Avdeev) is idealized in death — father mourns him only after losing him, mirroring how imperial glory erases the individual.
The absurd closeness of mundane life (a lost pipe) and death — Tolstoyan irony at maximum compression
The absurd fragility of power: a man's fate turns on the Tsar's New Year mood
The body as biography: turban, lameness, dagger each carry a history that precedes the spoken pledge
The body as political text — Gamzalo's one-eyed glare, Khanefi's moss-like chest read as threat signals by outsiders
The body as uniform: Chernyshov's numb feet, old hands, unreliable legs contrast with the grandeur he performs
The body fights even as the soul prepares — prayer and ablutions immediately followed by military action: sacred and violent inseparable
The body's stubborn pragmatism (plugging wounds, loading guns) vs. the mind's dissolving rush of images
The bureaucratic machinery of empire — Hadji Murad is just one item in a list alongside embezzlement and troop movements
The bureaucratic-military machine strips dignity even in death
The child as innocence between worlds — Bulka's fascination mirrors the reader's, defusing danger with wonder
The conqueror and the conquered meet as equals — for one brief moment
The conqueror's court is a hall of mirrors — flattery shapes 'facts' of war
The defection reframed as good news ('Simon a eu de la chance') — conquest as aristocratic anecdote
The dream as wish-fulfillment: revenge on Shamil collapses into animal howling — ambition mocked by nature
The enemy who approaches with open hands — friend/foe ambiguity in the dark
The envoy mission already in motion — Hadji Murad's defection is not impulsive; structure and planning are evident
The faces of power (Vorontsov, Shamil) intrude on the dying man's mind alongside the faces of love (Yusuf, Sofiat)
The farce of imperial ceremony at the edge of a war zone
The fatal cost of pride — Abu Nutsal Khan rides to his death because he cannot bear to be called afraid
The forest darkness as threshold — crossing the ravine is crossing a point of no return
The gap between official report and lived reality exposes systemic imperial dishonesty
The gap between romantic war narrative and its material reality (3 dead, 12 wounded, waxen hand, dark red spot)
The gilded cage tightens: money flows, but freedom and family remain out of reach
The handshake across cultures — hesitation before contact, then firm grip
The hostage family as the ultimate lever of control — neither side offers freedom, only a different captor
The hunter becomes the hunted — HM's tactical defiance (more murids, faster pace) signals he is orchestrating something
The individual soldier absorbed into 'an immense whole directed by a single will' — loss of moral agency in institutional violence
The individual swallowed by the machine — HM's defection becomes a bureaucratic data point, not a human moment
The invisible cost of empire: ordinary families fractured by military levy
The irresolvable double-bind: HM has no plan beyond rescue; fate is contingent, not chosen
The letter never reaches Peter — the gap between the home front and the battlefield is absolute and irremediable
The machinery of empire: bureaucratic theatre of ministers and portfolios surrounding a single arbitrary will
The machinery of imperial control collapses the moment the controlled agent refuses it
The murids as extensions of HM's will — each man a facet of his character (ecstasy, faith, impatience, service)
The oblivious blunderer vs. the socially aware intervener: two modes of navigating empire's self-image
The ordinary soldier's body as record of imperial violence — old scars compounding a fresh wound
The pen as weapon: Nicholas's misspelled death order carries more force than any sword
The poetry of warfare as self-delusion: Butler romanticises what is brutalising him
The powerful amuse themselves (ballet, diamond rings) while soldiers die
The scar as badge of origin — HM's body and his mother's body are linked through trauma
The seductive corruption of empire (wine, cards, women) vs. spiritual call of the Ghazavat
The seraglio fence as symbol: desire deferred by power's obligations
The thistle as emblem of indomitable will crushed by superior force
The thistle as emblem of stubborn vitality crushed by overwhelming force
The trapped family as Shamil's counter-move against HM's defection — the hostage logic that defines HM's impossible position
The trapped man chooses the devil he can use — pragmatic alliance over moral alignment
The trapped man — Hadji Murat hemmed in by restrictions (cannot go to Vozdvizhensk, must route through Kozlovsky) even while nominally free
The trapped man: Hadji Murad's value is acknowledged only as threat to be neutralized
The trapped man: loyalty to family vs. survival logic
The trapped wolf fights to the last — HM's tactical clarity under lethal pressure mirrors the thistle-frame image
The village as both refuge and trap — community loyalty to Shamil threatens HM
The war on Hadji Murat is decided by this man — the horror of that fact is the chapter's thematic weight
The watch as double sign: Russian gift worn close to the heart, Islam demanding prayer — two loyalties on one body
The watched man — Hadji Murad draws all eyes wherever he appears, already a legend in their midst
The widow in black — the human cost of the war reduced to a single petitioning figure amid men seeking money and power
The yawning gap between how power reads an event (opportunity) vs. how the subject experiences it (existential crisis)
Theocratic power as performance — Shamil's silence coded as divine communication
Tolstoi's anti-war irony: the most honest account of Avdeev's death is the family's silence about it
Translation failure as cultural gulf — Poltoratsky cannot understand HM's Tartar words
Trapped between two empires — neither Shamil nor the Russians offer safe harbour; escape is the only agency left
Trapped between two enemies: Shamil (kin-killer) and Russia (dishonorer) — the impossible position that drives all his choices
Trapped between two powers — rescue plan exists but depends on Shamil making a move
Treachery masked as honor — Hamzad's reception is theatrical manipulation
Trust as impossible architecture between conqueror and conquered
Trust as impossible — better treatment paradoxically deepens suspicion
Trust brokered through intermediaries — the envoy system as diplomatic theater
Trust mediated through language — Tarkhanov's Tartar fluency is the only conduit to HM's 'delicate and secret' inner world
Trust withheld: HM distrusts Russian justice on principle, foreshadowing his later distrust of Russian guarantees for his family
Trust without guarantee: Hadji Murad pledges service, Russians withhold full trust
Two worlds inside one fort: common soldiers in darkness, officers in candlelit luxury
Vanity and self-image as autocratic armor — Nicholas's mirror ritual before receiving reports reveals the performance beneath the power
Vengeance as Hadji Murad's true engine beneath the defection — not submission but strategy
Violence breeds violence in an unbroken chain: Khans killed, Khansha killed, Hamzad killed
Violence erupts from within the pastoral — the bright sky and clear air contrast the sudden bloodshed
Violence within intimacy: the family unit as site of coercion and survival
Violence witnessed but not committed — complicity by proximity, not by act
Vitality persisting past the moment of death — the body acts after consciousness departs
Vorontsov's unilateral action and its bureaucratic cost: personal gallantry vs institutional protocol
Vorontsov's vanity (Napoleon reference) reveals the ego underneath the uniform
Waiting as form of slow violence — HM's family trapped, HM trapped in gilded cage of Russian hospitality
War as social currency: Hadji Murad's name gives the dinner its energy while he sits elsewhere
War mythology vs reality — soldiers romanticise hand-to-hand combat they know never happens
War romanticized by those insulated from its violence — Butler sees only camaraderie, not death
War's moral blindness: Butler's euphoria is built on deliberate non-seeing — he rides past corpses without looking
Warmth across enmity — Marya Dmitrievna and HM connection transcends language
Warrior brotherhood — collective ritual on the eve of death
Warrior identity expressed through instant readiness — no hesitation, no excess word
Women's agency dismissed — Tolstoi's misogynistic framing of the Khansha's 'weakness'; in film this can be reframed as tragic isolation
Words as theater: formal speech masks mutual contempt — imperial diplomacy as performance
Written word vs. lived truth: letters record official suspicion, Hadji Murad's oral reply asserts self-authored identity
anti-imperial fable framing: the thistle thrives only in its proper place
anti-imperial fable: the conquered that refuses to surrender
beauty destroyed by the act of possession
boredom and humanity at the margins of war
collective versus individual responsibility (cashbox gossip)
common soldier as victim of imperial machinery
community solidarity vs. individual grievance
concealment and disguise: the warrior becomes the hunted
defection as calculated operation, not panic: every move is deliberate, brokered, resourced
defection as the only viable path: loyalty to Shamil is forfeit, Russians are the lesser evil
domestic beauty against the backdrop of war: Sado's daughter's vivid description contrasts the military plotting
economy of speech as masculine virtue: 'a rope should be long but a speech short' — Hadji Murad's code
existential dullness as the Russian soldier's private war — violence abroad, emptiness within
fall from power: once-celebrated naib now rides as lone fugitive
gendered silence: Hadji Murad halts his plans when women enter — patriarchal code shapes even military plotting

human destructiveness vs. natural/individual vitality

kunak (guest-friendship) as moral absolute vs. political coercion by Shamil

kunak hospitality as moral anchor in a world of betrayal
kunak hospitality as moral counter-code to imperial violence

memory as bridge between author's present and historical past
natural vitality vs. civilized intrusion
resourcefulness and camaraderie under constraint
ritual disarmament as trust-signal in a world of hidden threat
ritual prayer framing every transaction — religion and politics intertwined in Chechen life
tenacity and resistance against overwhelming force
the 'Tartar' thistle as emblem of Hadji Murad — proud, prickly, ultimately cut down
the alien Caucasus wilderness as psychological pressure — jackals as externalized despair
the eagle/hare proverb: colonial predation framed by the colonised
the thistle as emblem of Hadji Murad — battered but unbroken

Author-Narrator.name: Author-Narrator
Author-Narrator.initial_description: First-person narrator walking home through fields in midsummer, gathering wildflowers, reflecting on nature's beauty and resistance

Hadji Murad.name: Hadji Murad
Hadji Murad.initial_description: Shamil's naib, famous for his exploits, never rode out without his banner. A Chechen warrior chief entering the hostile aoul of Makhmet on a cold November evening in 1851.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_003: Shamil's former naib, famous warrior and leader, now a fugitive riding with only one murid, cloaked in hood and burka with rifle, limping slightly, alert eyes scanning faces — a man of commanding presence reduced to hiding.
Hadji Murad.social_status: Former naib (lieutenant) of Imam Shamil; renowned warrior chief; at story-start a fugitive seeking Russian protection
Hadji Murad.want: To rescue his family — mother, wives, and especially his son Yusuf — from Shamil's captivity by leveraging Russian military force against his former imam.
Hadji Murad.need: To find a way to remain himself — an Avar warrior bound by adat, namus, and faith — inside a world where every available alliance demands he become someone else's instrument. He needs the integrity that no patron, neither Shamil nor the Tsar, will grant him.
Hadji Murad.fear: That his family will be killed or dishonored while he is reduced to a decorative captive in Russian drawing rooms — that he will die a useless guest instead of a fighting man.
Hadji Murad.contradiction: He is a man of absolute personal courage who must spend the final months of his life waiting, smiling politely, and depending on the goodwill of men he privately despises. He swears loyalty to the Tsar while planning escape; he lives among Russians as a guest while measuring every door, every horse, every guard.

arc.starting_state_act1: A hunted naib arriving by night in a hostile aoul, stripped of army and banner, carrying only one murid and an iron resolve to defect to the Russians on his own terms in order to save his family.
arc.change_act2: Welcomed, feted, and slowly immobilized by Russian hospitality at Tiflis and Nukha — courted by Vorontsov, paraded before officers, granted comforts but never the troops or the freedom of action he was promised. As negotiations stall and word arrives that his family is being moved deeper into the mountains, the polite cage tightens.
arc.end_state_act3: Realizing the Russians will neither free his family nor let him fight, he breaks from his escort with a handful of murids and is run down in a swamp; he dies fighting alone, body riddled, head severed as a trophy — the thistle from the frame story made flesh.
arc.arc_type: Fall (tragic) — the warrior cannot be assimilated and cannot return; both empires use him and discard him.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hadji Murad speaks in a low, dry baritone with a guttural Avar-Turkic accent and long deliberate pauses; sentences are short, declarative, and ceremonious, weighted by silence rather than emphasis. His Russian is functional but accented and limited, so important matters are spoken in Avar through an interpreter — which lends every line the formality of translation. He never raises his voice; authority arrives through stillness, exact courtesy, and the absence of wasted words.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, oral-traditional register; no formal Western education; speaks within an Islamic warrior idiom (references to Allah, to his dead, to his oath, to honor) and the Caucasian protocol of guest-and-host. Words drawn from war, horse, mountain, family — never from abstraction or politics.

sentence_structure.average_length: short to medium (5–14 words); often single-clause
sentence_structure.complexity: low syntactic complexity, high semantic weight; subordinate clauses rare
sentence_structure.tendency: declarative statements and direct questions; almost never exclamations; fragments common when stating facts ('It is so.' / 'Allah knows.')

verbal_tics: 'Allah knows' (Allahu a'lam) used as a closure, not a hedge
'It is well' / 'It is so' — affirming with finality
Opens replies with the addressee's title or name ('Sardar...', 'Vorontsov...') before the substance
Refers to himself in the third person when speaking of his honor or his oath ('Hadji Murad has said it.')
Closes a difficult exchange by drawing his palms slowly down his beard
avoidances: Never boasts of his own deeds in battle — others must name them
Never insults Shamil openly, even when betrayed; 'the imam' or silence instead of curses
Never uses Russian salon flattery, irony, or wit — sarcasm is alien to his register
Never speaks his fears for his family in plain words; addresses them only obliquely
Never blasphemes; never jokes about Allah, women of the household, or the dead
Never pleads
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings register through what is omitted. Anger appears as longer silence and a stilling of the hands; grief as a quieter voice and a slower pace; affection as small concrete gestures (a gift of a horse, a touched shoulder, a remembered name) rather than declarations. He weeps only twice in the source — silently — and never explains why.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, with notable pauses between sentences; near-zero acceleration even under pressure; tempo barely changes between calm and crisis, which itself becomes the warning sign.
example_lines: a) Tell the sardar Hadji Murad has come. He brings only one murid. He waits at the gate.
b) I have given my word to the Tsar. My family is in Vedeno. Without soldiers, my word is air.
c) Yusuf is alive. Allah is great. Bring the boy a horse — a black one, like his grandfather rode.
d) You promised troops. I see carpets and wine. A man does not free his mother with carpets.
e) When I was a boy my mother sang me a song. She said: do not be Khan, do not be killed. I have failed her in both.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'It is well': the matter is decided in his head and no further discussion is welcome
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_mid-conversation: he is calculating distance, escape, or the cost of a man's life — not deferring
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_a_Russian_officer's_hors: he is measuring the man, his stable, and his guard
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_his_son_Yusuf_briefly: the wound is deepest there; the brevity is the grief
subtext_rules.when_he_invokes_Allah: he is closing a door — the topic ends with that sentence
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'I_am_the_Tsar's_servant': he is performing the role required for survival; the mountains remain his only true allegiance

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Hadji Murad speaks in a low, dry baritone with a guttural Avar-Turkic accent and long deliberate pauses; sentences are short, declarative, and ceremonious, weighted by silence rather than emphasis.
voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Formal, oral-traditional register; no formal Western education; speaks within an Islamic warrior…
verbal_tics: 'Allah knows' (Allahu a'lam) used as a closure…
'It is well' / 'It is so' — affirming with…
avoidances: Never boasts of his own deeds in battle — others…
Never insults Shamil openly, even when betrayed…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: a) Tell the sardar Hadji Murad has come. He brings only one murid. He waits at the gate.

Shamil.type: former imam and patron, now mortal enemy and captor of his family
Shamil.quality: reverence corroded into hatred; bond of oath turned to bond of vendetta
Shamil.power_dynamic: Shamil holds his family — absolute leverage; HM holds his own legend, which Shamil cannot replace
Shamil.register_shift: speaks of him only with controlled formality, never by curse; the restraint is itself the measure of the rage

Prince Vorontsov (Semyon Mikhailovich, v.quality: mutual respect between two professionals of power; warmth without trust
Prince Vorontsov (Semyon Mikhailovich, v.power_dynamic: Vorontsov has armies, treasure, and time; HM has a name Vorontsov wants to display
Prince Vorontsov (Semyon Mikhailovich, v.register_shift: most ceremonious register — full titles, slow tempo, careful translation; HM gives him the warrior's courtesy due a worthy adversary

Marya Vasilyevna (Vorontsov's wife).quality: quiet courtesy; she sees the man, not the trophy; he registers it
Marya Vasilyevna (Vorontsov's wife).power_dynamic: she has no political power but moral perception; he is briefly disarmed
Marya Vasilyevna (Vorontsov's wife).register_shift: softer, shorter sentences; gifts instead of arguments; the only register where he comes close to gentleness

Loris-Melikov.quality: the only Russian to whom he gives the chronological truth of his past
Loris-Melikov.power_dynamic: near-equal in the room of memory; Loris listens, HM speaks
Loris-Melikov.register_shift: longer answers, more first-person narrative, occasional Avar phrases left untranslated — the closest he comes to confession

Eldar (his murid).power_dynamic: patron and disciple; HM commands by glance
Eldar (his murid).register_shift: fewest words of any relationship — orders in two-word fragments, often only a nod

Yusuf (his son, captive of Shamil).type: son and central love-object of the want-line
Yusuf (his son, captive of Shamil).quality: absolute, mostly unspoken; the engine of every decision
Yusuf (his son, captive of Shamil).power_dynamic: the boy is the lever every other character uses against him
Yusuf (his son, captive of Shamil).register_shift: Yusuf's name is rationed; when spoken, the voice drops — the rarest and heaviest register

Patimat / his mother.quality: lifelong tension between her warning ('do not be Khan, do not be killed') and his life as both
Patimat / his mother.power_dynamic: she has only memory and song; that is enough to haunt him
Patimat / his mother.register_shift: invoked only in private or near death; the one register that admits failure

Butler (young Russian officer).quality: courteous distance; HM treats him kindly but knows he is a guard
Butler (young Russian officer).power_dynamic: HM is older, more dangerous, formally lower in rank — a Caucasian inversion
Butler (young Russian officer).register_shift: patient, almost paternal tone; teaches by example rather than instruction

Tsar Nicholas I.type: distant sovereign / abstract guarantor of the bargain
Tsar Nicholas I.quality: never met; an idea HM invokes for leverage and discards in private
Tsar Nicholas I.power_dynamic: absolute on paper, useless in practice; HM's family is not in St Petersburg
Tsar Nicholas I.register_shift: the most formal title-laden phrasing in public, complete silence about him in private

fusion_decision.rationale: Protagonist and historical anchor; cannot be fused with any other figure under novel_to_feature compression.

example_lines: a) Tell the sardar Hadji Murad has come. He brings only one murid. He waits at the gate.
b) I have given my word to the Tsar. My family is in Vedeno. Without soldiers, my word is air.
c) Yusuf is alive. Allah is great. Bring the boy a horse — a black one, like his grandfather rode.
d) You promised troops. I see carpets and wine. A man does not free his mother with carpets.
e) When I was a boy my mother sang me a song. She said: do not be Khan, do not be killed. I have failed her in both.

Hadji Murad's Murid.name: Hadji Murad's Murid
Hadji Murad's Murid.initial_description: A single strong, active murid accompanying Hadji Murad in flight; unnamed, acts as bodyguard and groom.

Old Man of Saklya.name: Old Man of Saklya
Old Man of Saklya.initial_description: Elderly Chechen man resting on a rooftop, toothless, wearing greasy beshmet and nightcap; recognizes Hadji Murad and receives him hospitably.

Boy of the Saklya.name: Boy of the Saklya
Boy of the Saklya.initial_description: Fifteen-year-old boy with black eyes 'like ripe sloes'; son of the host, sent to fetch his father from the mosque.

Woman of the Saklya.name: Woman of the Saklya
Woman of the Saklya.initial_description: Slight, spare, middle-aged woman in yellow smock, red beshmet, blue trousers; domestic host figure, arranges cushions for Hadji Murad.

Sado's Mother.name: Sado's Mother
Sado's Mother.initial_description: Slight, spare, middle-aged Chechen woman in yellow smock, red beshmet, and wide blue trousers; Sado's household woman who arranges cushions for the guest

Eldar.name: Eldar
Eldar.initial_description: Hadji Murad's murid — strong legs, fine eyes (described as cut off mid-sentence); silent, loyal companion who mirrors his leader's every ritual action
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_005: Hadji Murad's murid (devoted follower); young, silent, with fine ram-like eyes; sits cross-legged and observes the old man's talk.
Chunk chunk_008: One of Hadji Murad's murids; follows his leader's cue to stop eating, shows disciplined loyalty.
Chunk chunk_025: Young Lesghian murid, broad-shouldered with a slim waist, beautiful ram-like eyes, and a beginning brown beard. Physically striking.
Eldar.social_status: Murid (devoted disciple-warrior) sworn to Hadji Murad; lower-ranking by birth, elevated by personal loyalty to HM
Eldar.want: To stay at Hadji Murad's side and protect him through every step of his defection — to be the body that absorbs the bullet meant for his master.
Eldar.need: To matter as a person, not only as a function — to be seen by HM as an individual soul rather than as the silent shadow at his shoulder.
Eldar.fear: Outliving his master; surviving a moment in which his loyalty failed to be enough.
Eldar.contradiction: He is a trained killer with a wrestler's body and an iron grip on his dagger, yet his face, voice, and gaze are those of a shy, almost gentle young man — the most violent capacity in HM's retinue lives behind the softest eyes.

arc.starting_state: Act 1: An almost wordless presence at HM's side — a young, beautiful, ram-eyed murid who mirrors his leader's every ritual gesture (eats when HM eats, stops when HM stops), defined entirely by attachment.
arc.change: Act 2: As HM's defection deepens and the Russian fort becomes a gilded cage, Eldar's silence acquires weight — he begins to read HM's mood, to choose where to stand, to anticipate danger; his loyalty stops being reflex and becomes decision.
arc.end_state: Act 3: In the final break-out and ambush, he fights and dies beside HM — the last of the murids to fall, body found close to his master's, the wordless promise kept literally to the last breath.
arc.arc_type: Stagnation-into-Revelation (his nature does not change; what changes is what the audience sees inside that stillness)

voice_profile.voice_description: Eldar speaks in a low, chest-grounded baritone with the unhurried tempo of a man whose body has already done most of the talking. His sentences are short, clipped at the end as if each word costs effort, and he prefers a single weighed verb to a full clause. When he does open his mouth — which is rare — the room tends to quiet, because silence is his native register and speech is treated as a small, deliberate breach of it.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal, oral, mountain-dialect register translated into plain English; limited formal education, religious idioms drawn from murid culture (Allah, the Prophet, the Imam) used naturally but sparingly; no abstract or political vocabulary.

sentence_structure.complexity: Simple, mostly main clauses; occasional subordinate clause when answering a direct question; fragments dominate under stress.
sentence_structure.tendency: Statement and fragment, with rare yes/no questions; almost never asks open questions of superiors.

verbal_tics: Addresses HM as 'Agha' or simply by silence-then-action.
Religious anchor: 'Inshallah.' / 'As Allah wills.'
Confirmation by single word: 'Yes.' / 'Now.' / 'Ready.'
Begins many replies with the conjunction 'And —' as if continuing a thought he had been holding.
avoidances: Boasting or self-reference in the first person about courage or skill.
Complaints about cold, hunger, or fatigue.
Speaking before HM has spoken in any gathering.
Abstract political reasoning about Shamil, the Tsar, or empire — these are above his station.
Vulgarity and oaths sworn against another man's honour.
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling moves through his body before it reaches his voice: a tightened jaw, a hand resettled on the dagger, a slight bow of the head, a longer-than-usual silence. When emotion does break through into language, it arrives as a single short sentence and is immediately followed by an action.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, slightly behind the beat of the conversation — he answers a half-second after others would, which makes his replies feel decided rather than reactive.

example_lines.neutral_everyday: The horses are watered, Agha. We can ride.
example_lines.under_pressure: Two riders. From the ridge. Not ours.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: The road is open, Agha. Inshallah, by morning we are home.
example_lines.angry_hurt: He spoke of you like a dog. I did not answer him. I waited for you.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: If you fall, Agha — I do not go back. I stay where you stay.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'as_you_wish': He has already seen the danger and accepted it; the sentence is a vow, not a deferral.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_after_a_question: He disagrees but will not contradict his master in front of others — read the silence as opposition.
subtext_rules.when_he_repositions_his_hand_on_the_dagg: He has identified the man in the room he would kill first; the gesture is a target, not a habit.
subtext_rules.when_he_uses_'we'_instead_of_'you': He is binding his fate to HM's decision and quietly refusing the option of being left behind.
subtext_rules.when_he_invokes_Allah: He believes the next minutes may be his last and is making peace, not piety.

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Eldar speaks in a low, chest-grounded baritone with the unhurried tempo of a man whose body has already done most of the talking.
voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Informal, oral, mountain-dialect register translated into plain English; limited formal education…
verbal_tics: Addresses HM as 'Agha' or simply by…
Religious anchor: 'Inshallah.' / 'As Allah wills.'
avoidances: Boasting or self-reference in the first person…
Complaints about cold, hunger, or fatigue.
voice_profile_compact.example_line: The horses are watered, Agha. We can ride.

Hadji Murad.type: Master / sworn murid; surrogate father-son bond layered over military discipleship
Hadji Murad.quality: Absolute, wordless devotion; the central relationship of Eldar's life and the lens through which he reads every other person.
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: Total subordination — HM commands by glance; Eldar obeys before the sentence ends. Eldar's only sphere of independent judgement is the threat assessment of strangers.
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: Lowest, softest, most clipped register; eyes down, voice barely above the room. Religious vocatives ('Agha') used naturally.

Khanefi.quality: Wordless solidarity; they divide the watch without negotiation. No rivalry, no warmth — a working trust between two silent men.
Khanefi.power_dynamic: Roughly equal among the murids; Eldar slightly senior in HM's intimate orbit.
Khanefi.speech_register_shift: Even more compressed than with HM — often pure gesture; when he speaks, it is operational ('You take the door.').

Khan Mahoma.type: Brother-murid, but a different temperament — Khan Mahoma talks, Eldar does not
Khan Mahoma.quality: Quiet tolerance; Eldar registers Khan Mahoma's volubility as a small risk to be managed near HM.
Khan Mahoma.power_dynamic: Equal in rank; Eldar's stillness exerts a subtle disciplinary pressure on the others.
Khan Mahoma.speech_register_shift: Curt, almost monosyllabic; refuses to be drawn into banter.

Gamzalo.type: Brother-murid, senior in years and harshness
Gamzalo.quality: Respect without affection; Eldar accepts Gamzalo's grimness as a model of the discipline he himself embodies more softly.
Gamzalo.power_dynamic: Gamzalo slightly above by age and severity; Eldar defers in logistics, never in the matter of HM's personal safety.
Gamzalo.speech_register_shift: Formal, brief, deferential where pragmatic; immovable when HM is the subject.

Russian officers (Vorontsov, Butler, Lor.type: Hosts / captors / strangers across a cultural and political line
Russian officers (Vorontsov, Butler, Lor.quality: Wary courtesy; he treats them as potential threats in fine clothing and never relaxes his body in their presence.
Russian officers (Vorontsov, Butler, Lor.power_dynamic: Nominally below them inside the fort; in his own moral hierarchy they are guests in HM's orbit, not the other way round.
Russian officers (Vorontsov, Butler, Lor.speech_register_shift: Almost mute; answers only direct questions, in the shortest grammatically complete form, never volunteering.

Maria Dmitrievna and the women of the fo.type: Outsiders to the murid bond; alien gender register
Maria Dmitrievna and the women of the fo.quality: Respectful distance; he lowers his eyes further, will not be alone with them, treats kindness as a complication rather than a gift.
Maria Dmitrievna and the women of the fo.power_dynamic: Asymmetric and culturally coded — he gives way physically but does not adopt their idiom.
Maria Dmitrievna and the women of the fo.speech_register_shift: Reduced almost to gesture; a small bow of the head replaces most sentences.

fusion_decision.rationale: Eldar is THE archetypal murid — HM's closest body-double in loyalty and the one who dies beside him in the final ambush; he is the figure into whom other minor murids (Khanefi's song, Gamzalo's preparation beats) are absorbed, not the figure to be dissolved, and his silent visual presence carries the film's central image of unbought devotion across all 29 of his chunks.

example_lines.neutral_everyday: The horses are watered, Agha. We can ride.
example_lines.under_pressure: Two riders. From the ridge. Not ours.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: The road is open, Agha. Inshallah, by morning we are home.
example_lines.angry_hurt: He spoke of you like a dog. I did not answer him. I waited for you.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: If you fall, Agha — I do not go back. I stay where you stay.

Sado's Father.name: Sado's Father
Sado's Father.initial_description: Old man at the saklya; lifeless red eyes; keeps to the apiary; speaks in veiled proverbs about Russian raids; refuses to say directly what he knows

Sado.name: Sado
Sado.initial_description: Master of the house (saklya), about forty, small beard, long nose, black eyes; devout and hospitable; bound by kunak (guest-friendship) code; warns HM of Shamil's arrest order.
Sado.social_status: Master of his saklya; respected village elder; devout Muslim; bound by kunak (guest-friendship) code
Sado.want: To honor the sacred kunak bond by sheltering Hadji Murat under his roof, no matter the cost.
Sado.need: To prove that a man's word — given to a guest, given to God — outweighs the orders of any imam or tsar.
Sado.fear: That his house, his name, and his ancestors will be shamed if a guest under his protection is harmed.
Sado.contradiction: Devout follower of Shamil's faith yet defies Shamil's arrest order; a peaceful host who keeps a dagger within reach for his guest's sake.

arc.act_1_starting_state: Quiet master of his saklya, bound to the rhythms of prayer and village custom; receives Hadji Murat as kunak with grave, unhesitating welcome.
arc.act_2_change: Learns of Shamil's arrest order against HM; risks his own life and his household's safety to warn HM and shelter him through the night.
arc.act_3_end_state: Stands at his threshold as HM rides out, hand on chest — a man who kept his word and now lives with the consequences his village will visit on him.

voice_profile.voice_description: Sado speaks in a low, even baritone with the unhurried tempo of a man shaped by Quranic recitation and long mountain silences. His sentences are short, declarative, often ending in a proverb or an invocation of God. He never raises his voice; weight is carried by stillness, not volume.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal but elevated by religious register; uneducated by Russian standards yet steeped in Qur'an, Chechen oral tradition, and the proverbs of his ancestors
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium (5-12 words); simple declarative or imperative; questions are rare and never rhetorical; fragments common when emotion presses
verbal_tics: Inshallah (if God wills)
Bismillah (in the name of God)
'My house is your house'
Begins counsel with 'Listen'
'A guest is sent by God'
avoidances: Boasting or self-reference
Russian loanwords or imperial titles
Direct insult — even of an enemy
Breaking the name of a guest in public
Pleading for his own safety
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling registers in the body before the voice — a hand to the chest, a slow turn of the head, a longer silence. When words come, they are pared to a proverb or a single image. Tenderness sounds like a blessing; anger sounds like a verdict.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Sit. The bread is warm. My house is your house tonight.
example_lines.under_pressure: Shamil has spoken your name. Before the muezzin calls, you must be gone.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: God has sent you to my door. I have lived to see this — Bismillah.
example_lines.angry_hurt: A man who breaks the bread of a host breaks the hand of God. Let him answer for it.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I am one old man with one rifle. But while you stand in my saklya, you stand in my heart.

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When Sado says 'My house is your house,' he means 'I will die before they take you from this room.' When he says 'God wills it,' he means 'I have already decided and will not be moved.' When he falls silent and lays his hand on his chest, he is making a vow more binding than any spoken oath.

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Sado speaks in a low, even baritone with the unhurried tempo of a man shaped by Quranic recitation and long mountain silences.
voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Informal but elevated by religious register; uneducated by Russian standards yet steeped in Qur'an…
voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Short to medium (5-12 words); simple declarative or imperative; questions are rare and never…
verbal_tics: Inshallah (if God wills)
Bismillah (in the name of God)
avoidances: Boasting or self-reference
Russian loanwords or imperial titles
voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When Sado says 'My house is your house,' he means 'I will die before they take you from this room.' When he says 'God…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Sit. The bread is warm. My house is your house tonight.

Hadji Murat.quality: Reverent, protective, unspoken brotherhood forged by code rather than history
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: Sado yields the deference of a host to a great warrior, yet inside his own walls his moral authority is absolute
Hadji Murat.speech_register_shift: Drops to near-whisper; speech becomes more formal, more invocational; addresses HM by full name with the honorific weight of prayer

Shamil.type: Distant spiritual sovereign whose order he defies
Shamil.quality: Outward reverence, inward refusal — the Imam commands his faith but not his hearth
Shamil.power_dynamic: Subject to Shamil's religious-political authority, yet sovereign within his own threshold
Shamil.speech_register_shift: Names Shamil only with the title 'the Imam'; never argues against him aloud, only acts against him in silence

Sado's Household (fused: Father, Mother,.type: Household elders and dependents now consolidated into Sado's single voice
Sado's Household (fused: Father, Mother,.quality: Sado carries their welcome, their warning, and their farewell himself — the household speaks through him
Sado's Household (fused: Father, Mother,.power_dynamic: Sole spokesman for his roof; household presence becomes silent backdrop, gestures absorbed into his actions
Sado's Household (fused: Father, Mother,.speech_register_shift: When delivering what was once Father's proverb or Mother's blessing, Sado's cadence slows further and takes on the formal weight of inherited speech

The Village (Makhmet aoul).quality: Bound by blood and custom, yet he places kunak code above their immediate safety
The Village (Makhmet aoul).power_dynamic: He is one of the elders they listen to, yet he knows they may turn on him for this night's choice
The Village (Makhmet aoul).speech_register_shift: Public speech becomes terser, more proverbial, never explanatory — he will not justify a kunak's sheltering to anyone

fusion_decision.rationale: Sado's Father, Mother, Wife and Son all serve the single dramatic function of household-hospitality and warning; per CF-01 (each <2 scenes, identical kunak function) their gestures and the father's warning proverb collapse into Sado himself, giving the saklya one moral voice instead of four — Old Man of Saklya likewise absorbed as prologue-variant of the same hosting function.

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Sit. The bread is warm. My house is your house tonight.
example_lines.under_pressure: Shamil has spoken your name. Before the muezzin calls, you must be gone.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: God has sent you to my door. I have lived to see this — Bismillah.
example_lines.angry_hurt: A man who breaks the bread of a host breaks the hand of God. Let him answer for it.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I am one old man with one rifle. But while you stand in my saklya, you stand in my heart.

Sado's Son.name: Sado's Son
Sado's Son.initial_description: Fifteen-year-old boy, nimble, black-eyed; runs errands for his father.

Bata.name: Bata
Bata.initial_description: Sinewy, short-legged Chechen guide, deeply sun-burnt, tattered yellow Circassian coat; cheerful and confident; will escort Eldar to the Russians.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_006: Sinewy, short-legged Chechen, sun-darkened, wearing a worn yellow Circassian coat and crumpled black leggings; a skilled guide boasting no Chechen can pass where he can; loyal to Hadji Murad for honor, not money.
Chunk chunk_011: Shorter Chechen man who approaches Russian sentry post at night, declares himself friendly, unarmed, and acting as a messenger for a prince
Chunk chunk_012: Shorter Chechen scout; arrives at Russian outpost at night seeking Prince Vorontsov, speaks broken Russian, unarmed and diplomatic
Chunk chunk_019: Referenced as having already departed with Khan Mahoma; messenger/liaison role
Chunk chunk_020: Accompanies Khan Mahoma on the mission to the Russian prince; appears alongside him as a messenger/guide figure
Bata.age: approx. 30s (mature, experienced, no specific age in text)
Bata.origin: Chechen (mountain tribesman, hired guide working between auls and Russian lines)
Bata.social_status: Free Chechen mountaineer; not a murid; works as paid guide and messenger; commands respect for skill, not rank
Bata.want: To complete the mission entrusted to him by Hadji Murad — deliver word safely to the Russian prince and return — and to be paid in honor as much as in coin
Bata.need: To prove that a Chechen of no rank can move freely between two worlds without belonging to either, and that loyalty can be a craft, not a birthright
Bata.fear: Being trapped on the wrong side of a line he cannot cross back over — caught either as a Russian collaborator by his own people or as a spy by the Russians
Bata.contradiction: Boasts that no Chechen can pass where he can — yet his entire usefulness depends on serving men more powerful than himself; he is proudest of a freedom he can only exercise on someone else's errand

arc.start_state: Confident, professional intermediary — accepts Hadji Murad's mission with bravado, takes pride in skill
arc.change: As the relay between mountain and Russian lines tightens, his outsider position becomes precarious; he must speak broken Russian under sentry rifles, navigate a world where his usefulness is also his vulnerability
arc.end_state: Reabsorbed into Hadji Murad's small band as one of the murids in the final stand — the freelance guide ends as a loyalist, dying on a side he was never sworn to

voice_profile.voice_description: Bata speaks in short, practical bursts — a guide's speech, oriented to terrain, time, and risk. His Russian is broken but unembarrassed; his Chechen-Avar is quick, idiomatic, full of mountain proverbs about paths, horses, and weather. He undersells danger and oversells his own skill, but never wastes a word.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal, practical, low-literacy oral; mountaineer's lexicon (paths, fords, ridges, horses, weapons); broken Russian functional vocabulary when addressing Russians; rich Chechen idiom otherwise; era-appropriate (1850s Caucasus)
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short clauses, often verbless fragments; statement-heavy with occasional sharp questions; declarative under pressure; fond of two-part sayings ('the wolf knows the path, the path knows the wolf')
verbal_tics: Opens negotiations with a small boast about a route or a horse
Calls strangers 'brother' in his own tongue, 'sir' in Russian — and the listener can tell which he means
Marks distance in horse-time, not versts ('two smokes', 'before the mare tires')
Repeats key word at end of sentence when speaking Russian to make sure he is understood
avoidances: Never swears loyalty in absolute terms — never says 'forever' or 'until death' about any master
Never speaks of God or fate in long sentences; piety stays in proverbs, not declarations
Will not insult an enemy he is currently negotiating with, even in his own language — bad craft
Avoids his own backstory; family, village, debts — none of it surfaces
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings come out sideways — through a sharper joke, a longer silence, a sudden tightening of the bridle. Fear shows as exaggerated cheer; pride as a sly half-smile; grief as terseness. He never names an emotion; he describes weather, the horse, the road instead.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Alternating: clipped and fast in negotiation or under threat, slower and more proverbial when at ease around a fire; shifts register audibly when crossing language lines

example_lines.line: Two smokes to the river. The mare knows the way better than I do — let her lead.
Friend! No gun. Friend. I bring word for prince — Vorontsov. Word, only word.
If the moon holds, brother, we sleep on Russian bread tomorrow — and it will not poison us.
You call this a path? A blind goat would spit on it. Move aside — I will go first, as always.
A man like me — he belongs to the road, not to a house. The road is honest. It does not pretend to love you.

subtext_rules: When Bata talks about the horse or the weather, he is talking about the people in the room — read the metaphor.
When he boasts, he is reassuring himself, not the listener; the louder the boast, the higher the risk he sees.
When he uses 'brother' to a Russian, he is measuring distance, not closing it.
Silence from Bata means decision made; he does not announce moves before he makes them.
Praise of a master ('Hadji Murad is a real man') is contractual language — it means 'I will do what I said', not 'I love him'.

voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Informal, practical, low-literacy oral; mountaineer's lexicon (paths, fords, ridges, horses…
voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Short clauses, often verbless fragments; statement-heavy with occasional sharp questions…
verbal_tics: Opens negotiations with a small boast about a…
Calls strangers 'brother' in his own tongue…
avoidances: Never swears loyalty in absolute terms — never…
Never speaks of God or fate in long sentences…
voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When Bata talks about the horse or the weather, he is talking about the people in the room — read the metaphor.
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Two smokes to the river. The mare knows the way better than I do — let her lead.

Hadji Murad.quality: Respect-based, professional loyalty that hardens into personal loyalty; chosen, not inherited
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: Hadji Murad commands; Bata serves — but Bata's value lies in skills the leader cannot replace, giving him a quiet leverage
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: Drops boasting almost entirely with HM; speaks shorter, more direct, defers without grovelling — the only man around whom he stops performing

Khan Mahoma.type: Mission partner; insider-murid paired with outsider-guide
Khan Mahoma.quality: Functional alliance with quiet rivalry — they need each other but represent two different ways of belonging to Hadji Murad
Khan Mahoma.power_dynamic: Roughly equal in mission terms; Khan Mahoma has rank-by-name, Bata has skill-by-terrain
Khan Mahoma.speech_register_shift: Banter in Chechen/Avar, mountain humor, clipped jokes about who is leading whom; switches to formal cooperation when Russians are present

Eldar.quality: Older-brother-on-the-road dynamic; Bata's authority is teacher-like without tenderness
Eldar.power_dynamic: Bata leads, Eldar follows — Bata's experience outranks Eldar's loyalty
Eldar.speech_register_shift: Instructional, terse, occasional dry humor; uses imperative more than with anyone else

Russian sentries / Vorontsov's men.quality: Wary, transactional; Bata performs harmlessness deliberately
Russian sentries / Vorontsov's men.power_dynamic: They hold the rifles; he holds the message — temporary parity through usefulness
Russian sentries / Vorontsov's men.speech_register_shift: Broken Russian, simplified syntax, repeats key words, drops all proverbs, exaggerates politeness ('friend', 'sir'); body language goes deliberately small

Khanefi.type: Fellow murid in the final stand
Khanefi.quality: Comrade-in-arms by circumstance; functional bond at the end
Khanefi.power_dynamic: Equal — both subordinate to Hadji Murad's last decisions
Khanefi.speech_register_shift: Almost wordless; the talkative guide goes quiet at the end, communicating in glances and small actions

fusion_decision.rationale: Bata's role as the independent hired Chechen guide and outsider-intermediary is thematically distinct from Khan Mahoma's insider-murid status — fusing them would collapse the insider/outsider tension that Tolstoi uses to mirror Hadji Murad's own divided loyalty.

example_lines.line: Two smokes to the river. The mare knows the way better than I do — let her lead.
Friend! No gun. Friend. I bring word for prince — Vorontsov. Word, only word.
If the moon holds, brother, we sleep on Russian bread tomorrow — and it will not poison us.
You call this a path? A blind goat would spit on it. Move aside — I will go first, as always.
A man like me — he belongs to the road, not to a house. The road is honest. It does not pretend to love you.

Khan Mahoma.name: Khan Mahoma
Khan Mahoma.initial_description: One of Hadji Murad's four horsemen waiting at the Argun River bend; designated envoy to be escorted to Prince Vorontsov by Bata; knows what to do and say.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_019: Departed with Bata before Hadji Murad's arrival; messenger/envoy figure
Chunk chunk_020: One of Hadji Murad's murids, sent on a mission to the Russian prince; returns with news that the prince received him warmly
Khan Mahoma.want: To prove his worth as Hadji Murad's most trusted envoy — to be the voice that carries his master's word to Russian princes and home again.
Khan Mahoma.need: To find a cause larger than personal daring; to learn that loyalty is not performance but endurance.
Khan Mahoma.fear: Being thought useless or replaceable — a murid without a mission, a tongue without a master.
Khan Mahoma.contradiction: Frivolous and quick-laughing in camp, yet rides willingly into Russian lines where a single misread phrase means death; treats lethal errands like a sport.

arc.starting_state: Eager errand-rider at the Argun bend, half-boy still, proud of being chosen over older murids.
arc.change: Crosses repeatedly between Russian and Chechen worlds as envoy; sees Vorontsov's politeness and Shamil's reach and begins to understand his master is trapped between them.
arc.end_state: Stands with Hadji Murad in the final stand, the smile gone, firing until killed — the envoy who finally has nothing to deliver.

voice_profile.voice_description: Khan Mahoma speaks in short, bright bursts, often opening with a small joke or a proverb before delivering the substance. His Russian is broken but confident — he prefers a vivid wrong word to a careful right one. Even reporting danger he sounds amused, as if the world's seriousness were a private joke between him and his horse.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal; little formal schooling; mountain Avar/Chechen idiom carried into broken Russian; religious phrasings (Allah, inshallah) used casually rather than devoutly
voice_profile.sentence_structure: short sentences (5–10 words), often fragments; high ratio of statements to questions; frequent imperatives and exclamations; tendency to answer a question with a counter-image rather than a direct yes/no
verbal_tics: opens replies with a small laugh or 'Eh —'
'Allah knows' as a shrug
calls horses and men by the same affectionate diminutives
ends bad news with 'but we are alive, no?'
avoidances: long abstract reasoning
courtly Russian formality (sir, your excellency without irony)
self-pity
open admissions of fear
religious sermonizing
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Expresses feeling through the body and through jokes: laughter for tension, a sudden silence for grief, a click of the tongue for contempt. Affection comes out as teasing; anger as sharp short curses, then a grin to take the edge off.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: alternating — quick clipped bursts in danger, looser drawling phrases at rest; rarely measured for long

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Eh — the prince fed me well. White bread, like for a bride.
example_lines.under_pressure: Quiet, brother. The Cossacks have ears in the reeds — and we have only two horses left.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Allah is generous today! Tomorrow we ride home with our pockets ringing.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Dog. You sold our trail for a Russian coin — may your bread choke you.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I joke because if I stop, Hadji Murad, I will hear my mother calling me back.

subtext_rules.when_he_jokes_he_means: this is dangerous and I refuse to show it
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_he_means: I do not trust the man in front of us
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_a_horse_he_means: I am praising the rider but cannot say it
subtext_rules.when_he_says_Allah_knows_he_means: I know but will not be the one to tell you
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_broken_Russian_loudly_he_: let them think me a fool, it keeps us alive

Hadji Murad.quality: devoted, playful, slightly worshipful; the youngest-feeling of the four murids
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: fully subordinate; volunteers for the most exposed errands
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: drops the joking opener; voice tightens to clipped report-Russian/Avar; calls him only 'Hadji Murad', never a diminutive

Khanefi.quality: affectionate friction — the joker against the earnest one
Khanefi.power_dynamic: equal in rank, opposite in temperament
Khanefi.speech_register_shift: louder, more teasing; uses Khanefi as straight man for jokes about ritual and prayer

Eldar.power_dynamic: Khan Mahoma the worldlier, Eldar the more silent — Khan Mahoma fills Eldar's silences
Eldar.speech_register_shift: softer, almost gentle; teases without sting

Bata.type: operational partner on the Vorontsov mission
Bata.quality: wary cooperation; insider murid vs. hired Chechen guide
Bata.power_dynamic: equal in the field but Khan Mahoma carries Hadji Murad's words and therefore the authority
Bata.speech_register_shift: terser, more transactional; fewer jokes, no diminutives

Prince Vorontsov.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Khan Mahoma is the supplicant's mouth
Prince Vorontsov.speech_register_shift: broken Russian deliberately exaggerated; bows in word and posture; jokes withheld until safely outside the gates

Shamil (offstage).power_dynamic: Khan Mahoma's life and his master's family hang on Shamil's mood
Shamil (offstage).speech_register_shift: name lowered to a near-whisper; jokes vanish entirely when Shamil is mentioned

fusion_decision.rationale: Khan Mahoma carries a distinct frivolous-impulsive voice and the dramatic function of HM's outward-facing envoy/messenger murid; collapsing him into Khanefi (earnest) or Bata (outsider guide) would erase the contrast inside HM's murid quartet and remove the only murid whose levity humanizes the group before the final battle.

Sado's wife.name: Sado's wife
Sado's wife.initial_description: Thin middle-aged Chechen woman, Sado's wife; serves guests and arranged the cushions; carries domestic hospitality role.

Sado's daughter.name: Sado's daughter
Sado's daughter.initial_description: Young Chechen girl; wears red trousers and green beshmet; silver-coin necklace and a silver ruble at her plait; sloe-black eyes bright in a face trying to be stern; aware of visitors but does not look at them.

Sado's Boy.name: Sado's Boy
Sado's Boy.initial_description: Young son who sits by the door gazing at Hadji Murad's face with sparkling eyes, smiling in confirmation of his father's words of welcome

Panov.name: Panov
Panov.initial_description: Non-commissioned officer leading a night ambush patrol of three soldiers from Vozvizhensk fort beyond the Shahgirinsk Gate.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_009: Non-commissioned officer on night ambush duty; pragmatic, leads soldiers in casual camp behavior; addresses Avdeev familiarly
Chunk chunk_010: Russian soldier, calm and pragmatic; mediates disputes within the company; pipe smoker on night watch.
Panov.origin: Russian peasant stock; conscripted enlisted man, now non-commissioned officer in the Caucasus Corps
Panov.social_status: NCO (unter-ofitser) at Vozvizhensk fort; experienced soldier, informal leader among comrades
Panov.want: To get through each watch, each ambush, each dying comrade with the company intact and the duty done — the small, daily soldier's task.
Panov.need: To bear witness honestly to the men who die beside him, so that their deaths are not swallowed entirely by the indifference of the empire that spent them.
Panov.fear: Becoming the kind of soldier who no longer feels anything when a comrade dies on the cot beside him — the deadening that long service threatens.
Panov.contradiction: He is calm, dry, almost flippant about death and danger, yet it is he who sits longest at Avdeev's deathbed, he who phrases the letter home — the gruffness is a shell over an unguarded loyalty.

arc.act_1: Veteran NCO leading a routine night ambush; pragmatic, jokes, smokes, mediates petty quarrels among the soldiers — the war is just his job.
arc.act_2: Avdeev, the soldier who took another's place, is gut-shot and dies slowly; Panov is forced from camp-banter mode into the role of witness, scribe, comforter — the abstract cost of empire becomes one specific man on a cot.
arc.act_3: Returns to the line near Butler at the assault, identifies Shamil's banner with the same dry voice as before — but the camera/audience now reads the silence under it; he carries Avdeev forward as one more name.

voice_profile.voice_description: Panov speaks in short, dry, peasant-soldier Russian — the cadence of a man who has said the same things in the same trenches for fifteen years. His sentences are clipped, often built around a proverb, a soldier's joke, or a flat practical instruction. Underneath the gruffness is unshowy tenderness that surfaces only in half-lines, never in declarations.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal, low-register peasant Russian; minimal education; military slang of the Caucasus line; biblical/proverbial turns of phrase absorbed from village church and barracks; no foreign words, no abstractions.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short. Average 6-10 words. Heavy on imperatives, fragments, and rhetorical questions. Rarely subordinates a clause; prefers two short sentences joined by 'well' or 'so'. Statements outnumber questions except when addressing a wounded man.
verbal_tics: 'Well, brother...' as a softening opener
'It's nothing' / 'never mind' (nichego) — used to dismiss pain, his own and others'
Short proverbs ('a bullet finds its own', 'the soldier's lot')
Calls comrades by diminutive first names (Petrukha, Avdeyushka) when the moment turns serious
Tamps or relights pipe mid-sentence, breaking his own line
avoidances: Abstract patriotic rhetoric ('for the Tsar', 'for Russia') — he leaves that to officers
Self-pity or complaints about his own hardship
Explicit declarations of affection ('I love you, brother' would never pass his lips)
Religious sentimentality beyond a quick crossing of himself
Anything that would shame a dying man
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings travel through hands and silences, not words. Grief becomes a longer pause before relighting the pipe; tenderness becomes a diminutive name; anger becomes a flatter, quieter voice rather than a louder one. He understates by reflex.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and economical; steady tempo even under fire. Slows further at a deathbed — the only time his sentences shorten almost to single words.

example_lines.line: Quiet night. Pipe's drawing well. Pass the flint, Petrukha — and don't snore on watch, the Chechens hear better than the priest.
Down. Heads down. They're on the ridge — count to ten, then we move. No firing till I say. It's nothing, lads, just the usual.
Letter from home for Avdeyushka, look at that. Read it out, brother — slow, mind you, so the rest of us can pretend it's ours too.
Enough. He's dying, and you're arguing over a copeck. Sit down. Hold his hand or get out of the tent.
Write it like this. 'Dear mother. Your Petrushka took a bullet in the belly and went easy.' ...He didn't go easy. But she doesn't need to know that part.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_its_nothing: He means: this is bad, and I am not going to let you see how bad, because that would make it worse for you.
subtext_rules.when_he_uses_a_diminutive: He has stopped treating the man as a soldier and started treating him as someone whose mother he can picture.
subtext_rules.when_he_relights_the_pipe_mid_sentence: He needs the gesture to keep his voice level; the pause is the feeling he won't speak.
subtext_rules.when_he_quotes_a_proverb: He is borrowing the village's old wisdom because he himself has run out of words.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_at_a_bedside: Loyalty — the kind that does not require, and would be cheapened by, any statement.
