Avdeev.quality: Deep, unstated loyalty masked as gruff teasing; Panov is the witness to Avdeev's quiet sacrifice and his death
Avdeev.power_dynamic: Panov is older, NCO, the steady one; Avdeev is the soft-hearted private. Panov leads, but Avdeev's death exposes that the moral weight sits with the private, not the NCO.
Avdeev.register_shift: Drops from camp-banter and orders into near-whispered diminutives (Avdeyushka) and one-word reassurances at the deathbed.

Butler.quality: Respectful, professional; Panov is the experienced enlisted anchor who quietly informs the young officer about the field — including identifying Shamil's banner.
Butler.power_dynamic: Officer/NCO formal hierarchy; in practice Panov knows the ground better and Butler knows it.
Butler.register_shift: Tightens to clipped, deferential 'your honour' register; shorter sentences, no proverbs, pipe out of mouth.

Soldiers under his command (Petrukha, Bo.quality: Paternal-fraternal; mediates their squabbles, indulges their jokes, draws the line at carelessness on watch.
Soldiers under his command (Petrukha, Bo.power_dynamic: Quiet authority by experience rather than rank-fetish; obeyed because he is right, not because he shouts.
Soldiers under his command (Petrukha, Bo.register_shift: Uses diminutives and proverbs freely; teases; sharpens to flat imperatives only when discipline or safety demands it.

Hadji Murat (if scene contact).quality: Wary professional curiosity; Panov reads Hadji Murat the way he reads weather — as a fact of the field, not a moral question.
Hadji Murat (if scene contact).power_dynamic: None direct; Panov is line-soldier, HM is the legend at the officers' table.
Hadji Murat (if scene contact).register_shift: Speaks of him only in third person, dryly, to other enlisted men; never to his face.

fusion_decision.rationale: Panov is the dramatically active comrade in the Avdeev death-arc and the informational anchor near Butler at the assault — he carries dialogue, decisions, and the letter-writing scene; the proposed merge partners (Serogin, Nikitin, Bondarenko, Old Sergeant-Major) are functionally absorbable INTO Panov, not the other way around, so Panov stays as the canonical NCO-witness and the others fold upward.

example_lines.line: Quiet night. Pipe's drawing well. Pass the flint, Petrukha — and don't snore on watch, the Chechens hear better than the priest.
Down. Heads down. They're on the ridge — count to ten, then we move. No firing till I say. It's nothing, lads, just the usual.
Letter from home for Avdeyushka, look at that. Read it out, brother — slow, mind you, so the rest of us can pretend it's ours too.
Enough. He's dying, and you're arguing over a copeck. Sit down. Hold his hand or get out of the tent.
Write it like this. 'Dear mother. Your Petrushka took a bullet in the belly and went easy.' ...He didn't go easy. But she doesn't need to know that part.

Avdeev.name: Avdeev
Avdeev.initial_description: Russian soldier; cheerful, resourceful; improvises a ground-pipe for smoking; earns Panov's praise as 'smart chap'
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_010: Russian soldier, cheerful but plagued by existential dullness; admits to drinking away his sadness; confides openly about despair.
Avdeev.age: Mid-twenties (conscripted peasant, no precise age in source)
Avdeev.origin: Russian peasant village; serves in the Kurin regiment in the Caucasus
Avdeev.social_status: Common soldier; family of small peasants — younger of two brothers, drew the lot to serve in his elder brother's place
Avdeev.want: To soldier well, do his small duties cheerfully, and not be noticed for the dullness eating at him — to send word home that all is in order.
Avdeev.need: To be seen and named as a person, not as a unit of the empire — to have his death matter as more than a line in a dispatch.
Avdeev.fear: That his life is being spent for nothing he understands; that he will die exactly as the army will report it — 'one private killed' — and disappear.
Avdeev.contradiction: Outwardly the cheerful, resourceful soldier who improvises a ground-pipe and earns laughter from his comrades, yet inwardly drinks to numb a despair he confesses openly when sober enough to speak.

arc.act_1: Cheerful, useful soldier in the cordon line; the regiment's good-humored peasant who fixes things and makes others laugh.
arc.act_2: In a quiet moment confides his existential dullness — admits drinking away sadness, longing for the village, brother, wife; the empire's distance from his life shows through the seam.
arc.act_3: Killed in the skirmish in Section 8; his death is reported up the chain as a routine line, while the parallel scenes at home and in the dispatch expose the gap between the man and the report.

voice_profile.voice_description: Avdeev speaks in the unhurried, slightly singsong cadence of a Russian village, rounding hard edges off official army talk. He uses simple, concrete words, often borrows soldier-slang for the surface of a thought, and lets the real feeling slip out in a half-finished sentence. Even when he jokes, there is a small pause before the laugh, as if he is checking whether it is allowed.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal, low education, mid-19th-century Russian peasant register filtered into plain English; military slang sits on top of village idioms
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium sentences, often fragments; statements outnumber questions; thoughts trail off with 'and that's the way of it' rather than concluding cleanly
verbal_tics: 'brother' as an address to comrades, regardless of rank below officer
'and there you are' / 'that's how it is' as a closing shrug
small self-deprecating laugh before saying anything serious
calls the army 'the service', never 'the empire'
avoidances: Abstract political language ('empire', 'cause', 'glory')
Religious cursing aimed at superiors
Self-pity stated outright — sadness is admitted, never claimed
Romantic talk about war or heroism
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings come out sideways — through a chore, a joke, or one quiet line dropped between two practical sentences; tears would shame him, so grief sounds like fatigue and longing sounds like a question about home.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and even, with sudden small dips into quiet when something true is said; never hectic, even under fire

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Pass me the flint, brother — I'll have a pipe going out of this earth before the sergeant's done shouting.
example_lines.under_pressure: Keep low. Keep low and load. That's all there is to it now.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: When the snow goes, I'll write home. Tell my brother the cow's worth keeping. He'll laugh — he always laughs at my letters.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Don't tell me about the Tsar, brother. The Tsar doesn't know my name and never will. And there you are.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Some days I drink so I won't think. Some days I think anyway. I don't know what we're doing here. I really don't.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_im_fine: He is not fine — he is performing the cheerfulness the regiment expects.
subtext_rules.when_he_jokes_about_the_service: He is naming a despair he cannot say plainly.
subtext_rules.when_he_talks_about_his_brother_or_wife: He is asking, without asking, whether his life still belongs to anyone.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_mid_sentence: He has come too close to the truth and is stepping back from it.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_thats_how_it_is: He has accepted something he does not agree with.

'and there you are' / 'that's how it is' as a…
avoidances: Abstract political language ('empire', 'cause'…
Religious cursing aimed at superiors
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Pass me the flint, brother — I'll have a pipe going out of this earth before the sergeant's done shouting.

Panov.quality: Warm, unequal — Panov calls him 'smart chap'; Avdeev accepts the nickname with quiet pleasure
Panov.power_dynamic: Panov senior in experience; Avdeev defers easily, without resentment
Panov.speech_register_shift: Slightly more clipped and soldierly around Panov; fewer half-sentences, more 'yes, brother'

Fellow_soldiers_in_the_cordon.quality: Easy, joking, mutually protective; the only social world he has in the Caucasus
Fellow_soldiers_in_the_cordon.power_dynamic: Equal; he is the cheerful one others lean on for small repairs and small laughs
Fellow_soldiers_in_the_cordon.speech_register_shift: Loosest register here — village idioms, jokes, complaints about food and weather

His_brother_at_home.type: Family — the brother whose lot he took
His_brother_at_home.quality: Loved, missed, faintly resented in a way he will not name
His_brother_at_home.power_dynamic: Avdeev gave up his life for the brother's; the debt runs in both directions and neither speaks of it
His_brother_at_home.speech_register_shift: Softer, slower, more rural — when he speaks of him, the soldier-slang drops away

His_wife.type: Family — distant, barely known after years of service
His_wife.quality: Tender, abstract, almost imagined; she exists for him as a direction more than a person
His_wife.power_dynamic: Absent; the relationship survives on letters and memory
His_wife.speech_register_shift: Quietest register — short sentences, no jokes, often a question he does not finish

Russian_officers_above_him.quality: Polite, opaque; he performs the cheerful soldier they want to see
Russian_officers_above_him.power_dynamic: Total — they name him only when he dies, and then only as a statistic
Russian_officers_above_him.speech_register_shift: Stiff, clipped, all 'yes sir' and 'no sir'; the real Avdeev disappears entirely

The_Empire_as_abstraction.type: Structural — the system that owns his life
The_Empire_as_abstraction.quality: Unseen, unanswerable; he never addresses it directly
The_Empire_as_abstraction.power_dynamic: He is a unit; it is a machine — the gap between 'Avdeev' and 'one private killed' is the whole point of his arc
The_Empire_as_abstraction.speech_register_shift: He never speaks to it; when it is named near him, he goes quiet

fusion_decision.rationale: The Avdeev episode is a self-contained symbolic counterweight to the imperial dispatch — fusing him into another soldier would erase Tolstoy's anti-imperial statement about the namelessness of peasant casualties.

example_lines.under_pressure: Keep low. Keep low and load. That's all there is to it now.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: When the snow goes, I'll write home. Tell my brother the cow's worth keeping. He'll laugh — he always laughs at my letters.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Don't tell me about the Tsar, brother. The Tsar doesn't know my name and never will. And there you are.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Some days I drink so I won't think. Some days I think anyway. I don't know what we're doing here. I really don't.

Nikitin.name: Nikitin
Nikitin.initial_description: Russian soldier, morose and dissatisfied; wants the company commander held accountable for borrowing from the chest.

Ukrainian Soldier.name: Ukrainian Soldier
Ukrainian Soldier.initial_description: Third unnamed soldier; makes a dry joke about Nikitin's crooked mouth; minor voice in the night-watch scene.

Bondarenko.name: Bondarenko
Bondarenko.initial_description: Russian soldier on night watch with Avdeev and Panov; minor background presence, about to recount a dream when interrupted

Simon Vorontsov.name: Simon Vorontsov
Simon Vorontsov.initial_description: Prince Simon Mikhailovich Vorontsov, Commander of the Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp, son of the Commander-in-Chief; identified as the 'Prince' the scouts seek
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_013: Prince, Commander of the Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp, son of the Commander-in-Chief; long-faced, wearing insignia and gold cords
Simon Vorontsov.age: approximately 30s (only inferred from rank as Imperial Aide-de-Camp and active regimental commander; not stated explicitly in source)
Simon Vorontsov.origin: Russian aristocracy; son of Prince Mikhail Vorontsov, Viceroy of the Caucasus
Simon Vorontsov.social_status: Russian Prince, Commander of the Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp
Simon Vorontsov.want: To deliver Hadji Murat as a coup of his command — to prove that the Vorontsov name on the Caucasian line is more than nepotism, that he can hand his father a victory bigger than any battlefield.
Simon Vorontsov.need: To recognise that personal honour and imperial service are not the same thing — that the system he serves will betray the men he personally pledged to protect, and that his polished hospitality cannot save Hadji Murat from Petersburg.
Simon Vorontsov.fear: Being seen as the inadequate son — the Prince who inherited a regiment but bungled the most important guest the Caucasus ever sent him.
Simon Vorontsov.contradiction: Treats Hadji Murat with genuine warmth in his tent and at his table — but does not, will not, cannot intervene against the orders that surround and ultimately doom him. His decency is real and useless at the same time.

arc.act_1_start: Confident regimental commander receiving the surrendered Avar at his outpost; sees Hadji Murat's defection as a personal triumph and a vindication of Vorontsov policy.
arc.act_2_change: Hosts Hadji Murat in his household, observes him as a man rather than a prize, begins to sense the gulf between his own pledged word and what Tiflis and Petersburg intend to do.
arc.act_3_end: Stays within the system. Continues to write the correct dispatches as Hadji Murat is moved further from his protection. His complicity is by silence, not by malice — which is the colder Tolstoian indictment.
arc.arc_type: Revelation (without action) — he understands what is happening but does not break ranks; the recognition itself is the arc.

voice_profile.voice_description: Simon speaks in the rounded, faintly French-inflected Russian of a young aristocrat raised in salons and seasoned at the front. His sentences are courteous, slightly formal, and almost never raised; even orders sound like invitations. There is a measured warmth in his voice that conceals — sometimes from himself — the iron of the institution he represents.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Highly formal, classical-gymnasium educated, code-switches into French phrases when at ease; military register clean and unadorned when on duty
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Medium-length, balanced, complete clauses; rarely fragments; tendency toward declarative reassurance rather than questions; occasional periodic sentence when offering hospitality or apology
verbal_tics: 'You have my word' — used sincerely, often, and in retrospect tragically
'My dear ___' as opener even to subordinates
Slips into French ('je vous en prie', 'mon cher') under social ease
'It is only a matter of...' — softening formula before delivering bad news
avoidances: Crude language; will never swear, even under fire
Direct contradiction of a superior, even when he privately disagrees
Open expression of doubt about imperial policy
Boasting about his own lineage; he will not mention his father unprompted
Anything that would publicly shame a guest
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion is filtered through manners. Distress shows as exaggerated courtesy; anger as a quieter voice and a longer pause; affection as small, formal gestures (a hand on the shoulder, a dish offered, a chair drawn closer). He almost never names what he feels.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, even-paced, with deliberate pauses; rarely accelerates; the rhythm itself is a form of authority and of self-restraint

example_lines.line: You will dine with us tonight, of course. My wife has been impatient to meet you.
The order is from Tiflis. I have written what I can. You have my word that you will be treated as a guest, not a prisoner.
If we can bring his family out of the mountains, gentlemen, this whole war changes shape. Imagine it.
I am not in the habit of breaking my word, Colonel. I will thank you to remember that, even when Petersburg forgets.
I cannot move him from where they have placed him. I find that... I find that I cannot.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'you_have_my_word': He means: I will protect you as far as my rank allows — and we are about to discover together how short that distance is.
subtext_rules.when_he_offers_food_or_quarters: He means: I am converting a political prisoner into a personal guest, because hospitality is the only register in which I can treat you decently.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_after_a_dispatch: He means: I have read the order, I disagree, I will not say so aloud.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'it_is_only_a_matter_of_tim: He means: it will not happen, and I am asking you to share the lie with me.

voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Highly formal, classical-gymnasium educated, code-switches into French phrases when at ease…
voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Medium-length, balanced, complete clauses; rarely fragments; tendency toward declarative…
verbal_tics: 'You have my word' — used sincerely, often, and…
'My dear ___' as opener even to subordinates
avoidances: Crude language; will never swear, even under fire
Direct contradiction of a superior, even when he…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: You will dine with us tonight, of course. My wife has been impatient to meet you.

relationships.quality: Genuine respect tinged with romantic orientalism; pledges protection in good faith and watches that pledge erode upward through the chain of command.
relationships.power_dynamic: Simon has formal power (rank, freedom, language); Hadji Murat has moral power (he is the man who trusted him). The dynamic inverts the longer they sit at the same table.
relationships.speech_register_shift: Drops French entirely with Hadji Murat. Slows his Russian, simplifies his syntax, uses Hadji Murat's name often. The voice becomes plainer, almost reverent, when they are alone.

relationships.quality: Affectionate, slightly indulgent; he relies on her as the household's emotional translator while he keeps his official mask on.
relationships.power_dynamic: Public deference to him, private equality; she sees the costs on his face that he will not put into words.
relationships.speech_register_shift: Most relaxed register; French phrases return; sentences shorten; the only person to whom he will admit, in fragments, that something is wrong.

relationships.quality: Filial respect intertwined with professional ambition and a quiet anxiety about adequacy.
relationships.power_dynamic: Heavily asymmetric upward — his father is both parent and commander-in-chief; almost everything Simon does is, in some sense, addressed to him.
relationships.speech_register_shift: Most formal of all; rank-titles ('Your Excellency', 'father' only in private letters); reports rather than converses.

relationships.quality: Courteous authority; runs his staff by example rather than fear; quietly disliked by careerists who find him too soft on the Avar.
relationships.speech_register_shift: Crisper, shorter sentences; military register; the warmth retracts but the courtesy does not.

relationships.quality: His tragedy lives here. He believes — and continues to believe — that the system he serves is honourable enough to honour his word for him. It is not.
relationships.power_dynamic: He is small. The institution is total.
relationships.speech_register_shift: Written-dispatch register: passive constructions, precedent-citations, no first person.

fusion_decision.rationale: Simon Vorontsov and his father Mikhail Vorontsov occupy distinct dramatic functions — Simon is the personal host who pledges his word in his own tent, Mikhail is the distant Viceroy whose institutional authority overrides that word; collapsing them would erase the father-son betrayal vector that gives the Vorontsov plotline its Tolstoian moral weight, and the suggested fusion with Marya Vasilevna is a CUT-not-fusion (her thematic Baryatinsky subplot belongs elsewhere or is dropped, not absorbed into a male officer).

example_lines.line: You will dine with us tonight, of course. My wife has been impatient to meet you.
The order is from Tiflis. I have written what I can. You have my word that you will be treated as a guest, not a prisoner.
If we can bring his family out of the mountains, gentlemen, this whole war changes shape. Imagine it.
I am not in the habit of breaking my word, Colonel. I will thank you to remember that, even when Petersburg forgets.
I cannot move him from where they have placed him. I find that... I find that I cannot.

Marya Vasilevna.name: Marya Vasilevna
Marya Vasilevna.initial_description: Princess Vorontsova, Simon's wife, famous Petersburg beauty; large-built, large-eyed, black-browed; exercises magnetic social power over men around her
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_026: Princess at Vorontsov's house; mentioned at chunk's end as Vorontsov arrives home with Hadji Murad
Chunk chunk_027: Princess, Vorontsov's wife; elegantly dressed, socially adept, charmed by Hadji Murad's flush when she extended her hand
Chunk chunk_028: Wife of Prince Vorontsov; speaks French with her husband; observes and translates Hadji Murad's gestures socially; acts as social lubricant in the reception scene.
Chunk chunk_045: Vorontsov's wife, the 'Sirdar's wife'; hosts the evening party, goes about half-dressed among visitors, exchanges pleasantries with Hadji Murad through interpreter
Marya Vasilevna.social_status: Princess; wife of the Viceroy of the Caucasus, Prince Simon Vorontsov; one of the famous beauties of Petersburg
Marya Vasilevna.want: To remain the irreplaceable social center of Tiflis — to host the war, the Viceroy and the wild guest from the mountains all in the same evening, and to do it more elegantly than Petersburg ever could.
Marya Vasilevna.need: To feel, even for one evening, that her beauty signifies something beyond ornament — that she has touched a real human being and not merely arranged him on the dinner-plan.
Marya Vasilevna.fear: That she is, at bottom, decoration: that the empire her husband administers will go on perfectly well without her, and that her famous magnetism is only the small currency of garrison gossip.
Marya Vasilevna.contradiction: She prides herself on a cosmopolitan, French-tongued openness, yet she can only meet Hadji Murad through an interpreter and a fan; her warmth toward him is genuine and at the same time a performance staged for the room.

arc.starting_state: The reigning hostess of the Vicerégal palace; assured, half-dressed among visitors, treating Hadji Murad's arrival as the season's most exquisite social coup.
arc.change: The closer she comes to Hadji Murad — his blush when she gives him her hand, his refusal to be charmed in the ordinary way — the more she senses an order of seriousness her salon cannot domesticate; her witty French commentary thins.
arc.end_state: Withdraws to the edge of the frame; when news of his death reaches Tiflis she is silent in a way she would once have filled with talk — a small, private acknowledgement that one of her guests was real.

voice_profile.voice_description: Marya Vasilevna speaks in the polished, slightly amused register of a Petersburg salon transplanted to a war zone — French sliding into Russian and back without seam, every sentence shaped to keep the room comfortable. Her phrasing is light, parenthetical, fond of the affectionate diminutive, and edged with a dry irony she never lets curdle into sarcasm. Beneath the lightness, the rhythm is controlled: she finishes other people's awkward moments for them, and she rarely lets a silence last long enough to become a question.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Highly formal-aristocratic; trilingual (Russian, French, German) with French as the language of intimacy and wit; Biblical and literary allusions used playfully; era-correct mid-19th-century court Russian; no military jargon, no peasant idiom.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Medium-length, well-balanced sentences with frequent subordinate clauses; tendency to soften statements into questions ('n'est-ce pas?', 'don't you think?'); occasional fragments when she is genuinely moved — those fragments are the tell.
verbal_tics: switches into French at the moment a thought becomes intimate or risky ('mais voyons…', 'mon cher')
addresses everyone with a small endearment ('my dear prince', 'cher ami')
ends observations with a soft tag question that gives the listener the last word
refers to Tiflis as 'our little Petersburg' with audible quotation marks
avoidances: raised voice or raw anger — vulgarity, in her code, is louder than cruelty
direct political opinion in mixed company; she lets her husband carry the line
religious or moral pronouncements — she finds them provincial
any admission of boredom, fatigue or fear, especially in front of officers
the language of the war itself: words like 'killing', 'corpse', 'mutilated' do not pass her lips
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is signaled by a shift in tempo, not volume: a sentence that breaks off, a French phrase where Russian was expected, a hand placed briefly on a sleeve. When she is genuinely struck — by Hadji Murad's bow, by a piece of news from the line — she goes quieter, not louder, and finishes the room's sentences a beat too quickly.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and legato in public; in private with her husband, faster and more clipped, French-inflected, almost conspiratorial.

example_lines.line: Tell the prince I am dressing — and that he is, as always, half an hour too punctual for Tiflis.
Smile, mon cher, smile — our guest has ridden a long way to be looked at, and we owe him at least that much courtesy.
Did you see him blush when I gave him my hand? Simon — a man like that, blushing in our drawing-room. The evening is saved.
No, no — speak French, please. I will not have that word said in this room, not tonight, not in front of him.
He was not a guest, Simon. He was a man. And we — we set a place for him, and went on talking.

subtext_rules.I_am_in_control_of_this_room: spoken as a compliment to someone else ('how splendidly you tell that story, general')
subtext_rules.I_find_you_interesting: asks for the translator a second time, slowly, watching the listener and not the interpreter
subtext_rules.I_disapprove: switches into French mid-sentence and lets the silence afterwards do the work
subtext_rules.I_am_frightened: becomes more charming, never less; the wit gets faster, the eyes get stiller
subtext_rules.I_have_been_moved: stops speaking before her sentence is finished and turns toward the window

voice_profile_compact.example_line: Tell the prince I am dressing — and that he is, as always, half an hour too punctual for Tiflis.

relationships.quality: affectionate, slightly maternal, conspiratorial; she is his unacknowledged co-administrator of Tiflis society
relationships.power_dynamic: publicly his consort, privately his equal — she edits him in French while he speaks Russian
relationships.register_shift: in private: faster, more familiar, French diminutives, dry edits of his evening; in public: ceremonious, deferential, finishes his half-sentences only when they begin to falter

relationships.type: hostess and exotic guest; an unexpected, unspoken fascination
relationships.quality: warm, curious, slightly disturbed; he is the first guest she cannot fully translate
relationships.power_dynamic: she is the social superior in her own house and yet, in his presence, briefly the supplicant — the language barrier inverts her usual advantage
relationships.register_shift: speaks more slowly, drops the irony, prefers French even though he understands neither French nor Russian; the lightness becomes a kind of formality

relationships.power_dynamic: absolute within the drawing-room; she decides who is interesting this season
relationships.register_shift: fluid French-Russian code-switching, abundant endearments, tag questions that disguise commands as invitations

relationships.type: (post-fusion) the same role — she now also delivers the single intervention that interrupts a general's tactless remark at the Tiflis dinner
relationships.quality: social-correction by elegance, never by reproach
relationships.power_dynamic: her interruption is unanswerable because it is phrased as concern for the guest's comfort
relationships.register_shift: from drawing-room banter to one short, perfectly weighted French sentence that closes the subject

fusion_decision.rationale: Absorb Manana Orbelyani's single intervention at the Tiflis dinner into Marya Vasilevna; she already fulfills the identical dramatic function (social moderator at the imperial salon) and a feature script cannot carry two interchangeable hostess figures.

example_lines.line: Tell the prince I am dressing — and that he is, as always, half an hour too punctual for Tiflis.
Smile, mon cher, smile — our guest has ridden a long way to be looked at, and we owe him at least that much courtesy.
Did you see him blush when I gave him my hand? Simon — a man like that, blushing in our drawing-room. The evening is saved.
No, no — speak French, please. I will not have that word said in this room, not tonight, not in front of him.
He was not a guest, Simon. He was a man. And we — we set a place for him, and went on talking.

Poltoratsky.name: Poltoratsky
Poltoratsky.initial_description: Company commander, broad red-faced man who exchanged out of the Guards; infatuated with Marya Vasilevna to the point of distraction during card play
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_021: Russian officer commanding two companies of woodcutters beyond the Shagirinsk Gate; formerly at Cadet College with Baron Freze
Poltoratsky.origin: Russian; former Guards officer (St. Petersburg social milieu) transferred to active service in the Caucasus
Poltoratsky.social_status: Nobility; company commander, ex-Guards — declassed by transfer to a frontier line regiment but retains Petersburg manners
Poltoratsky.want: To distinguish himself in the field and win Marya Vasilevna's regard — the two pursuits are entangled in his mind, both about being seen as a man of consequence
Poltoratsky.need: To stop performing the Guards-officer role and confront the actual cost of the Caucasus war he has volunteered into
Poltoratsky.fear: Being unmasked as ridiculous — the provincial Guards dandy who lost his composure over a married woman and his nerve under Chechen fire
Poltoratsky.contradiction: Carries himself with Petersburg sophistication while serving in a war he treats half as a sporting expedition; mocks the war's pettiness yet commands woodcutters into ambush with cheerful obedience

arc.start_state: Bored, infatuated, half-playing soldier — loses at cards because he cannot stop staring at Marya Vasilevna; treats the next morning's expedition as routine
arc.change: Beyond the Shagirinsk Gate the woodcutting party takes fire; the same red face that blushed in the salon now flushes under real bullets; meets Hadji Murat's world as a witnessing officer rather than a salon hero
arc.end_state: Sobered without being transformed — still a Guards officer at heart, but the Caucasus has stopped being a backdrop and become a thing that kills men he commanded

voice_profile.voice_description: Poltoratsky speaks in the polished, slightly drawling cadence of a Petersburg drawing room transplanted to a forward camp — quick to a witticism, slower to a serious admission. His sentences carry Guards-mess affectation: French tags, jocular understatement, the practiced ease of a man who has been taught that emotion is provincial. Under pressure the polish thins and a clipped, almost barked command-voice surfaces, as if a second man steps forward from beneath the salon manner.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, educated nobility; military-French interlarded with cavalry-mess slang; mid-19th-century officer-class register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average length medium; in society longer, balanced, with subordinate clauses and ironic asides; in command short, verbal-led, almost telegraphic; tendency toward statements with rhetorical questions used for charm
verbal_tics: Mais voyez-vous as a soft conversational opener
'My dear fellow' / 'mon cher' when addressing brother officers
'Splendid' as default approval, regardless of stakes
Trailing 'eh?' on light questions to invite agreement
Murmured 'enchanting' under his breath when Marya Vasilevna is in the room
avoidances: Never speaks plainly about his own feelings for Marya Vasilevna — to do so would be vulgar
Never complains aloud about the Caucasus posting; that would admit it was a punishment, not an adventure
Never uses peasant or coarse soldiers' language even with his own men
Never admits fear in the moment; only afterwards, and then as anecdote
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Deflects feeling into wit and gallantry; expresses warmth through extravagant courtesy; expresses fear through accelerated cheerfulness; expresses tenderness only obliquely, in a stilled face or a sentence left unfinished
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Alternating — languid and ornamental in society, clipped and percussive under fire

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Splendid hand, gentlemen — and if I keep losing at this rate I shall have to marry an heiress before breakfast.
example_lines.under_pressure: Skirmishers forward — keep the line, keep the line, do not bunch on me.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Mais voyez-vous, when she laughs the whole room is suddenly worth the journey from Petersburg.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You will oblige me, Captain, by remembering that those were my men in the ravine — not yours, not the staff's, mine.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I thought it would be... lighter than this. One imagines the Caucasus, you see. One does not imagine the smell.

subtext_rules.I am charmed: means 'I am undone and afraid she sees it'
subtext_rules.It was nothing: means 'it was very bad and I will not give it the dignity of words'
subtext_rules.Splendid fellow: means either genuine admiration or a polite disposal of someone he finds tedious — tone carries the difference
subtext_rules.We had a warm morning of it: means men under his command were killed
subtext_rules.She plays cards beautifully: means he cannot stop watching her hands

voice_profile_compact.example_line: Splendid hand, gentlemen — and if I keep losing at this rate I shall have to marry an heiress before breakfast.

Marya Vasilevna.type: Unconfessed romantic infatuation across a social barrier (she is married, her husband is his superior's circle)
Marya Vasilevna.quality: Tender, distracted, slightly absurd — observed by everyone except, he hopes, her
Marya Vasilevna.power_dynamic: She holds all of it; his rank cannot reach her station as hostess and married woman
Marya Vasilevna.speech_register_shift: Drops into his most polished, French-flecked, ironic-gallant register; sentences shorten and slow; never directly addresses feeling

Baron Freze.type: Old comrade — fellow Cadet College alumnus, now both posted in the Caucasus
Baron Freze.quality: Easy, fraternal, the shorthand of shared schooldays
Baron Freze.power_dynamic: Roughly equal; mutual ribbing without status anxiety
Baron Freze.speech_register_shift: Most relaxed register — schoolboy nicknames, half-finished sentences, mess-room laughter

His soldiers / woodcutting company.quality: Paternal-cheerful in the Guards manner — courteous to NCOs, formally correct, not intimate
His soldiers / woodcutting company.speech_register_shift: Drops the French and the irony; voice becomes clipped, declarative, audible at distance

Hadji Murat (as observed enemy-then-defe.type: Enemy commander turned object of fascinated Russian observation
Hadji Murat (as observed enemy-then-defe.quality: Curiosity tinged with the romance of the 'noble mountaineer' — Poltoratsky reads him through Petersburg eyes
Hadji Murat (as observed enemy-then-defe.power_dynamic: None directly; Poltoratsky is a witness, not an interlocutor
Hadji Murat (as observed enemy-then-defe.speech_register_shift: Speaks of him in the elevated, slightly theatrical register reserved for legends — the Caucasus made into a story he can tell back home

Senior staff officers (e.g. Vorontsov ci.type: Subordinate to a circle he socially belongs to but militarily depends on
Senior staff officers (e.g. Vorontsov ci.quality: Punctilious deference layered over salon familiarity
Senior staff officers (e.g. Vorontsov ci.power_dynamic: They outrank him; he flatters without grovelling
Senior staff officers (e.g. Vorontsov ci.speech_register_shift: Formal address, military correctness, the French tags retained as a class-marker rather than a flourish

fusion_decision.rationale: Poltoratsky is the load-bearing Russian-officer POV that bridges the Tiflis salon world (Marya Vasilevna, card play, infatuation) with the active Caucasus front (woodcutting expedition, fire beyond the Shagirinsk Gate); collapsing him into Butler, Avdeev or Major Petrov would either fuse incompatible functions (Butler is the late-novel disillusioned newcomer; Avdeev is the doomed peasant-soldier; the Major is the cynical Mentor-Herald) or destroy the salon-to-front bridge that no other single character carries — instead, the smaller satellites Adjutant, Tikhonov and Freze can be absorbed into Poltoratsky per the extraction notes.

example_lines.under_pressure: Skirmishers forward — keep the line, keep the line, do not bunch on me.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Mais voyez-vous, when she laughs the whole room is suddenly worth the journey from Petersburg.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You will oblige me, Captain, by remembering that those were my men in the ravine — not yours, not the staff's, mine.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I thought it would be... lighter than this. One imagines the Caucasus, you see. One does not imagine the smell.

Regimental Adjutant.name: Regimental Adjutant
Regimental Adjutant.initial_description: Officer sitting straight with cold expression on handsome face; partners against Vorontsov; flushes with irritation at Poltoratsky's blunders

Petersburg Tutor.name: Petersburg Tutor
Petersburg Tutor.initial_description: Shaggy young man of gloomy appearance, graduate of Petersburg University; sent to Caucasus by Princess Vorontsova to tutor her son from first marriage

Adjutant.name: Adjutant
Adjutant.initial_description: Prince Vorontsov's adjutant, white-ringed hands, deals cards, pleased when princess joins game against him

Tikhonov.name: Tikhonov
Tikhonov.initial_description: Poltoratsky's comrade sharing his bedroom; wakes briefly to ask about cards and Marya Vasilevna
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_016: Poltoratsky's orderly or companion, reminds him they must start at six, speaks practically
Chunk chunk_021: Poltoratsky's subaltern, present at the woodcutting operation in the Shalin glade

Vovilo.name: Vovilo
Vovilo.initial_description: Poltoratsky's domestic serf, over forty, taken into household service as a boy; drunk on return; broods over his unfree life and the illusory promise of emancipation after the Caucasus campaign
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_016: Poltoratsky's personal servant, takes his boots and clothes, protests about waking him if he fights

Ivan Petrovich.name: Ivan Petrovich
Ivan Petrovich.initial_description: Ordnance sergeant, off-screen; Vovilo's envy-object — has a salary, is married, expects discharge

Khanefi.name: Khanefi
Khanefi.initial_description: Hadji Murad's sworn brother who manages his household affairs; first to take hold of Hadji Murad's bridle and stirrup upon arrival at the forest camp
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_020: One of Hadji Murad's murids who carries equipment (ewer) in the group's packs; logistical support figure
Chunk chunk_025: Thick-set, hairy Avar murid with meeting eyebrows; manages all of Hadji Murad's property and leads the pack-horse with saddle bags.

Chunk chunk_054: Hadji Murad's fourth murid, an Avar from the same aoul as HM. Hairy face and neck, arched moss-like chest; strong, hardworking, quietly obedient. Became HM's blood-brother after a traditional ritual (no shaving or nail-cutting for two months, then nursing from HM's mother Patimat) to escape a blood feud — his father killed HM's uncle.
Chunk chunk_091: One of Hadji Murad's two murids at the fort; shaggy, volatile — nearly killed Marya Dmitrievna after she expelled him from the kitchen
Chunk chunk_092: Hadji Murad's shaggy sworn brother, a gifted singer with a high tenor voice who performs mountain songs with extraordinary clarity and expression
Khanefi.origin: Avar; same aoul as Hadji Murad (Khunzakh region, Dagestan)
Khanefi.social_status: Murid and sworn blood-brother of Hadji Murad; household-steward role within HM's inner circle
Khanefi.want: To serve Hadji Murad's household with absolute fidelity — to keep the horses fed, the saddlebags packed, the master's roof in order
Khanefi.need: To repay the blood-debt that bound him to HM's family — to know that his life, given to HM through Patimat's milk, has been honourably spent
Khanefi.fear: Dishonour — failing the man whose mother nursed him, or being separated from HM's household and cast back toward the blood feud he escaped
Khanefi.contradiction: Tender enough to weep when he sings the mountain songs in his clear high tenor, yet volatile enough to nearly kill a Russian woman for shooing him from a kitchen

arc.act_1: Silent steward at HM's side in the forest camp — first to seize the bridle, last to sleep, body bent over saddlebags
arc.act_2: At the Russian fort his volatility surfaces (the Marya Dmitrievna incident) and his voice surfaces (the song of the avenger) — the murid reveals he is also a man with a throat
arc.act_3: Falls beside HM in the final stand, loading rifles, holding horses — anonymous in death as he was in life, his loyalty consummated

voice_profile.voice_description: Khanefi speaks rarely and in fragments, the way a man speaks who believes words are owed to God and the master, not wasted on the world. When he does open his mouth it is either a curt logistical report or, unexpectedly, song — a high clear tenor that carries the grief of the mountains. His Russian, when he must use it, is broken and percussive; his Avar is pious, proverb-laden, and economical.

vocabulary.level: informal, low-literate, rural Avar; pious register through Quranic and proverbial fragments

sentence_structure.complexity: simple, paratactic; coordinated by 'and' rather than subordinated
sentence_structure.tendency: statements and imperatives dominate; questions rare; fragments common

verbal_tics: 'Bismillah' before any action involving horses or weapons
Calls HM 'agha' or 'my brother' — never by name
'It is done' as a closing on any report
Mutters proverbs about horses, blood, and milk under his breath
avoidances: Never speaks of his own father (the man who killed HM's uncle)
Never jokes — leaves levity to Khan Mahoma
Never questions an order from HM
Never speaks Russian when Avar will do
Never boasts of his own deeds
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Affect bleeds through the body, not the mouth — a tightened jaw, a hand on the dagger, a longer silence. Joy and grief alike find their only release in song; outside song, his face is the message.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured and heavy, with long pauses; accelerates only under threat; in song, suddenly fluent and soaring

example_lines.line: The bay is lame. I will lead him.
Agha — the road north is closed. Two hours, no more.
(singing, high tenor) The earth dried on Hamzad's grave, my brothers — but the avenger did not sleep.
Woman. Step back. I do not warn twice.
Your mother gave me her milk. What I have is yours. There is nothing else to say.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_it_is_done: the matter is closed and questioning him would be an insult
subtext_rules.when_he_calls_someone_agha: he places himself beneath them in the Caucasian honour-hierarchy — a vow, not a courtesy
subtext_rules.when_he_sings: he is grieving or steeling himself; song replaces the confession he cannot speak
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_mid_sentence: the unspoken half is the blood feud he escaped — his father's crime

voice_profile_compact.example_line: The bay is lame. I will lead him.

Hadji_Murad.type: Sworn blood-brother and murid; nursed by HM's mother Patimat in the ritual that ended the feud between their families
Hadji_Murad.quality: Reverential, total, almost filial — HM is master, brother, and saviour in one
Hadji_Murad.power_dynamic: Khanefi absolutely subordinate; obeys without comment
Hadji_Murad.speech_register_shift: Voice drops, addresses HM as 'agha'; sentences shorten; eyes lower

Eldar.type: Fellow murid; closest peer in HM's inner circle
Eldar.quality: Wordless solidarity; the two move as a single body around HM
Eldar.power_dynamic: Equal, but Eldar is the active companion and Khanefi the household steward
Eldar.speech_register_shift: Speaks Avar in clipped half-sentences; often communicates by gesture alone

Gamzalo.type: Fellow murid; the warrior beside the steward
Gamzalo.quality: Cool respect; each guards a different flank of HM's life
Gamzalo.power_dynamic: Parallel functions; neither commands the other
Gamzalo.speech_register_shift: Operational, terse — orders about horses, watches, ammunition

Khan_Mahoma.quality: Quiet disapproval of Khan Mahoma's frivolity; tolerates him for HM's sake
Khan_Mahoma.power_dynamic: Equal in rank; Khanefi the gravity to Khan Mahoma's lightness

Bata.quality: Mentoring without warmth — corrects, does not praise
Bata.power_dynamic: Senior; gives instructions about pack-horses and weapons

Marya_Dmitrievna.type: Russian woman at the fort who expelled him from her kitchen
Marya_Dmitrievna.quality: Combustible, near-violent — the only relationship in which his rage shows openly
Marya_Dmitrievna.power_dynamic: Khanefi denied agency in her domain, responds with the dagger he never draws on his own
Marya_Dmitrievna.speech_register_shift: Broken, percussive Russian; or no words, only the threat in his body

Patimat_HM_mother.type: Foster-mother through the milk ritual; the woman who ended the blood feud by nursing him
Patimat_HM_mother.quality: Sacred; the source of every loyalty he has
Patimat_HM_mother.power_dynamic: Filial reverence — she is the reason he lives
Patimat_HM_mother.speech_register_shift: Speaks her name only in invocation, never in narration

fusion_decision.rationale: The five murids (Eldar, Khan Mahoma, Gamzalo, Bata, Khanefi) form a deliberate ensemble in which Khanefi alone carries the milk-brotherhood backstory, the household-steward function, and the singer's voice — collapsing him erases the blood-feud-resolution motif and the song-of-the-avenger scene that thematically mirrors HM's own arc.

example_lines.line: The bay is lame. I will lead him.
Agha — the road north is closed. Two hours, no more.
(singing, high tenor) The earth dried on Hamzad's grave, my brothers — but the avenger did not sleep.
Woman. Step back. I do not warn twice.
Your mother gave me her milk. What I have is yours. There is nothing else to say.

Gamzalo.name: Gamzalo
Gamzalo.initial_description: A Chechen murid; silently takes up a rifle and stations himself on guard without being asked when Hadji Murad warns of pursuit
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_025: Chechen murid, short red beard, no eyebrows or lashes, blind in one eye, scar across nose and face. Menacing presence.
Gamzalo.social_status: Murid in Hadji Murad's inner circle; veteran warrior, sworn man
Gamzalo.want: To guard Hadji Murad's body and honor through every step of the defection, and if it comes to it, to die well at his side.
Gamzalo.need: To accept that loyalty to a man can outlast the cause that bound them — that his oath is to Hadji Murad, not to Shamil's banner or the Russian paper.
Gamzalo.fear: Dying disarmed, indoors, by a stranger's bullet rather than on horseback with a rifle in his hand; being separated from Hadji Murad at the moment of reckoning.
Gamzalo.contradiction: He keeps the silence of a man who trusts no one, yet has placed his entire life inside another man's decisions without ever asking why.

arc.act1_start: Wordlessly takes up a rifle and posts himself when Hadji Murad warns of pursuit — the ready instrument, fully formed, no doubt in him.
arc.act2_change: Watches Hadji Murad ride further into Russian hands; the orders he carries out begin to feel more like rituals of a man preparing his own death. He starts to speak, briefly, where before he only acted.
arc.act3_end: Falls fighting beside Hadji Murad in the final stand — the warrior's death he wanted, the only ending his loyalty allowed.

voice_profile.voice_description: Gamzalo speaks the way he moves — short, low, deliberate, as if every word costs a cartridge. His speech is Avar-flavored, formal in religious phrasing, blunt in everything else. He prefers silence; when he does speak, the sentence ends before a Russian would expect it to.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal mountaineer register with Islamic formulae (Allah, the Prophet, the imam); no Russian loanwords used willingly; no abstractions, no metaphors he did not learn from elders.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average length 4–8 words. Mostly statements and imperatives. Questions are rare and clipped. Fragments common. Grammar plain, almost paratactic — one fact set beside the next without subordination.
verbal_tics: If God wills
It is enough
Hadji Agha (when addressing Hadji Murad)
leaves silences where another man would explain
avoidances: Never jokes
Never speaks of his own feelings directly
Never praises or flatters Russians, even in safety
Never asks Hadji Murad to explain a decision
Never uses Russian forms of address
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion shows in what he does with his rifle and his good eye, not in words. Anger is a longer silence; grief is a shorter sentence; affection is the act of standing one pace closer than necessary.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, slow, with hard stops. No run-on. Occasional muttered prayer under the breath as a private metronome.

example_lines.line: The horses are watered. I will sleep by them.
Riders. From the ravine. Two, maybe three. Hadji Agha — say the word.
If God wills it, by Friday we ride home and your sons eat from your hand again.
You trust their paper. I trust this rifle. We will see which holds.
I have followed you a long time, Hadji Agha. Wherever you fall, I fall one step before.

subtext_rules: When he says 'It is enough', he means: stop talking, decide, I am ready to die now.
When he reports a fact about terrain or horses, he is really saying: I have already calculated our escape route.
When he names Hadji Murad 'Hadji Agha', he is reaffirming the oath, not addressing him.
When he goes silent in front of Russian officers, he is refusing to translate himself for them.
When he prays audibly, he is preparing himself, and warning the others, that he expects to fight.

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Gamzalo speaks the way he moves — short, low, deliberate, as if every word costs a cartridge.
voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Informal mountaineer register with Islamic formulae (Allah, the Prophet, the imam); no Russian…
voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Average length 4–8 words. Mostly statements and imperatives. Questions are rare and clipped.…
verbal_tics: If God wills
It is enough
avoidances: Never jokes
Never speaks of his own feelings directly
voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When he says 'It is enough', he means: stop talking, decide, I am ready to die now.
voice_profile_compact.example_line: The horses are watered. I will sleep by them.

Hadji_Murad.power_dynamic: Subordinate by oath, equal in the moment of combat
Hadji_Murad.register_shift: Speaks least of all, addresses him as 'Hadji Agha', never contradicts in front of others; in private will state a tactical disagreement in one flat sentence and then obey.

Khanefi.type: Fellow murid, comrade in HM's inner circle
Khanefi.quality: Functional brotherhood — household-keeper and warrior, two halves of one service
Khanefi.power_dynamic: Roughly equal; Gamzalo carries the harder edge
Khanefi.register_shift: Curt, practical, two-word exchanges about horses, watches, supplies; no warmth in language, full warmth in reliance.

Eldar.quality: Quiet mentorship under fire; corrects by example, not lecture
Eldar.register_shift: Imperatives only — 'Take the left', 'Eyes up' — never explanations.

Russian_officers_Vorontsov_etc.type: Hosts who are also captors in his eyes
Russian_officers_Vorontsov_etc.quality: Cold civility on the surface; absolute distrust beneath
Russian_officers_Vorontsov_etc.power_dynamic: Outwardly subordinate guest, inwardly an armed witness
Russian_officers_Vorontsov_etc.register_shift: Speaks only when addressed, in the shortest possible answers, never volunteers; declines Russian forms of politeness.

Shamil.quality: Reverence for the office, no comment on the man's quarrel with Hadji Murad
Shamil.power_dynamic: Absent authority; Gamzalo refuses to weigh in
Shamil.register_shift: Will not name Shamil in conversation if he can avoid it; if forced, uses the title only.

fusion_decision.rationale: Among the five murids around Hadji Murad, Gamzalo holds a distinct dramatic function — the grim, scarred veteran whose silent readiness embodies the cost of loyalty — which neither Khanefi (logistics) nor Eldar (younger follower) can carry without flattening the inner-circle's tonal range.

Baron Freze.name: Baron Freze
Baron Freze.initial_description: Ex-officer of the Guards, reduced to the ranks for dueling; fellow cadet-college classmate of Poltoratsky; present at the Shalin glade encampment

Freze.name: Freze
Freze.initial_description: Russian officer, Poltoratsky's comrade; addressed as 'Kostya', sent back to his company when shots are fired
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_023: Russian officer (Baron Freze per graph context), jumps up and moves quickly toward his smoke-covered company position; minor field officer.

Bulka.name: Bulka
Bulka.initial_description: Vorontsov's curly-haired, keen-eyed stepson, age six; fascinated by Hadji Murad's warrior reputation; briefly sits on Hadji Murad's knee
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_028: Young son of Vorontsov and Marya Vasilevna; admires Hadji Murad's dagger and receives it as a gift per Chechen kunak custom; sits on Hadji Murad's knee.

General Kozlovsky.name: General Kozlovsky
General Kozlovsky.initial_description: Commander of the left flank at Grozny; named recipient of Vorontsov's formal report of Hadji Murad's defection
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_097: Commander of the left flank until Baryatinsky's arrival; honored at farewell dinner; nervous about toasts

Meller-Zakomelsky's Aide-de-Camp.name: Meller-Zakomelsky's Aide-de-Camp
Meller-Zakomelsky's Aide-de-Camp.initial_description: Military aide who delivers the general's demand that Hadji Murad be presented immediately; functionally anonymous messenger.

Meller-Zakomelsky's wife.name: Meller-Zakomelsky's wife
Meller-Zakomelsky's wife.initial_description: Model-looking little lady, wife of Baron Meller-Zakomelsky, present during the confrontation but silent.

Doctor (Fort Hospital).name: Doctor (Fort Hospital)
Doctor (Fort Hospital).initial_description: Military doctor who examines Avdeev's wound and notices old scars on his back

Typhus Patient.name: Typhus Patient
Typhus Patient.initial_description: Soldier with high fever, one of four patients already in the hospital ward

Ague Patient.name: Ague Patient
Ague Patient.initial_description: Pale soldier with dark shadows under his eyes, expecting a fever attack, yawning continuously

Hand-Wound Soldier.name: Hand-Wound Soldier
Hand-Wound Soldier.initial_description: Soldier wounded in the hand during a raid three weeks prior; up and about

Shoulder-Wound Soldier.name: Shoulder-Wound Soldier
Shoulder-Wound Soldier.initial_description: Soldier wounded in the shoulder during a raid three weeks prior; sitting on a bed

Avdeev's Father.name: Avdeev's Father
Avdeev's Father.initial_description: Old peasant farmer, wakes before dawn to prepare threshing floor; stoic and hardworking
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_034: Old peasant man, patriarchal head of threshing household, bitterly compares Akim unfavorably to the absent Peter/Avdeev
Chunk chunk_035: Frugal old man who reluctantly agrees to send Peter one ruble from oat-sale proceeds; regrets having let Peter go

Avdeev's Sister-in-Law.name: Avdeev's Sister-in-Law
Avdeev's Sister-in-Law.initial_description: Wife of the brother in whose stead Peter enlisted; works the threshing floor

Avdeev's Niece.name: Avdeev's Niece
Avdeev's Niece.initial_description: Daughter of Avdeev's brother, nearly marriageable age; works alongside family

Akim.name: Akim
Akim.initial_description: Eldest son of the peasant family, scolded by father for laziness and drinking; he attends to horses before joining threshing

Avdeev's Mother.name: Avdeev's Mother
Avdeev's Mother.initial_description: Old woman who brings news of the Elder's work order, sides with father in scolding Akim
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_035: Old woman who mourns Peter's absence and persuades her husband to send him money; dictated the letter to the church clerk
Chunk chunk_036: Peter's mother, who dictates the farewell letter through tears; receives the returned letter with news of Peter's death and weeps, then has a requiem said for him.

Avdeev's Daughter-in-law.name: Avdeev's Daughter-in-law
Avdeev's Daughter-in-law.initial_description: Turns oats with flail; tries briefly to defuse father-son quarrel

Akim Avdeev.name: Akim Avdeev
Akim Avdeev.initial_description: Peter Avdeev's elder brother, stays home while Peter was conscripted; accused by mother of being Peter's inferior

Akim's Wife (daughter-in-law).name: Akim's Wife (daughter-in-law)
Akim's Wife (daughter-in-law).initial_description: Attempts to mediate between mother-in-law and Akim with 'let bygones be bygones'

Avdeev's Grandson.name: Avdeev's Grandson
Avdeev's Grandson.initial_description: The old man's grandson who drives the last sledge to town.

Aksinya.name: Aksinya
Aksinya.initial_description: Peter Avdeev's widow, who had left the family home and gone into service; laments loudly upon hearing of his death.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_037: Peter Avdeev's widow; publicly mourns her husband while privately relieved — she is pregnant by a shopman she lives with and hopes to marry.

Shopman (Aksinya's lover).name: Shopman (Aksinya's lover)
Shopman (Aksinya's lover).initial_description: Unnamed merchant who has promised to marry Aksinya once she is free; father of her unborn child.

Vanka.name: Vanka
Vanka.initial_description: Aksinya's infant son from her marriage to Peter Avdeev; mentioned as the orphaned child she laments.

Michael Vorontsov.name: Michael Vorontsov
Michael Vorontsov.initial_description: Russian Viceroy of the Caucasus, 70+; English-educated, ambitious, charming aristocrat who wields imperial power with elegant ease. Conqueror of Napoleon at Krasnoe. Receives the news of Hadji Murad's surrender.
Michael Vorontsov.social_status: Prince, Viceroy of the Caucasus, Adjutant-General; one of the wealthiest and most powerful men in the Empire
Michael Vorontsov.want: To deliver Hadji Murad as a political prize to St. Petersburg — a bloodless trophy that will confirm his mastery of the Caucasus and consolidate the Tsar's favor.
Michael Vorontsov.need: To recognize that the elegant machinery of empire he serves cannot accommodate a man like Hadji Murad without grinding him to pieces — and that his own civility is a polite form of complicity in that grinding.
Michael Vorontsov.fear: Being seen as an old man whose moment has passed; falling out of imperial favor; being judged a soft administrator rather than the hero of Krasnoe and conqueror of the East.
Michael Vorontsov.contradiction: He treats Hadji Murad with a courtesy and warmth that is not entirely feigned — he genuinely admires the man — yet he simultaneously and without hesitation reports him to Petersburg in terms designed to keep him a hostage of the Russian system.
Michael Vorontsov.justification: An empire is held together by the patience of men like me; if I can turn the most dangerous warrior of the Caucasus into a guest at my table without firing a shot, then what others would call duplicity I call statesmanship.

arc.starting_state: Self-assured viceroy at the height of his prestige, receiving the news of Hadji Murad's defection as a personal triumph and a delicate problem he is uniquely qualified to manage.
arc.change: As Hadji Murad's situation tightens — his family in Shamil's hands, his impatience growing — Vorontsov maintains diplomatic poise while routing the matter through Petersburg, refusing to commit either to ransom or to release; his gentleness becomes the cage.
arc.end_state: When Hadji Murad bolts and is killed, Vorontsov receives the head as official news, expresses dignified satisfaction to the court, and returns untouched to his routines — confirmed in his methods, untroubled by the man he had charmed.
arc.arc_summary: He does not change; the world breaks around him while his composure remains intact. That untouchedness IS his tragedy — and the film's indictment of empire.

voice_profile.voice_description: Vorontsov speaks in a low, even register that almost never rises, delivering both pleasantries and lethal decisions in the same musical cadence. His sentences are graceful and slightly long, lightly perfumed with French phrases when he wishes to mark social distance, and he prefers the conditional and the suggestion to the imperative. The effect is of a man who never gives orders because the room has already understood what he wants.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Highly formal; aristocratic 19th-century Russian-court register, fluent French interpolations, English-educated polish; no military slang, no profanity
voice_profile.education_level: Highest — English schooling, European travel, classical reading; quotes literature and history with ease

sentence_structure.complexity: Subordinated, periodic, often hinging on a polite qualifier ('it would seem,' 'one might suppose')
sentence_structure.tendency: Statements framed as suggestions; questions used as soft commands; almost no fragments

verbal_tics: Mon cher / mon ami when addressing subordinates he wishes to flatter
'It would seem...' as a softener before a hard judgment
'I shall write to Petersburg' as a recurring deferral
Pausing on a guest's name and repeating it once, gently, as if tasting it
Closing statements with 'is it not so?' to extract assent without demanding it
avoidances: Raised voice or open anger — never
Direct refusal; he defers, qualifies, or postpones
Crude or soldierly language; coarse jokes
Open admission of political calculation; he presents calculation as kindness
Emotional self-disclosure; he does not say 'I feel'
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings are expressed through micro-shifts: a longer silence, a slower closing of the hand, a smile held a beat too long. Affection arrives as quoted poetry or a remembered detail about the listener's family. Displeasure arrives as cooler courtesy and a sentence shortened by one clause.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured throughout; he does not accelerate under pressure — pressure makes him slower, quieter, more precise.

example_lines.a_neutral: Tell our guest the carriage is ready whenever it pleases him, and that my wife has arranged for the children to greet him in the garden.
example_lines.b_under_pressure: I shall write to Petersburg this evening, mon cher, and until His Majesty has been so good as to reply, we shall make Hadji Murad's stay among us as agreeable as our hospitality allows.
example_lines.c_hopeful: If we handle this gently — and we shall, is it not so? — the Caucasus will be quieter by spring than it has been in twenty years, and not a single regiment moved.
example_lines.d_angry_hurt: It would seem, Colonel, that you have mistaken my patience for permission. I am sure, on reflection, you will wish to reconsider the tone of your last dispatch.
example_lines.e_vulnerable_honest: I am an old man, Maria Vasilyevna. I have outlived Napoleon and most of my friends. What troubles me tonight is that I rather like our Tatar — and I am not certain that liking him will save him.

subtext_rules: When he says 'I shall write to Petersburg,' he means 'I am placing you in a cage of paperwork from which there is no exit.'
When he says 'You are entirely free, of course,' he means 'You are entirely watched.'
When he praises a man's bravery, he is measuring whether that bravery can be put to imperial use.
When he speaks French, he is reminding the listener which class of conversation they are permitted to enter.
When he falls silent and folds his hands, the decision has already been made.

verbal_tics: Mon cher / mon ami when addressing subordinates…
'It would seem...' as a softener before a hard…
avoidances: Raised voice or open anger — never
Direct refusal; he defers, qualifies, or postpones

voice_profile_compact.example_line: Tell our guest the carriage is ready whenever it pleases him, and that my wife has arranged for the children to greet him in the garden.

Hadji Murad.quality: Outwardly warm, genuinely curious, fundamentally instrumental; admires Hadji Murad as one admires a rare animal one intends to keep
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: Total — Vorontsov controls movement, communication, and ransom; Hadji Murad's freedom is a fiction Vorontsov maintains for both their dignities
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: Slows further, softens further, French recedes; he speaks almost intimately, as if to a brother-in-arms — which makes the betrayal subtler and deeper

Maria Vasilyevna (his wife).quality: Respectful partnership; she is one of the few people to whom he reveals genuine fatigue or doubt
Maria Vasilyevna (his wife).power_dynamic: Equal in private, public asymmetry observed in form only
Maria Vasilyevna (his wife).speech_register_shift: Drops court formality; uses her name and patronymic, allows fragments and sighs, may admit moral ambivalence

Nikolai I (the Tsar).quality: Loyal, deferential, strategically careful; Vorontsov knows the Tsar distrusts independent prestige and writes accordingly
Nikolai I (the Tsar).power_dynamic: Total subordination upward; Vorontsov bows even on paper
Nikolai I (the Tsar).speech_register_shift: Letters become stately and impersonal; the lyrical Vorontsov disappears into court formula

Russian officers (Loris-Melikov, junior.quality: Cordial, encouraging, occasionally testing them with a soft question that has a sharp answer hidden inside
Russian officers (Loris-Melikov, junior.power_dynamic: Absolute, but exercised through suggestion, not command
Russian officers (Loris-Melikov, junior.speech_register_shift: Adds 'mon cher' and first names; becomes slightly playful when satisfied, fractionally cooler when displeased

Hadji Murad's nukers (murids/escort).quality: Performed hospitality; he ensures their comfort to the centimetre while his officers watch every door
Hadji Murad's nukers (murids/escort).speech_register_shift: Speaks through interpreters with elaborate courtesy, choosing nouns that flatter their honor while binding their movement

fusion_decision.rationale: Vorontsov is a historical anchor and the structural face of imperial soft power on the Caucasian side; his function — civilized captor whose courtesy is the cage — cannot be absorbed by Nikolai I (raw autocracy) or by any military officer (overt force) without collapsing the novel's central indictment that empire kills most efficiently when it smiles.

example_lines.b_under_pressure: I shall write to Petersburg this evening, mon cher, and until His Majesty has been so good as to reply, we shall make Hadji Murad's stay among us as agreeable as our hospitality allows.

example_lines.d_angry_hurt: It would seem, Colonel, that you have mistaken my patience for permission. I am sure, on reflection, you will wish to reconsider the tone of your last dispatch.
