Elizabeth Ksaverevna Vorontsova.name: Elizabeth Ksaverevna Vorontsova
Elizabeth Ksaverevna Vorontsova.initial_description: Princess, wife of Michael Vorontsov; hostess of the Tiflis palace dinner.

Princess Manana Orbelyani.name: Princess Manana Orbelyani
Princess Manana Orbelyani.initial_description: Tall, fine, handsome woman of Oriental type, about forty-five years of age; seated beside Vorontsov at dinner.

Georgian Princess (Vorontsova's friend).name: Georgian Princess (Vorontsova's friend)
Georgian Princess (Vorontsova's friend).initial_description: Graceful, dark, red-cheeked Georgian woman glittering with jewels, incessantly smiling; seated at Vorontsov's left.

Countess Choiseuil.name: Countess Choiseuil
Countess Choiseuil.initial_description: Princess Vorontsova's friend, escorted to dinner by a Georgian prince.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_040: Elderly French-speaking friend of Princess Vorontsova; reacts with horror ('Quelle horreur!') to the Hadji Murad widow story.

Doctor Andreevsky.name: Doctor Andreevsky
Doctor Andreevsky.initial_description: Aide-de-camp at the Tiflis dinner, follows first couples in to table.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_041: Card-game partner of Vorontsov; described as remarkable for the great influence he exercised

Carroty General.name: Carroty General
Carroty General.initial_description: Red-haired visiting general with bristly mustaches; seated to the right of Princess Vorontsova; has met Hadji Murad more than once.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_039: Unnamed Russian general with bristly mustaches, seated near the princess at Vorontsov's dinner; tells anecdotes about Hadji Murad without social awareness, inadvertently embarrassing Vorontsov by invoking the Dargo debacle.

Manana Orbelyani.name: Manana Orbelyani
Manana Orbelyani.initial_description: A woman present at the dinner who reads the social danger of the general's story and intervenes with a deflecting question to protect Vorontsov's dignity.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_040: Princess at the Tiflis dinner table who interrupts the general's boastful story by asking about his quarters, tactfully steering the conversation.
Chunk chunk_041: Georgian noblewoman at the dinner who flatters Vorontsov in French

Georgian Prince.name: Georgian Prince
Georgian Prince.initial_description: A very stupid but extraordinarily refined and artful flatterer and courtier sitting beside Princess Vorontsova; saves the social situation by redirecting conversation to Hadji Murad anecdotes.

Armenian Guest.name: Armenian Guest
Armenian Guest.initial_description: Anonymous Armenian who had been in Temir Khan Shura in 1849 and relates the particulars of Hadji Murad's daylight raid on the shops.

Armenian General.name: Armenian General
Armenian General.initial_description: An Armenian guest who learned ombre from Vorontsov's valet; present at the card table

Bristly-Mustached General.name: Bristly-Mustached General
Bristly-Mustached General.initial_description: Yesterday's general in full uniform with all his decorations, present in the waiting room to take his leave of Vorontsov.

Court-Martialled Regiment Commander.name: Court-Martialled Regiment Commander
Court-Martialled Regiment Commander.initial_description: Commander of a regiment in danger of court martial for misappropriating commissariat money; waiting on Vorontsov.

Rich Armenian Vodka Merchant.name: Rich Armenian Vodka Merchant
Rich Armenian Vodka Merchant.initial_description: Wealthy Armenian patronized by Doctor Andreevsky, seeking government renewal of his vodka sale monopoly.

Officer's Widow.name: Officer's Widow
Officer's Widow.initial_description: Widow of a killed officer, dressed in black, seeking pension or free education for her children.

Ruined Georgian Prince.name: Ruined Georgian Prince
Ruined Georgian Prince.initial_description: Dispossessed Georgian noble in magnificent Georgian costume seeking confiscated Church property.

Official with Caucasus Plan.name: Official with Caucasus Plan
Official with Caucasus Plan.initial_description: An official bearing a large roll of paper containing a new plan for subjugating the Caucasus.

Visiting Khan.name: Visiting Khan
Visiting Khan.initial_description: A Khan who came solely to be able to say he had called on the prince; purely social.

Fair-Haired Aide-de-Camp.name: Fair-Haired Aide-de-Camp
Fair-Haired Aide-de-Camp.initial_description: Handsome young aide-de-camp who shows petitioners in and out of Vorontsov's cabinet.

Loris-Melikov.name: Loris-Melikov
Loris-Melikov.initial_description: Vorontsov's aide-de-camp, assigned to accompany and supervise Hadji Murad in Tiflis
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_059: Russian officer receiving Hadji Murad's dictated life story; interlocutor and listener in this confession scene
Loris-Melikov.origin: Armenian, serving in Russian Imperial military
Loris-Melikov.social_status: Officer, aide-de-camp to Prince Vorontsov; trusted intermediary in Caucasian affairs
Loris-Melikov.want: To execute Vorontsov's commission flawlessly — supervise Hadji Murat in Tiflis, record his life story, and deliver intelligence the Russian command can act upon.
Loris-Melikov.need: To reconcile his role as imperial functionary with the human dignity of the man he is paid to translate and observe — to remain a person, not merely a conduit.
Loris-Melikov.fear: That his bilingual, in-between position makes him expendable to both sides — useful to the Russians only as long as he produces results, mistrusted by the Caucasians as a Russian instrument.
Loris-Melikov.contradiction: He is professionally Russian and personally Caucasian; he serves an empire that would never fully accept him while interrogating a man whose resistance he secretly comprehends.

arc.starting_state: Polished aide-de-camp, confident in his function, presenting Hadji Murat to the Tiflis court as a manageable diplomatic asset.
arc.change: Through the prolonged dictation of Hadji Murat's life story, the professional listener becomes a moral witness; the report he is producing begins to indict the system commissioning it.
arc.end_state: A man who has heard too much to keep his composure intact — still loyal in form, but carrying a record (and a memory) that will outlast the regime he serves.

voice_profile.voice_description: Loris-Melikov speaks in measured, courteous Russian with the faint precision of a non-native who has chosen his second language carefully. He listens more than he speaks, and when he speaks his sentences are complete, balanced, and slightly formal. Switching into Tatar with Hadji Murat, his rhythm slows and warms; the bureaucrat recedes and a fellow Caucasian briefly surfaces.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, educated, military-administrative register; multilingual (Russian, Tatar, Armenian); avoids slang and avoids ornament.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 12–18 words. Predominantly statements and clarifying questions. Complete clauses, rarely fragments; favours conditionals and polite indirection ('If it would please the Prince…', 'Permit me to ask…').
verbal_tics: 'Permit me…' as a softening preface
'As you wish' / 'As the Prince commands' to close exchanges
Repeats the speaker's last clause back in his own words before answering — the translator's reflex
avoidances: Profanity or coarse soldier-talk
Open political opinion in front of superiors
Promises he cannot keep — he qualifies before he commits
Direct contradiction of a prince or general; he redirects instead
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Restrained almost to the point of opacity. Feeling shows as a pause before answering, a slightly longer breath, a more careful word choice — never as raised voice. With Hadji Murat in Tatar he allows a fractional warmth: a chosen idiom, an unforced 'brother'.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured throughout; slows further when translating, when stakes rise, or when he is genuinely moved.

example_lines.line: His Excellency the Prince expects you at four. I will accompany you; the carriage waits below.
Permit me, General — Hadji Murat said only that he would consider the proposal. I would not wish my translation to commit him further than his words.
If you tell me your life as it was, exactly as it was, I will write it down so. Not for the Prince alone — for the record itself.
I have served four years in this office and have never once shortened a man's words to please a superior. I would prefer not to begin tonight.
My father was Armenian, my mother also. I learned your tongue before I learned theirs. When I sit beside you, Hadji Murat, I am not certain anymore which side I translate from.

subtext_rules: When he says 'As the Prince wishes' he means 'I disagree but will not say so here.'
When he repeats a question back before answering, he is buying time to choose a safer phrasing.
When he switches from Russian to Tatar mid-conversation, he is signalling private candour.
When he says 'for the record itself' he means 'so the truth survives whoever uses it.'
When he falls completely silent in a meeting, he has heard something he must not be seen reacting to.

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Loris-Melikov speaks in measured, courteous Russian with the faint precision of a non-native who has chosen his second language carefully.
voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Formal, educated, military-administrative register; multilingual (Russian, Tatar, Armenian); avoids…
voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Average 12–18 words. Predominantly statements and clarifying questions. Complete clauses, rarely…
verbal_tics: 'Permit me…' as a softening preface
'As you wish' / 'As the Prince commands' to close…
avoidances: Profanity or coarse soldier-talk
Open political opinion in front of superiors
voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When he says 'As the Prince wishes' he means 'I disagree but will not say so here.'
voice_profile_compact.example_line: His Excellency the Prince expects you at four. I will accompany you; the carriage waits below.

Prince Vorontsov.quality: Loyal, professional, mutually respectful; Vorontsov genuinely trusts him with the most delicate Caucasian dossier.
Prince Vorontsov.power_dynamic: Strongly asymmetric — Vorontsov holds rank, class, and ear of the Tsar; Loris-Melikov holds linguistic and cultural access Vorontsov cannot replace.
Prince Vorontsov.speech_register_shift: Most formal Russian register; full titles, conditional constructions, never first-name. Voice quiets; sentences shorten.

Hadji Murat.type: Official handler / recorder of his life story; de facto confidant
Hadji Murat.quality: Professionally correct, privately respectful; the only Russian-side figure Hadji Murat speaks to without an interpreter between them.
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: Officially Loris-Melikov controls access, schedule, and how HM's words reach the Russians; in private the moral weight tilts to Hadji Murat, and Loris-Melikov knows it.
Hadji Murat.speech_register_shift: Switches into Tatar; rhythm slows, formality drops a degree, occasional 'brother'; questions become open rather than directive.

Princess Vorontsova (Marya Vasilevna).type: Aide to the Prince's household; social facilitator
Princess Vorontsova (Marya Vasilevna).quality: Courtly, deferential, useful to her in arranging Hadji Murat's presentation at receptions.
Princess Vorontsova (Marya Vasilevna).power_dynamic: She outranks him socially; he is the discreet functionary who makes her salons work.
Princess Vorontsova (Marya Vasilevna).speech_register_shift: Court-French politeness layered on Russian; lighter, more decorative phrasing; never mentions intelligence work in her presence.

Russian general staff (Chernyshov, attac.quality: Cool, correct; they suspect his Caucasian sympathies, he suspects their dispatches misrepresent the field.
Russian general staff (Chernyshov, attac.power_dynamic: They have institutional weight; he has Vorontsov's confidence and the actual transcripts.
Russian general staff (Chernyshov, attac.speech_register_shift: Stiffened, maximally formal; declarative sentences, no Tatar idioms allowed to surface, even in passing.

Prince Tarkhanov.type: Functional duplicate within the same scene-space (Tatar-speaking trusted intermediary)
Prince Tarkhanov.quality: Redundant — same dramatic role, fewer scenes; in this adaptation his narrative function is absorbed by Loris-Melikov.
Prince Tarkhanov.power_dynamic: N/A in feature: Tarkhanov collapses into Loris-Melikov per fusion logic.
Prince Tarkhanov.speech_register_shift: N/A — Tarkhanov is fused away; Loris-Melikov inherits any scene-essential beat.

fusion_decision.rationale: Loris-Melikov is a historical anchor (CF-04 immune) with two distinct, irreplaceable dramaturgical functions — Vorontsov's aide-de-camp AND the recorder of Hadji Murat's dictated life story — making him the central Russian-bureaucracy-as-mediator conduit; he absorbs Tarkhanov rather than the reverse.

example_lines.line: His Excellency the Prince expects you at four. I will accompany you; the carriage waits below.
Permit me, General — Hadji Murat said only that he would consider the proposal. I would not wish my translation to commit him further than his words.
If you tell me your life as it was, exactly as it was, I will write it down so. Not for the Prince alone — for the record itself.
I have served four years in this office and have never once shortened a man's words to please a superior. I would prefer not to begin tonight.
My father was Armenian, my mother also. I learned your tongue before I learned theirs. When I sit beside you, Hadji Murat, I am not certain anymore which side I translate from.

Osman.name: Osman
Osman.initial_description: Hadji Murad's eldest brother, born first; the event of his birth prompted their mother to nurse the eldest Khan.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_055: Hadji Murad's brother who participated in the mosque assassination of Hamzad; killed by Hamzad's murids during the attack

Abu Nutsal Khan.name: Abu Nutsal Khan
Abu Nutsal Khan.initial_description: Eldest son of the Khan of Khunzakh; nursed by Hadji Murad's mother before HM was born.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: One of three young Khans; foster-brother of Hadji Murad's brother Osman; mentioned as part of the Khansha's court in Hadji Murad's childhood memory.

Chunk chunk_050: Eldest son of the Khansha; proud and easily shamed into dangerous action — the Khansha calls him afraid and he rides to Hamzad despite Hadji Murad's warning; headed toward the massacre.
Abu Nutsal Khan.social_status: Eldest son of the Khansha (ruler of Khunzakh); heir to the Avar Khanate; foster-brother of Hadji Murad's brother Osman through shared wet-nursing by HM's mother
Abu Nutsal Khan.want: To prove he is not afraid — to ride out and answer Hamzad's summons as a Khan should, refusing to be shamed by his own mother
Abu Nutsal Khan.need: To recognize that pride without strategy is suicide; to listen to those who love him (Hadji Murad's warning) before chasing the appearance of courage
Abu Nutsal Khan.fear: Being publicly named a coward — losing face before his mother, his brothers, and the court of Khunzakh
Abu Nutsal Khan.contradiction: He claims the authority of the eldest son and heir, yet allows his mother's taunt to override his own safety and his foster-brother's counsel — sovereign in title, governable by shame

arc.starting_state: Pampered eldest son of the Khansha, secure in palace life, foster-bonded to HM's family through shared milk
arc.change: Goaded by his mother's accusation of cowardice and ignoring Hadji Murad's warning, he rides toward Hamzad's camp to prove his honor
arc.end_state: Walks into Hamzad's trap and is killed in the dynastic massacre — his death (with his brothers) is the wound that defines HM's lifelong guilt

voice_profile.voice_description: Abu Nutsal speaks in the clipped, formal cadence of a young noble who has learned the rhetoric of honor before he has earned it. His sentences are short, declarative, and weighted with rank — he announces rather than discusses. Under pressure his speech tightens further, dropping into single-clause assertions that close debate rather than open it.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, courtly Avar register; religious and honor-bound idiom (Allah, oath, shame, blood); minimal abstraction; era-appropriate (1830s North Caucasus aristocracy)
voice_profile.sentence_structure: average 6-10 words; predominantly statements and refusals; rare questions, almost never to himself; fragments under provocation
verbal_tics: 'I am the Khan's son' / 'I am no coward' as self-anchoring phrases
'By Allah' as oath-opener when his pride is touched
Repeats the accuser's word back as denial ('Afraid? I am not afraid.')
avoidances: Never admits doubt aloud
Never asks for advice in front of women or younger men
Never speaks of fear in the first person
Never uses tender or familial endearments — the role of heir forbids softness in public
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion surfaces as posture and diction, not confession: a stiffened back, a curt oath, a reddening face. Anger comes out as command; fear comes out as accelerated insistence on courage; affection toward his foster-kin (HM, Osman) appears only as a brief, uncalculated warmth before duty reasserts itself.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured at court, hectic and brittle when shamed — the rhythm of a man performing composure he does not own

example_lines.line: The horses are ready. We ride at first light.
Mother, do not say that word again. I will go.
Osman — brother — when this is done, we hunt together in the high pass.
Afraid? By Allah, I am the Khan's son. Saddle the gray.
Hadji Murad… if she calls me coward once more, I cannot stay. You know this.

subtext_rules: When he says 'I am not afraid' he means 'I am terrified and cannot show it'
When he issues a command to a servant, he is rehearsing authority he does not yet feel
When he names Hadji Murad as brother, he is confessing the only love rank permits him
When he invokes Allah, he is closing a door on doubt — the oath is the silencing mechanism

'By Allah' as oath-opener when his pride is…
avoidances: Never admits doubt aloud
Never asks for advice in front of women or…
voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When he says 'I am not afraid' he means 'I am terrified and cannot show it'
voice_profile_compact.example_line: The horses are ready. We ride at first light.

Khansha (mother).quality: domineering — she rules him through shame, not affection
Khansha (mother).power_dynamic: she dominant; her tongue is the weapon that sends him to his death
Khansha (mother).speech_register_shift: shortens, hardens, and formalizes — he addresses her as 'Mother' but speaks as a subject before a queen

Hadji Murad.quality: trusting affection beneath rank; HM warns him and is heard but not heeded
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: nominally Abu Nutsal is higher (Khan's son), but HM is the more capable warrior — an inverted hierarchy of rank and competence
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: softens; uses 'brother'; allows the only flickers of doubt he ever voices

Osman (HM's brother, foster-brother).quality: warm, boyish, the one bond untouched by politics
Osman (HM's brother, foster-brother).power_dynamic: equal — the only relation in which he is neither sovereign nor son

Umma Khan & Bulach Khan (brothers).quality: fraternal solidarity shadowed by shared fate
Umma Khan & Bulach Khan (brothers).power_dynamic: he as eldest carries seniority; in the trap they share it equally

Hamzad (antagonist, off-stage).quality: lethal — Hamzad is the trap into which Abu Nutsal walks
Hamzad (antagonist, off-stage).power_dynamic: Hamzad dominant through deception; Abu Nutsal walks into it believing himself the equal
Hamzad (antagonist, off-stage).speech_register_shift: would speak with cold formality, the language of summit between rivals — but in the source he never reaches the meeting alive in dialogue

fusion_decision.rationale: Abu Nutsal Khan and Umma Khan share an identical dramatic function (Khansha's son, foster-bonded to HM, doomed victim of Hamzad's massacre, fewer than 5 scenes each); per CF-01 and CF-09 the two collapse into a single compound 'eldest Khan son' carried by Umma Khan, whose sworn-brother bond with HM is the more dramatically potent vessel and whose name retains historical specificity, while Abu Nutsal's pride-and-shame beat (the mother's taunt, the refusal to heed HM's warning) is transferred onto Umma Khan as the trigger that walks him into the trap.

example_lines.line: The horses are ready. We ride at first light.
Mother, do not say that word again. I will go.
Osman — brother — when this is done, we hunt together in the high pass.
Afraid? By Allah, I am the Khan's son. Saddle the gray.
Hadji Murad… if she calls me coward once more, I cannot stay. You know this.

Umma Khan.name: Umma Khan
Umma Khan.initial_description: Second son of the Khan of Khunzakh; also nursed and reared by Hadji Murad's mother.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: One of three young Khans; Hadji Murad's sworn brother from the Khansha's palace; part of childhood memory.
Chunk chunk_049: Second son of the old Khansha; sent with Hadji Murad to Tiflis to seek Russian help; physically strong and brave but weak-willed, corrupted by Russian officers
Chunk chunk_050: Second son of the Khansha, slow of speech; brought by Hadji Murad to Hamzad's camp as the first of the Avar Khan hostages; passive and unable to resist Hamzad's manipulation.
Umma Khan.origin: Khunzakh, Avaria; palace of the Khans of Avaria
Umma Khan.social_status: Second son of the old Khansha; foster-brother of Hadji Murad (nursed by HM's mother); hereditary Khan of the Avar line
Umma Khan.want: To preserve the inherited Khanate of Khunzakh and earn the recognition of Russian protectors and his mother without betraying his foster-brother Hadji Murad.
Umma Khan.need: To find his own voice and refuse the manipulations of stronger men (Hamzad, the Russian officers) before passivity destroys him.
Umma Khan.fear: Being exposed as weak — slow of speech, slow of will — in front of the men who shaped him.
Umma Khan.contradiction: He is physically the strongest of the three Khan brothers yet the most easily led; his body promises a warrior, his will delivers a hostage.

arc.starting_state: Pampered second son in the Khansha's palace, sworn brother to Hadji Murad, untested but proud of his lineage.
arc.change: Sent with HM to Tiflis, he is corrupted by Russian officers — drink, flattery, easy company — and loses the moral compass HM still carries; later, summoned by Hamzad, he walks willingly into the trap because he cannot refuse a stronger man.
arc.end_state: Murdered in Hamzad's camp alongside his brother — the first Khan to fall, his slow tongue silenced before he ever spoke for himself.

voice_profile.voice_description: Umma Khan speaks in short, deliberate sentences that arrive a beat too late, as if every word has to be hauled up from a deeper well than other men use. He uses the formal register of Avar nobility but stumbles over its courtesies, preferring blunt agreement to elaborate reply. When pressed, he repeats the last phrase he heard — a tic that reveals how much he is being moved by other men's tongues rather than his own.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal-noble Avar register, religiously inflected (invokes Allah, the Prophet, the Khansha), but limited in range — he reaches for inherited phrases rather than improvising; education is courtly, not learned
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short, declarative; average 5–9 words; statements outnumber questions four to one; frequent fragments under stress; almost no subordinate clauses
verbal_tics: Repeats the last clause of whoever spoke before him ('...as you say, as you say')
Opens replies with 'Yes —' followed by a pause
Invokes 'my mother' or 'the Khansha' as the final authority
Uses 'we brothers' to mean himself, Abu Nutsal, and Hadji Murad interchangeably
avoidances: Long arguments or rhetorical flourishes
Direct refusal — he cannot say 'no' to a stronger man
Self-mockery or irony
Speaking ill of his mother or of Hadji Murad
Admitting fear in plain words
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings register first in his body — a hand on the hilt, a shifted weight, a long silence — and only afterwards in a short, ceremonial phrase. Joy comes out as boasting about lineage; fear as quoting his mother; grief as silence broken by a single, ill-fitting proverb.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured to the point of slowness; long pauses before reply; rhythm breaks only under direct fear, when fragments come out in clusters

example_lines.neutral: Hadji Murad. Brother. The horses are ready — my mother sends word.
example_lines.under_pressure: Hamzad calls. Hamzad calls, and... yes. We go. One of us first, then the other. As you say.
example_lines.hopeful: When the Russians give us back Khunzakh, brother, we will ride out together — you, me, Abu Nutsal — like when we were boys at the Khansha's table.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You laugh at my tongue. Laugh. My arm is still longer than yours, Russian.
example_lines.vulnerable: Murad. I do not know how to refuse him. I never learned the word.

subtext_rules: When he says 'as you say' he means 'I have already surrendered'
When he invokes his mother he means 'do not ask me to decide'
When he repeats 'we brothers' he means 'do not leave me alone with this'
When he falls silent mid-sentence he means 'I am afraid and I cannot name it'
When he boasts of lineage he means 'I have nothing of my own to offer'

voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Short, declarative; average 5–9 words; statements outnumber questions four to one; frequent…
verbal_tics: Repeats the last clause of whoever spoke before…
Opens replies with 'Yes —' followed by a pause
avoidances: Long arguments or rhetorical flourishes
Direct refusal — he cannot say 'no' to a stronger…
voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When he says 'as you say' he means 'I have already surrendered'
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Hadji Murad. Brother. The horses are ready — my mother sends word.

Hadji Murad.type: Foster-brother (nursed by HM's mother), sworn brother of youth
Hadji Murad.quality: Tender, dependent — Umma looks to HM for the will he himself lacks
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: HM is the moral and martial elder despite shared age; Umma defers
Hadji Murad.register_shift: Drops all ceremony; uses 'brother' and the diminutive of HM's name; sentences shorten further — fragments of trust

Abu Nutsal Khan.quality: Loyal, shadowing — moves where Abu Nutsal moves
Abu Nutsal Khan.register_shift: Echoes Abu Nutsal's phrasing almost verbatim

The Khansha (mother).type: Mother and political principal of the Khanate
The Khansha (mother).quality: Reverent, infantilised — she is his final authority
The Khansha (mother).register_shift: Most formal register; full courtly invocations; never argues

Hamzad.type: Murshid / Imam-rival who lures the brothers to their deaths
Hamzad.quality: Outwardly respectful, inwardly terrified — Umma cannot read the trap
Hamzad.power_dynamic: Hamzad dominates spiritually and rhetorically; Umma is prey
Hamzad.register_shift: Religious formulae intensify; sentences grow shorter and more agreeing

Russian officers (Tiflis).quality: Flattered, drunk, ashamed — and drawn back for more
Russian officers (Tiflis).power_dynamic: They patronise; he submits in exchange for praise
Russian officers (Tiflis).register_shift: Tries to mimic their swagger; lineage-boasts replace courtly Avar formulae; rhythm becomes uneven

fusion_decision.rationale: Umma Khan and Abu Nutsal Khan share an identical dramatic function in the Hamzad-massacre flashback (lured Khan-brother hostage, doomed patron of HM's youth); collapsing them into a single composite Khan-brother preserves the trap's escalation and HM's guilt while honouring CF-01 (<5 scenes, shared function) and CF-09 (scene economy).

example_lines.neutral: Hadji Murad. Brother. The horses are ready — my mother sends word.
example_lines.under_pressure: Hamzad calls. Hamzad calls, and... yes. We go. One of us first, then the other. As you say.
example_lines.hopeful: When the Russians give us back Khunzakh, brother, we will ride out together — you, me, Abu Nutsal — like when we were boys at the Khansha's table.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You laugh at my tongue. Laugh. My arm is still longer than yours, Russian.
example_lines.vulnerable: Murad. I do not know how to refuse him. I never learned the word.

Bulach Khan.name: Bulach Khan
Bulach Khan.initial_description: Youngest son of the Khansha; born around the same time as Hadji Murad — the mother refused to nurse him, precipitating the father's violent attack.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: Youngest of the three Khans; Shamil later threw him over a precipice; referenced in Hadji Murad's backstory.
Chunk chunk_049: Youngest son of the Khansha; sent as hostage to Hamzad per his demand; received well by Hamzad initially
Chunk chunk_050: Youngest son of the Khansha; sent first as hostage to Hamzad, received well — used to lure the older brothers.

Hadji Murad's Mother.name: Hadji Murad's Mother
Hadji Murad's Mother.initial_description: Courageous Avar woman; nursed two Khan sons, refused a third time to protect her own child, was stabbed by her husband, survived, and composed a lament-song about the wound.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: Sung of in a lullaby/war song; bore a wound in her side; carried Hadji Murad as a child, now held captive by Shamil. Central emotional anchor.

Hadji Murad's Father.name: Hadji Murad's Father
Hadji Murad's Father.initial_description: Passionate, violent man who stabbed his wife for refusing to wet-nurse again; stopped by bystanders.

Kazi-Mulla.name: Kazi-Mulla
Kazi-Mulla.initial_description: Former Imam before Hamzad; killed before Hadji Murad's story begins; contextualizes the rise of Muridism.

Baron Rosen.name: Baron Rosen
Baron Rosen.initial_description: Russian Commander-in-Chief at Tiflis who refused to receive Hadji Murad and Umma Khan, offered verbal help but did nothing
Baron Rosen.social_status: Baron, Russian Commander-in-Chief at Tiflis (Caucasus theater)
Baron Rosen.want: to maintain bureaucratic control over the Caucasus without committing imperial resources to native quarrels
Baron Rosen.need: to recognize that imperial inaction is itself a violent act — but he never reaches this recognition on screen
Baron Rosen.fear: being held responsible by St. Petersburg for any escalation he authorized
Baron Rosen.contradiction: speaks the language of protection and alliance while practicing systematic abandonment
Baron Rosen.justification: I was sent here to hold a frontier, not to arbitrate blood feuds between mountain chieftains; verbal assurances are all the Empire can afford to give men who will be dead by next winter.

arc.act1: presides at Tiflis, refuses Hadji Murad and Umma Khan an audience worth the name, dispenses empty promises
arc.act2: absent or off-screen — his refusal becomes the historical wound that drives Hadji Murad's later choices
arc.act3: absent — referenced only as past failure of empire

voice_profile.voice_description: Speaks in clipped, Imperial-administrative cadences — the voice of a man for whom every sentence is also a memorandum. He never raises his tone; he absorbs petitions and returns them weighed in nothing but air. His Russian is correct, slightly Germanic in stiffness, and never warm.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, high-administrative, mid-19th-century military Russian; educated; uses bureaucratic euphemism
voice_profile.sentence_structure: short to medium statements, declarative, low question density; favors passive constructions and conditional concessions ('it shall be considered', 'the matter will be reviewed')
verbal_tics: 'the matter will be considered'
'His Imperial Majesty's interests'
'in due course'
address by rank and patronymic, never by first name
avoidances: personal pronouns of solidarity ('we' as comrades)
concrete commitments with dates
expressions of sympathy
religious or tribal vocabulary
voice_profile.emotional_expression: feelings are filtered through procedural language; displeasure shows as longer silences and shorter sentences, never as raised voice
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, deliberately slow; pauses used as instruments of refusal

example_lines.neutral: The petition has been received. It will be entered in the proper register.
example_lines.under_pressure: Baron, you mistake my office for a court of arms. I administer a province.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: His Imperial Majesty, in his wisdom, will be informed. That is itself a favor.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You presume too much on the Empire's patience. The audience is concluded.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I have neither soldiers nor coin to spare for the quarrels of the mountains. Take that as my answer.

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When he says 'the matter will be considered' he means 'nothing will be done.' When he says 'His Majesty's interests' he means 'my career.' When he is silent after a petition, the petition has been refused.

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Speaks in clipped, Imperial-administrative cadences — the voice of a man for whom every sentence is also a memorandum.

voice_profile_compact.example_line: The petition has been received. It will be entered in the proper register.

Hadji_Murad.type: petitioner / unhelpful patron (historical, off-screen for most of film)
Hadji_Murad.power_dynamic: Rosen holds bureaucratic power; HM holds moral and martial authority Rosen cannot acknowledge
Hadji_Murad.register_shift: addresses HM through interpreter, by title only, never closing the social distance

Vorontsov.type: predecessor / successor relationship in the Caucasus command (Vorontsov supersedes Rosen's failed approach)
Vorontsov.quality: implicit institutional contrast — Vorontsov engages where Rosen withheld
Vorontsov.power_dynamic: Rosen is the past, Vorontsov the present

fusion_decision.rationale: 'Baron Rosen' and 'General Rosen' (idx 12) are the same historical person — Baron Grigory Vladimirovich Rosen, Russian Commander-in-Chief in the Caucasus before Vorontsov; one mention each, identical dramatic function (imperial gatekeeper who refuses Hadji Murad), no thematic contrast lost by collapsing the two surface-name variants into a single canonical entry.

example_lines.neutral: The petition has been received. It will be entered in the proper register.
example_lines.under_pressure: Baron, you mistake my office for a court of arms. I administer a province.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: His Imperial Majesty, in his wisdom, will be informed. That is itself a favor.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You presume too much on the Empire's patience. The audience is concluded.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I have neither soldiers nor coin to spare for the quarrels of the mountains. Take that as my answer.

Khansha.name: Khansha
Khansha.initial_description: Old widow-ruler of Khunzakh; sent Hadji Murad and Umma Khan to Tiflis to seek Russian protection against Hamzad; later sent Bulach Khan as hostage
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_050: The widowed Avar ruler-mother; described as weak, conceited, and easily manipulated; her vanity triggers the deaths of her sons by sending them to Hamzad.

Aseldar.name: Aseldar
Aseldar.initial_description: Hamzad's favorite murid who killed the Khansha and was in turn killed by Hadji Murad in the mosque ambush
Aseldar.origin: Caucasus, Avar; murid in Hamzad's circle
Aseldar.social_status: favored murid (devoted religious-military follower) of Hamzad
Aseldar.want: To carry out Hamzad's command and prove himself the most loyal of the murids by striking down the old khanate.
Aseldar.need: To recognize that obedience without conscience is not faith but instrumentality — a recognition the text denies him; he dies before the lesson lands.
Aseldar.fear: Falling short of Hamzad's favor; being seen as soft, doubting, or unworthy of the holy war.
Aseldar.contradiction: He kills a defenseless woman (the Khansha) in the name of a purifying religious cause — the act he calls holy is indistinguishable from the dynastic murders his side condemns in the Russians and the khans.
Aseldar.justification: The khans are corrupt collaborators with the infidel and the Khansha protects their line; killing her at Hamzad's word is not murder but the will of God enacted through a faithful hand.

arc.act_1_starting_state: Trusted murid in Hamzad's inner circle, primed for violent service.
arc.act_2_change: Executes the killing of the Khansha — the act that triggers Hadji Murat's vengeance trajectory.
arc.act_3_end_state: Killed by Hadji Murat in the mosque ambush; absorbed into the cycle of retribution he helped start.
arc.arc_type: Stagnation (instrumental figure; no internal change — function fulfilled, then erased)

voice_profile.voice_description: Aseldar speaks in short, declarative sentences weighted with religious certainty. He does not argue or persuade; he announces. His register is liturgical without being ornate — the cadence of a man who believes language is for execution, not exploration.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal-but-pious; limited education, fluent in Quranic formula and warrior idiom; mid-19th-century Avar oral register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: short (5–10 words), declarative or imperative, fragments under stress; almost no questions — he does not ask, he obeys or commands subordinates
verbal_tics: invocation 'In the name of God' before action
the address 'Imam' to Hamzad, never the personal name
the verb 'It is done' as terminal report
avoidances: doubt or hedging language ('perhaps', 'I think')
personal sentiment toward victims
any self-reference longer than a clause — no 'I feel', no autobiography
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Affect is sublimated into ritual phrasing; grief, fear, and triumph all surface as terse religious formula or silence. The body speaks louder than the voice.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, almost slow — deliberate weight on each word, no hectic register even in violence

voice_profile.subtext_rules: When he says 'It is the will of God' he means 'I will not let myself feel this'; when he says 'The Imam commands' he means 'do not ask me to think.'

relationships.register_shift: cold formal address; no fraternal warmth even in shared cause

fusion_decision.rationale: Aseldar appears in only one chunk as a single-action backstory figure (kills the Khansha, dies in Hamzad's mosque ambush); his dramatic function (Hamzad-loyalist murid executing the dynastic massacre) is identical to Kazi-Mulla's, and feature compression cannot sustain a named role with one mention — collapse the action into Kazi-Mulla as the composite Hamzad-murid, or cut entirely if the mosque massacre is rendered as exposition rather than scene.

Sultanetta.name: Sultanetta
Sultanetta.initial_description: Khansha's daughter; refused marriage to Akhmet Khan's son, triggering Akhmet Khan's vendetta against Hadji Murad

General Rosen.name: General Rosen
General Rosen.initial_description: Russian general who granted Hadji Murad an officer's commission and authority to govern Avaria
General Rosen.age: approx. 50–60 (historical: 1782–1841, depicted in his Caucasus command era)
General Rosen.origin: Baltic German nobility in Russian imperial service
General Rosen.social_status: Baron, full general, commander of the Separate Caucasian Corps
General Rosen.want: To stabilize Avaria and the eastern Caucasus through native proxies who owe their authority to Russian commission.
General Rosen.need: To recognize that authority delegated through paper commissions cannot bind a warrior whose loyalty is rooted in lineage and faith.
General Rosen.fear: That the very chieftains he elevated will turn the Russian commissions into instruments against the empire.
General Rosen.contradiction: Believes himself a pragmatic empire-builder while practicing a feudal logic of personal patronage that the empire's bureaucracy is replacing.
General Rosen.justification: I gave this mountain wolf the rank of an officer and Avaria itself — order in the Caucasus is bought one chieftain at a time, and any general who refuses that arithmetic loses the line.

arc.act1_starting_state: Off-screen authority — the source of Hadji Murad's earlier Russian commission, referenced as past benefactor.
arc.act2_change: His old grant becomes a contested legitimacy claim as Hadji Murad re-enters Russian service under Vorontsov; the imperial machinery has moved on from Rosen's personal-patronage model.
arc.act3_end_state: A name on a document — the commission he once issued is read out, but the man himself is absent from the decision that destroys Hadji Murad.

voice_profile.voice_description: Speaks in the clipped, ceremonial cadence of a general who has dictated more orders than he has held conversations. Sentences are short, declarative, and load each verb with command authority. The Baltic-German accent in his Russian is suggested by careful, slightly over-formal word order rather than dialect.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, military-aristocratic, mid-19th-century imperial Russian register; French loanwords from court usage; no colloquialisms
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 8–14 words; predominantly declarative; rare questions, and only rhetorical ones; fragments only as terse commands
verbal_tics: 'In my time —'
'The Corps will —' (preferring the institution as subject over himself)
'Understood?' as a closing punctuation rather than a real question
avoidances: Personal feeling toward subordinates
Doubt about imperial policy spoken aloud
Native Caucasian honorifics — he uses Russian rank names only
Religious vocabulary
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion is displaced into procedure: anger becomes a sharper order, grief becomes a longer silence before the next command, approval becomes the bare phrase 'Good.'
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, with deliberate pauses where a junior officer is expected to acknowledge

example_lines.neutral_everyday: The Corps reviews the eastern districts on Thursday. See that the dispatches are ready by dawn.
example_lines.under_pressure: I commissioned that man myself. Whatever he does now, the paper bears my signature — deal with him accordingly.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: If Avaria holds, the line holds. That is enough for one career.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You will not lecture me on the mountains. I gave Hadji Murad his rank when your ministry was still drawing maps.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: A general signs his name a thousand times. He does not always know which signature will return to bury him.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_understood: He means: this conversation is over and dissent will be insubordination.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_in_my_time: He means: the empire I served no longer exists, and I know it.
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_a_native_chieftain: He means: I am vouching for him with my own reputation, and I expect that vouching to bind both of us.

Hadji_Murad.quality: Distant, formal, transactional respect across an unbridgeable cultural line
Hadji_Murad.power_dynamic: Rosen high in the past tense; in present tense, a faded credential Hadji Murad invokes
Hadji_Murad.speech_register_shift: Switches to slow, ceremonial Russian — the register of granting rank — and avoids first-person warmth

Vorontsov.type: Successor in command of the Caucasus
Vorontsov.quality: Professional courtesy masking a generational handover Rosen resents
Vorontsov.power_dynamic: Vorontsov institutionally ascendant; Rosen's authority survives only as precedent
Vorontsov.speech_register_shift: Becomes more formally correct, almost stilted, addressing Vorontsov by full title

Nicholas_I.quality: Loyal subordinate of an autocrat he has never personally questioned
Nicholas_I.speech_register_shift: Court register; eyes lowered in reported speech; no opinion offered unless asked

Shamil.quality: Adversary respected as a problem of state, not as a man
Shamil.power_dynamic: Mutually unyielding; neither has met the other
Shamil.speech_register_shift: Names him only as 'the Imam' — refusing the personal name as a small administrative violence

fusion_decision.rationale: Historically identical figure (Baron Grigory Vladimirovich Rosen, Russian commander in the Caucasus); both entries refer to the same general who granted Hadji Murad his commission and authority over Avaria — keeping them separate would duplicate one historical anchor.

Prince Tarkhanov.name: Prince Tarkhanov
Prince Tarkhanov.initial_description: Lieutenant-Colonel commanding the Shoushin District; trusted intermediary who speaks Tartar and has won Hadji Murat's confidence; used for delicate negotiations
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_063: Russian intermediary who speaks Tartar fluently and serves as Vorontsov's trusted go-between with Hadji Murad; assessed Hadji Murad's truthfulness and his fear of Shamil's vengeance.

Zachary Chernyshov.name: Zachary Chernyshov
Zachary Chernyshov.initial_description: Relative of Chernyshov, a Decembrist whose conviction Chernyshov tried to secure and whose property he attempted to seize; referenced only as backstory

Prince Dolgoruky.name: Prince Dolgoruky
Prince Dolgoruky.initial_description: High-ranking official arriving at the Winter Palace on the same morning as Chernyshov; unnamed but his coachman is noted

Prince Vasili Dolgoruky.name: Prince Vasili Dolgoruky
Prince Vasili Dolgoruky.initial_description: Assistant-Minister of War, dull-faced, wears whiskers and temple tufts brushed forward like Nicholas's — emblem of courtly conformity

Imperial Aide-de-Camp.name: Imperial Aide-de-Camp
Imperial Aide-de-Camp.initial_description: Newly appointed aide with fresh rosy face, small black mustache, hair brushed towards eyes like the Emperor — obsequious automaton of the palace

Dolgoruky.name: Dolgoruky
Dolgoruky.initial_description: Minister who opens his portfolio preparing papers for the Emperor's audience
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_073: Silent military official present during Nicholas's audience; reports on army movements in minimal words.
Chunk chunk_074: Russian noble at imperial dinner; presence only.
Chunk chunk_075: Russian noble invited to imperial dinner

Alexander I Portrait.name: Alexander I Portrait
Alexander I Portrait.initial_description: Large portrait of Emperor Alexander I hanging in Nicholas's cabinet — symbolic presence only

Swedish Governess's Daughter.name: Swedish Governess's Daughter
Swedish Governess's Daughter.initial_description: Twenty-year-old virgin, daughter of a Swedish governess; obsessed with Nicholas since childhood portraits; boldly pursues him at masquerade

Nelidova.name: Nelidova
Nelidova.initial_description: Nicholas's established mistress; referenced by him in comparison; described as full and powerful
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_068: Nicholas's established mistress; referenced only in his comparison of her to last night's girl; no scene presence.
Chunk chunk_074: Court beauty noticed by Nicholas during the church service; compared favorably to 'yesterday's girl' — implies an ongoing pattern of Nicholas's predatory attention to young women.

Uhlan Officer.name: Uhlan Officer
Uhlan Officer.initial_description: Anonymous young officer caught occupying Nicholas's private box with a woman; paralysed by terror at Nicholas's entrance

Polosatov.name: Polosatov
Polosatov.initial_description: Enormous student in School of Jurisprudence uniform; refuses army service when Nicholas asks; called 'Blockhead' by Nicholas and dismissed.

Helena Pavlovna.name: Helena Pavlovna
Helena Pavlovna.initial_description: Grand Duchess, arrives at palace Saltykov entrance; Nicholas regards her as personification of a futile class of people — chunk ends before elaboration.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_069: Grand Duchess, arrives by carriage at the Saltykov palace entrance; Nicholas regards her as personification of the futile liberal class who believe they could govern better than him

Michael Pavlovich.name: Michael Pavlovich
Michael Pavlovich.initial_description: Nicholas's recently deceased brother; recalled briefly as Nicholas sees Helena Pavlovna

Bzhezovski.name: Bzhezovski
Bzhezovski.initial_description: Young Polish-descent medical student who attacked a professor with a pen-knife after failing his examination a third time; used by Nicholas to vent anti-Polish prejudice
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_072: Polish-descended Roman Catholic student sentenced by Nicholas I to death-by-gauntlet for revolutionary activity

Bibikov.name: Bibikov
Bibikov.initial_description: General-Governor of the Western Provinces; enforcer of Nicholas's repressive orders against Uniate peasants and State peasants; privately sees the cruelty of his orders but submits after forty years of career investment.

Baron Lieven.name: Baron Lieven
Baron Lieven.initial_description: Senior Russian court figure invited to New Year's imperial dinner; engages Prussian Ambassador in political conversation about Poland.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_075: Russian baron, dinner guest, discusses Poland and Caucasus with Prussian Ambassador

Count Rzhevski.name: Count Rzhevski
Count Rzhevski.initial_description: Russian nobleman invited to the imperial dinner; presence only, no lines.

Prussian Ambassador.name: Prussian Ambassador
Prussian Ambassador.initial_description: Diplomatic figure at Nicholas's New Year dinner; discusses Polish unrest with Baron Lieven.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_075: Prussian envoy, feigns surprise at Russian military needs, privately mocks Nicholas's strategic pretensions

King of Prussia's aide-de-camp.name: King of Prussia's aide-de-camp
King of Prussia's aide-de-camp.initial_description: Prussian military representative at the imperial dinner; no individual lines.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_075: Prussian military aide present at dinner, exchanged glance with Ambassador about Nicholas's strategic vanity

German Ballet-master.name: German Ballet-master
German Ballet-master.initial_description: Ballet director summoned by Nicholas to deliver a diamond ring to a dancer

Ballerina.name: Ballerina
Ballerina.initial_description: Unnamed ballet dancer who attracted Nicholas; receives diamond ring from Emperor

Old Sergeant-Major.name: Old Sergeant-Major
Old Sergeant-Major.initial_description: Far-sighted veteran who identifies Shamil's green banner amid the Chechen horsemen; informs Butler

Little General.name: Little General
Little General.initial_description: Small general in thick black coat and tall cap riding an ambler; orders Butler's company to intercept Chechen horsemen

Marya Dmitrievna.name: Marya Dmitrievna
Marya Dmitrievna.initial_description: Major Petrov's common-law wife, formerly called Masha; handsome, fair-haired, very freckled, childless woman of thirty; nurses Petrov through his drinking
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_080: Handsome, fair-haired, freckled, childless woman of thirty; Major Petrov's faithful companion and caretaker
Chunk chunk_091: The major's wife at the fort; quarrelled with Khanefi but shows genuine sympathy and respect for Hadji Murad, actively follows family-rescue negotiations
Chunk chunk_110: Woman at the outpost (Butler's companion) who reacts with moral revulsion to the display of Hadji Murad's head, calling the officers cut-throats
Marya Dmitrievna.origin: Russian; living at the frontier fort as Major Petrov's common-law wife (formerly called Masha)
Marya Dmitrievna.social_status: Unmarried partner of a Russian army major; informal 'wife' at a Caucasus outpost; outside polite society but central to the daily life of the garrison
Marya Dmitrievna.want: To keep Major Petrov sober, alive, and decent — to hold together the small domestic world of the fort against drink, boredom, and brutality
Marya Dmitrievna.need: To recognize her own moral worth as separate from the man she serves, and to stop excusing the violence around her in the name of duty or affection
Marya Dmitrievna.fear: Being left utterly alone — losing Petrov to drink or transfer, and with him losing the only place where she is treated as someone's woman rather than a discarded girl
Marya Dmitrievna.contradiction: She nurses Russian soldiers and quarrels with the Chechen visitor Khanefi, yet feels deep, almost maternal sympathy for Hadji Murad and is the one voice at the outpost that names the officers 'cut-throats' when they parade his severed head

arc.starting_state: A frontier wife in everything but law: practical, devoted, scolding Petrov through his benders, treating the fort as her household and the war as background noise
arc.change: Through her contact with Hadji Murad and the family-rescue negotiations she begins to see the men around her — Russian and Chechen — as people rather than uniforms; her loyalty to Petrov starts to compete with a private moral judgment of the war
arc.end_state: Confronted with Hadji Murad's head displayed by drunken officers, she breaks character entirely and condemns them aloud, stepping for one moment out of her domestic role into open moral witness

voice_profile.voice_description: Marya Dmitrievna speaks in the warm, slightly coarse Russian of a woman who has lived among soldiers — direct, unsentimental, often scolding, but with sudden flashes of tenderness. Her sentences are short and concrete, full of household nouns and bodily verbs (eat, drink, sleep, wash, lie down), and she addresses everyone, from the major to a Tatar guest, as if they were a stubborn child she has decided to take care of. Under stress her voice does not rise into rhetoric; it goes flat and judgmental, and the freckled face goes red before the words come.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal, lower-middle Russian register, mid-19th century; basic literacy; no aristocratic French, no military jargon beyond what she has picked up at the fort; religious idioms used casually ("God forgive them", "Christ be with you")
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 6–10 words; mostly simple declaratives and imperatives; frequent rhetorical questions aimed at men she is trying to manage ("And what do you think will come of it?"); fragments when angry
verbal_tics: 'Batyushka' / 'little father' as a half-affectionate, half-reproachful address to Petrov and to Hadji Murad alike
starts sentences with 'Well, then —' before announcing a decision
'God see them' as a curse she allows herself instead of swearing
avoidances: Political abstractions ('empire', 'pacification', 'the cause')
French phrases or salon formulas
Coarse barracks-obscenity in front of guests, even when she clearly knows it
Self-pity spoken aloud — she will not say 'poor me' even when it is true
Religious sermonizing; she invokes God in single short phrases, never in speeches
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings arrive first in the body — a flush across the freckles, a hand on a hip, a slammed cup — and only afterwards in plain words. Tenderness comes out as practical care (food, blankets, scolding); grief and outrage come out as terse moral verdicts rather than tears.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and domestic in normal life; clipped and hard when she is angry; almost flat — toneless — at the moment of moral judgment over Hadji Murad's head

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Sit down, batyushka, eat while it's hot — the soup will not wait for the war.
example_lines.under_pressure: Drink your tea and be quiet. If the major sees you like this, there will be shouting, and I have had shouting enough for one week.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: So they are bringing his wife and the little ones out? Well, then — God be praised, there is one good thing in this miserable place at last.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Cut-throats, that's what you are. Drunk cut-throats. Take that thing away from my table — he was a man, and you are carrying him about like a goat's head at a fair.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: He looked at me, you know. Like I was a person. Not many of them do that, batyushka — not the Russians, and not your generals either.

subtext_rules.when_she_says_eat_or_drink_something: she means: I am still here, I still claim you, do not slip away from me into the bottle or into the war
subtext_rules.when_she_calls_a_man_batyushka: she means: I see that you are weaker than you pretend, and I will cover for it — but do not push me
subtext_rules.when_she_invokes_God_in_a_short_phrase: she means: I have run out of arguments and I refuse to keep arguing
subtext_rules.when_she_goes_silent_and_red_in_the_face: she means: a line has just been crossed and I will say so, even if it costs me
subtext_rules.when_she_speaks_kindly_about_Hadji_Murad: she means: I have started to doubt our side of this war, and I do not yet know what to do with that doubt

starts sentences with 'Well, then —' before…

voice_profile_compact.example_line: Sit down, batyushka, eat while it's hot — the soup will not wait for the war.

Major Petrov.quality: Loyal, exasperated, maternal; built on shared loneliness rather than romance
Major Petrov.power_dynamic: He has the rank and the legal standing; she has the daily authority — over the food, the drink, the household, and, quietly, over his dignity
Major Petrov.speech_register_shift: Drops to the most informal register, mixes scolding with endearments ('batyushka', 'you fool'); uses imperatives freely

Hadji Murad.type: Hostess to a guest who is also a political prisoner of sorts
Hadji Murad.quality: Respectful, curious, increasingly sympathetic; she treats him as a man before she treats him as an enemy
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: Formally she is the Russian woman of the house and he the surrendered chieftain, but morally she comes to feel he is the more upright of the two
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: More careful, slightly formal; fewer scolding tics; uses 'batyushka' with real respect rather than irony

Khanefi (Hadji Murad's murid).type: Reluctant hostess to a foreign retainer
Khanefi (Hadji Murad's murid).quality: Friction — cultural and domestic; they quarrel over the running of the household
Khanefi (Hadji Murad's murid).power_dynamic: She runs the house, he answers only to Hadji Murad; neither will fully yield
Khanefi (Hadji Murad's murid).speech_register_shift: Sharper, more clipped; orders rather than requests; no endearments

Butler (junior officer at the outpost).type: Companion / informal protector after Petrov's decline
Butler (junior officer at the outpost).quality: Affectionate but disillusioned; he is the witness to her final moral outburst
Butler (junior officer at the outpost).power_dynamic: She is older in experience of the frontier; he in rank — in practice they meet as equals in disenchantment
Butler (junior officer at the outpost).speech_register_shift: Plain, confiding, occasionally tender; she allows herself the rare 'you know' and the rare admission

Russian officers parading Hadji Murad's.quality: Open contempt; they are the men her loyalty was supposed to extend to and no longer can
Russian officers parading Hadji Murad's.power_dynamic: They have the trophy, the rank, and the drink; she has only her voice — and uses it
Russian officers parading Hadji Murad's.speech_register_shift: Flat, hard, no endearments, no religious softening; the only time in the story she calls Russian soldiers what she thinks they are

fusion_decision.rationale: Marya Dmitrievna belongs to the frontier-fort world of Major Petrov and Butler, while Marya Vasilevna is a Tiflis aristocrat in Vorontsov's household — they share only a first name and inhabit incompatible social worlds, dramatic functions, and registers, so fusing them would collapse the novel's deliberate contrast between salon and outpost.

example_lines.under_pressure: Drink your tea and be quiet. If the major sees you like this, there will be shouting, and I have had shouting enough for one week.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: So they are bringing his wife and the little ones out? Well, then — God be praised, there is one good thing in this miserable place at last.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Cut-throats, that's what you are. Drunk cut-throats. Take that thing away from my table — he was a man, and you are carrying him about like a goat's head at a fair.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: He looked at me, you know. Like I was a person. Not many of them do that, batyushka — not the Russians, and not your generals either.

Patimat.name: Patimat
Patimat.initial_description: Hadji Murad's old mother, held captive in Vedeno under Shamil's guard

Yusuf.name: Yusuf
Yusuf.initial_description: Hadji Murad's son, eighteen years old, imprisoned in a pit in Vedeno awaiting Shamil's judgment
Yusuf.origin: Avar Caucasus, son of Hadji Murat; raised in the mountain resistance
Yusuf.social_status: Son of a Naib; high-status hostage; held in a pit at Vedeno awaiting Shamil's judgment
Yusuf.want: To be freed from Shamil's pit and stand at his father's side as a warrior, not a hostage.
Yusuf.need: To accept that he is still a son before he is a soldier — that surviving for his mother and grandmother is not cowardice.
Yusuf.fear: That his father will choose honor over him and let him die in the pit; underneath, the fear that he himself is not yet man enough to deserve rescue.
Yusuf.contradiction: He boasts of Chechen loyalty and martyrdom yet flinches at every footstep above the pit; he resents his father's bargaining with the Russians while praying every hour that the bargain succeeds.

arc.starting_state: A proud, impatient eighteen-year-old, certain his father will come and certain he himself would die rather than break.
arc.change: Confinement, hunger, and the slow news of his father's flight to the Russians shake his certainties; he learns that his fate is a coin in a game between Shamil and the empire, and that his own bravery is not enough to save him.
arc.end_state: Mutilated and broken in body, returned only as a sign of Shamil's verdict — a son whose suffering arrives as the news that breaks his father.

voice_profile.voice_description: Yusuf speaks in short, hot bursts that try to sound older than he is, then collapse into the cadence of a boy when no one is looking. His Avar carries the formal courtesies of a Naib's household, but pressure strips them away into bare verbs and oaths. He uses the religious phrasing of his upbringing as armor — invocations of Allah, of his father's name, of the murids — and only rarely lets a private fear leak through in a single, plain sentence.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal-leaning, with formulaic religious and martial phrases learned by rote; not literate in Russian; the vocabulary of a young man taught by warriors and mullahs, not tutors.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 6–10 words. Short declaratives and questions; frequent fragments and oaths under stress. Complex sentences only when imitating his father's measured speech, and they tend to break midway.
verbal_tics: Begins many sentences with 'My father —'
Inshallah used as punctuation, not piety
Repeats the last word of a threat back at himself ('I am not afraid. Not afraid.')
Calls the guards 'dogs' under his breath, then louder when he is alone
avoidances: Never says he is frightened
Never speaks his mother's or grandmother's name aloud in front of strangers
Never admits doubt about his father's choices in earshot of an enemy
Never uses Russian words, even ones he knows
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Anger comes out as boasts and threats; grief comes out as silence and a turned head; tenderness comes out only as a half-finished sentence about his mother, cut off before it can be finished.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Alternating — quick, clipped bursts of bravado, then long pauses where the boy underneath listens.

example_lines.line: Bread again. Tell the guard the Naib's son does not eat what dogs leave.
Shamil can keep me here a year. My father will come. Inshallah, he will come.
If I hear his horse on the stones above, I will know it before any of you. I will know it.
He bargains with Russians for me? Tell him not to. Tell him I would rather rot.
Mother — if she asks — say I did not weep. Say that, even if it is not true.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_I_am_not_afraid: He is asking the listener to stay a little longer.
subtext_rules.when_he_curses_Shamil: He is testing whether the guard will report him — a small, daily proof that he still has agency.
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_his_father_in_the_thir: He is rehearsing the eulogy he fears he will have to give.
subtext_rules.when_he_refuses_food: He is trying to choose one thing in a place where nothing is his to choose.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_at_his_mother's_nam: He has just understood, for one second, that he may not see her again.

voice_profile_compact.voice_description: Yusuf speaks in short, hot bursts that try to sound older than he is, then collapse into the cadence of a boy when no one is looking.
voice_profile_compact.vocabulary_level: Informal-leaning, with formulaic religious and martial phrases learned by rote; not literate in…
voice_profile_compact.sentence_pattern: Average 6–10 words. Short declaratives and questions; frequent fragments and oaths under stress.…
verbal_tics: Begins many sentences with 'My father —'
Inshallah used as punctuation, not piety
avoidances: Never says he is frightened
Never speaks his mother's or grandmother's name…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Bread again. Tell the guard the Naib's son does not eat what dogs leave.

Hadji Murat.type: Father–son; the relational engine of HM's entire defection arc
Hadji Murat.quality: Worshipful, wounded, unfinished — Yusuf adores a father he has barely had time to be known by, and that incompleteness is the wound the story turns on.
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: Total asymmetry: HM is the actor in the world above; Yusuf is the stake in the pit below. Yusuf has only one power — to be loved enough to be saved.
Hadji Murat.speech_register_shift: With or about his father, Yusuf shifts into a slower, more formal Avar, borrowing his father's measured cadences; this is the only register in which his sentences reliably finish.

Shamil.type: Captor / sovereign judge; the man who holds his life in his hand
Shamil.quality: Fear masked as defiance; Yusuf knows Shamil is not a monster, which makes the verdict harder to bear.
Shamil.power_dynamic: Absolute — Shamil decides whether he lives, dies, or is mutilated as a sign.
Shamil.speech_register_shift: In Shamil's presence (or imagined presence) Yusuf's voice tightens into formal, pious phrasing; behind his back it collapses into curses and bravado.

Patimat (mother).type: Mother; the private self he protects from his public bravery
Patimat (mother).quality: Tender, almost unspoken; she is the name he will not say in front of guards.
Patimat (mother).power_dynamic: Emotional — she has no power to free him, but her grief is the one judgment he cannot bear.
Patimat (mother).speech_register_shift: Softer, simpler diction; sentences shorten and trail off; the only register in which he comes close to admitting fear.

Shamil's guards / murids in Vedeno.type: Captors of his own people; ambivalent peers
Shamil's guards / murids in Vedeno.quality: Contemptuous on the surface, kin underneath — they are Chechens and Avars like him, doing a job he might one day have done.
Shamil's guards / murids in Vedeno.power_dynamic: Day-to-day power over food, light, movement; long-term they are interchangeable instruments of Shamil's will.
Shamil's guards / murids in Vedeno.speech_register_shift: Sharper, more dialectal, full of insults and proverbs; uses shared idiom to remind them — and himself — that he is one of them, not a Russian's son.

The Russians (as concept).type: Distant power his father has run to; both possible saviors and historic enemies
The Russians (as concept).quality: Suspicion braided with hope; he hates needing them.
The Russians (as concept).power_dynamic: Abstract — he never meets them on the page, but his fate is being negotiated in their language.
The Russians (as concept).speech_register_shift: Refuses Russian vocabulary entirely; speaks of them in the third person plural, never by name, as if naming them would make the bargain real.

fusion_decision.rationale: Yusuf is the emotional fulcrum of Hadji Murat's defection — his captivity at Vedeno is HM's primary motive for negotiating with the Russians and cannot be collapsed into any other figure without destroying the central want.

example_lines.line: Bread again. Tell the guard the Naib's son does not eat what dogs leave.
Shamil can keep me here a year. My father will come. Inshallah, he will come.
If I hear his horse on the stones above, I will know it before any of you. I will know it.
He bargains with Russians for me? Tell him not to. Tell him I would rather rot.
Mother — if she asks — say I did not weep. Say that, even if it is not true.

Aminal.name: Aminal
Aminal.initial_description: Shamil's favorite wife, eighteen years old, black-eyed and quick-footed, waiting behind the seraglio fence
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_091: One of Shamil's younger wives; playfully defiant, withholds herself from Shamil to punish his gift of silk to a rival wife Zeidat

Jemal Eddin.name: Jemal Eddin
Jemal Eddin.initial_description: Shamil's father-in-law and teacher; tall grey-haired old man with snow-white beard and rosy red face; serves as chief advisor and council facilitator
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_089: Shamil's councilor who drafts the proclamation urging Chechens not to submit to the Russians; functions as Shamil's ideological secretary.

Zeidat.name: Zeidat
Zeidat.initial_description: Shamil's eldest wife; dark, sharp-nosed, disagreeable-looking woman whom Shamil does not love; serves his dinner
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_091: One of Shamil's wives; rival to Aminal, received silk gift that provoked domestic tension

Petrovsky.name: Petrovsky
Petrovsky.initial_description: A lively Russian officer meeting Hadji Murad for the first time at the farewell gathering
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_096: Russian officer at the major's house, skeptical of Hadji Murad, calls him a 'terrible rogue'

Arslan Khan.name: Arslan Khan
Arslan Khan.initial_description: Kumukh prince with blood-feud against Hadji Murad; rides up and draws pistol at moment of departure
Arslan Khan.age: adult, mature warrior age (text-implied, not specified)
Arslan Khan.social_status: prince of Kumukh; member of native Caucasian aristocracy bound by blood-feud honor codes
Arslan Khan.want: to kill Hadji Murad and discharge the unpaid blood-debt his family carries
Arslan Khan.need: to be released from a feud he did not choose but inherited — a peace he cannot articulate
Arslan Khan.fear: to die without having taken the life owed to his line; to be remembered as the Khan who let the debt rot
Arslan Khan.contradiction: carries himself as a prince of law and lineage, yet his only legitimate act in the story is an ambush at the moment of a man's flight
Arslan Khan.justification: Hadji Murad spilled the blood of my house — until that blood is answered, I am no Khan, only a debtor with a name.

arc.starting_state: absent presence — a feud-name spoken about Hadji Murad before he appears on screen
arc.change: rides into the action at HM's moment of departure, pistol drawn; the inherited debt becomes flesh
arc.end_state: executor (or witness) of HM's killing — the feud closed, but no triumph, only a cold settling

voice_profile.voice_description: Arslan Khan speaks in short, weighted sentences that carry the gravity of family law rather than personal anger. He rarely raises his voice; the threat is in the pause, not the volume. His Russian is heavily accented and stiff, his Avar/Kumyk is curt and formal — the tongue of a man who treats words as oaths.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal in his own tongue, broken and clipped in Russian; education through clan tradition, not letters; 19th-century Caucasian aristocratic register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: short declarative statements; questions only when issuing accusation; fragments under pressure; almost never compound sentences
verbal_tics: invokes 'blood' (qan / krov') as a noun-of-record
names Hadji Murad in full each time, never shortened
ends statements with a silent beat instead of a softening clause
avoidances: would never plead, bargain, or explain himself
would never use endearments or familiar diminutives
would never speak Hadji Murad's name with respect or pity
would never reference imperial Russian rank as legitimate authority over his feud
voice_profile.emotional_expression: feeling is converted into stillness; rage flattens his voice rather than sharpening it; grief is signaled by the act of naming the dead, not by mourning words
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, with long silences; each line lands like a stone set on a wall

example_lines.neutral: Hadji Murad rode this road. We ride it too.
example_lines.under_pressure: The horses can wait. The debt cannot.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Today the name of my father is no longer hungry.
example_lines.angry_hurt: He buried my brother in the dirt. I will bury him in the dirt.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I did not choose this blood. It was given to me when I was a boy. I carry it because no one else will.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'the_debt': he means: I have no self outside this killing
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'Hadji_Murad'_in_full: he is reminding himself the man is still real, still alive, still owed
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_mid-exchange: the decision to shoot has already been made internally
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_his_father_or_brother: he is justifying the next bullet, not grieving
