avoidances: would never plead, bargain, or explain himself
would never use endearments or familiar…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Hadji Murad rode this road. We ride it too.

Hadji Murad.quality: lethal, inherited, impersonal hatred dressed as duty
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: subordinate while HM lives free; dominant the moment HM is unhorsed and exposed
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: addresses HM by full name only, never directly in dialogue if avoidable; in confrontation, drops to single-clause threat-register

Karganov (Russian pursuit officer).quality: cold cooperation; mutual use without trust
Karganov (Russian pursuit officer).power_dynamic: Karganov holds imperial authority; Arslan Khan holds the moral claim and the local knowledge
Karganov (Russian pursuit officer).speech_register_shift: switches to broken, formal Russian; clipped military nouns; refuses any familiar register

His own clan / dead kin.speech_register_shift: softens only when naming them; uses possessives ('my father', 'my brother') as the warmest words he speaks in the script

fusion_decision.rationale: Arslan Khan's blood-feud claim is morally and structurally distinct from Hadji Aga (rival warlord politics) and Karganov (imperial Russian pursuit) — collapsing them would erase Tolstoi's three-front antagonism (clan honor, native rivalry, empire) which is the thematic spine of HM's death.

example_lines.neutral: Hadji Murad rode this road. We ride it too.
example_lines.under_pressure: The horses can wait. The debt cannot.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Today the name of my father is no longer hungry.
example_lines.angry_hurt: He buried my brother in the dirt. I will bury him in the dirt.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: I did not choose this blood. It was given to me when I was a boy. I carry it because no one else will.

Prince Baryatinsky.name: Prince Baryatinsky
Prince Baryatinsky.initial_description: New commander of the left flank, old friend of the Viceroy, commands the Kabarda Regiment, carries out the Tsar's campaign orders; involved in an affair with Marya Vasilevna
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_097: Senior Russian officer who replaced Kozlovsky as commander of the left flank; known for affair with Marya Vasilevna
Prince Baryatinsky.origin: Russian high aristocracy, Petersburg court circle
Prince Baryatinsky.social_status: Prince, senior Russian general, newly appointed commander of the left flank, old friend of Viceroy Vorontsov
Prince Baryatinsky.want: To execute the Tsar's Caucasus campaign with distinction and secure his standing as the Empire's preferred general
Prince Baryatinsky.need: To recognize that career advancement bought through colonial subjugation is a hollow form of honor
Prince Baryatinsky.fear: Falling out of imperial favor — being seen as a courtier rather than a soldier, or worse, as a soldier who failed
Prince Baryatinsky.contradiction: Presents himself as a man of duty and honor, yet conducts an open affair with Marya Vasilevna — the wife within the same Russian command circle whose loyalty he depends on
Prince Baryatinsky.justification: The Empire has entrusted me with its will in this region; my orders come from the Tsar, my friendships from the Viceroy, and my conscience answers to both — what looks like ambition from below is service from where I stand.

arc.starting_state: Arrives as the new commander of the left flank, replacing Kozlovsky, confident in his mandate and his personal connection to Vorontsov
arc.change: Inherits the Hadji Murat problem — must balance the Tsar's demand for results, Vorontsov's political caution, and the personal entanglement with Marya Vasilevna that quietly undermines his authority
arc.end_state: Carries out the campaign as ordered; his trajectory continues upward in imperial terms while the human cost (HM's family, HM himself) remains invisible to him

voice_profile.voice_description: Baryatinsky speaks the polished French-inflected Russian of the Petersburg salons, but he keeps his sentences short and operational when on duty. His tone shifts between the languid drawl of the courtier and the clipped certainty of a commanding officer, often within a single conversation. He never raises his voice; rank does the work for him.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Highly formal, French loanwords natural in private speech, military precision in command contexts; educated to the highest aristocratic standard, mid-19th century
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium length, declarative dominant, occasional rhetorical questions used as soft commands; rarely fragmentary
verbal_tics: Mon cher when addressing equals or favored subordinates
Opens orders with 'You will...' rather than 'I want...'
Closes difficult conversations with 'That is all'
avoidances: Never pleads, never apologizes outright
Never names the affair with Marya Vasilevna in public speech
Never expresses doubt about the Tsar's orders to subordinates
Never uses Caucasian or Tatar terms — they belong to the people he commands against
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is filtered through irony, understatement, or strategic silence; affection appears only in lowered voice and proximity, anger as a colder politeness
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured; alternates between languid social cadence and crisp military tempo depending on context

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Mon cher, the dispatches from Tiflis can wait until after dinner — they always say the same thing.
example_lines.under_pressure: The Tsar wants the left flank quiet by spring. We will give him spring.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: If Hadji Murat truly comes over to us, the whole eastern line shifts in a single week — imagine it.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You will explain to me, Colonel, how a single Avar managed to ride out of a town garrisoned by my men. Slowly. That is all.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Marya, I do not know any longer where the Empire ends and my own name begins.

subtext_rules: When he says 'as the Tsar wishes' he means 'as I have already decided'
When he says 'an interesting man' about a Caucasian he means 'a useful instrument'
When he is silent at a dinner table he is not absent — he is registering who said what
When he praises a subordinate's loyalty, he is testing it

voice_profile_compact.subtext_rule_short: When he says 'as the Tsar wishes' he means 'as I have already decided'
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Mon cher, the dispatches from Tiflis can wait until after dinner — they always say the same thing.

Vorontsov.type: Old friend, political patron and military superior
Vorontsov.quality: Warm in private, deferential in form, mutually instrumentalizing in substance
Vorontsov.power_dynamic: Vorontsov outranks him as Viceroy, but Baryatinsky carries the Tsar's direct mandate on the left flank — a quiet equilibrium
Vorontsov.speech_register_shift: French phrases, first-name intimacy, longer sentences with rhetorical flourish

Marya Vasilevna.quality: Genuine attraction wrapped in social danger; never named publicly
Marya Vasilevna.power_dynamic: Asymmetric — his rank protects him, her reputation does not
Marya Vasilevna.speech_register_shift: Lowered voice, dropped formality, single-word sentences carrying weight

Hadji Murat.type: Strategic asset / colonial subject (no direct intimacy)
Hadji Murat.quality: Distant, instrumental — HM is a card to be played, not a man to be known
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: Total in Baryatinsky's framing; he never grasps that HM has his own logic
Hadji Murat.speech_register_shift: Speaks about HM, almost never to him; uses third person and military designators

Tsar Nicholas I.speech_register_shift: Formal third-person reference ('His Majesty'), no irony, no qualification

Russian junior officers (Kozlovsky's for.speech_register_shift: Clipped imperatives, no French, no warmth

fusion_decision.rationale: Baryatinsky appears in only 3 mentions, enters at chunk_096 with no arc of his own, and serves the same dramatic function as Vorontsov (Russian imperial commander whose orders shape HM's fate) — his command authority over the left flank and his Petersburg-court entanglements can be absorbed into Vorontsov without losing dramatic information, consistent with Seger's fusion principle (collapse one figure, transfer one gesture/line) for novel-to-feature compression.

example_lines.under_pressure: The Tsar wants the left flank quiet by spring. We will give him spring.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: If Hadji Murat truly comes over to us, the whole eastern line shifts in a single week — imagine it.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You will explain to me, Colonel, how a single Avar managed to ride out of a town garrisoned by my men. Slowly. That is all.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Marya, I do not know any longer where the Empire ends and my own name begins.

Councillor Kirillov.name: Councillor Kirillov
Councillor Kirillov.initial_description: Government official sent from Tiflis by Vorontsov to summon Hadji Murad for a meeting with General Argutinski; he also delivers overdue payment
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_102: Fat, bald, civilian official sent from Tiflis to deliver Vorontsov's message and Hadji Murad's allowance; unarmed bureaucrat contemptuous of him

Tavlinian Spy.name: Tavlinian Spy
Tavlinian Spy.initial_description: Fleshy, swarthy hooded spy from the hills bringing news about the failed rescue attempt on HM's family

Old Spy.name: Old Spy
Old Spy.initial_description: Thin old man, second hooded spy from the hills delivering the same bad news

Tavlinian Messenger (fleshy).name: Tavlinian Messenger (fleshy)
Tavlinian Messenger (fleshy).initial_description: Fleshy, swarthy Tavlinian man; one of two messengers bearing bad news from the mountains

Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev.name: Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev
Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev.initial_description: Officer who formerly served with Major Petrov, arrives on horseback with escort bearing news
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_109: Russian officer, formerly served under the major; arrives at night bearing Hadji Murad's severed head in a sack, delivers it matter-of-factly, announces HM 'died like a hero'

Chikhirev.name: Chikhirev
Chikhirev.initial_description: Don Cossack in Kamenev's escort; carries the sack containing Hadji Murad's head

Kamenev.name: Kamenev
Kamenev.initial_description: Russian officer tasked with carrying and displaying Hadji Murad's severed head to forts and aouls as a trophy of war

Nazarov.name: Nazarov
Nazarov.initial_description: Non-commissioned Cossack officer, Cross of St. George recipient; young, brown-haired Old Believer who supports widowed mother and siblings; assigned to escort Hadji Murad

Ferapontov.name: Ferapontov
Ferapontov.initial_description: Tall, thin Cossack; known thief and plunderer; previously sold gunpowder to Gamzalo

Ignatov.name: Ignatov
Ignatov.initial_description: Sturdy, boastful Cossack peasant, near end of service

Petrakov.name: Petrakov
Petrakov.initial_description: Young fair-haired Cossack, his mother's only son; amiable and jolly

Karganov.name: Karganov
Karganov.initial_description: Military commander of the district who leads the militia and Cossack force that surrounds Hadji Murad in the thicket
Karganov.origin: Russian Imperial Army officer, district military commander in the Caucasus frontier
Karganov.social_status: Russian military commander of the district; mid-rank field officer with authority over local militia and Cossack detachments
Karganov.want: To close the encirclement around Hadji Murat cleanly and deliver a decisive military result that earns him recognition from Tiflis and Vorontsov.
Karganov.need: To recognize that the man he hunts is not an abstraction of 'the enemy' but a human equal — a recognition the role of district commander structurally forbids him from reaching.
Karganov.fear: Failure of command in the eyes of his superiors; that Hadji Murat slips the net and Karganov is named in the dispatch as the officer who lost him.
Karganov.contradiction: Calls for a parley and offers the form of mercy (surrender), yet has already arranged the militia and Cossacks in a tightening ring that makes refusal the only honourable answer — he performs negotiation while ensuring there is nothing to negotiate.
Karganov.justification: A sworn Russian officer with a fugitive insurgent commander cornered on his district's ground has one duty — to close the ring and end it; any other choice betrays the men under his command and the Tsar he serves.

arc.starting_state: District commander functioning as the regional instrument of Russian imperial authority — confident, procedural, untouched by doubt.
arc.change: When the encirclement tightens and Hadji Murat refuses surrender, Karganov commits to the assault; the act of giving the order brings the abstraction of 'pursuit' into the concrete reality of killing a renowned warrior.
arc.end_state: Operationally successful (Hadji Murat is killed under his command) but morally inert — he has done his duty and felt nothing he is willing to name.

voice_profile.voice_description: Karganov speaks in the clipped, command-grade register of a Russian field officer trained to be heard over wind and rifle-fire. His sentences are short, declarative, and structured around verbs of order ('move', 'hold', 'send', 'close'); courtesy appears only as a function of rank, never as warmth. When he addresses the enemy, his voice carries the cold civility of a man offering terms he knows will be refused.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, military-administrative, mid-19th-century Russian officer's idiom rendered in English; educated but not literary
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 8–14 words. Heavy on imperatives and short declaratives. Few subordinate clauses. Questions rare and always tactical, never reflective.
verbal_tics: addresses subordinates by rank, not name ('Sotnik', 'Cornet')
prefaces orders with 'You will —'
ends parley statements with a fixed formula: 'Those are the terms.'
refers to Hadji Murat as 'the fugitive' or 'the abrek', almost never by name
avoidances: first-person emotional disclosure ('I feel', 'I fear')
religious or fatalistic phrasing — leaves God to chaplains
praise of the enemy's courage in front of subordinates
any admission of uncertainty in tactical matters
Caucasian languages or local idioms — speaks through interpreters on principle
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion surfaces as procedural intensification: when angry, he shortens his sentences and lowers his voice; when satisfied, he simply nods and turns to the next order. Feeling is displaced into command grammar.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, with sudden compressions under pressure — never hectic

example_lines.neutral: Post the Cossacks along the ravine. Two pickets at the ford. Report at the half-hour.
example_lines.under_pressure: He will not slip this thicket. Tighten the line. No one fires until I give the word.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: The ring is closed, gentlemen. By evening Tiflis will have its dispatch.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You let the militia break formation, Sotnik. That mistake belongs to you. Do not repeat it.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: He fought well. Note that in the report — accurately. The Tsar should know what kind of man we ended today.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_those_are_the_terms: the negotiation is finished and the assault is already organised
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_the_fugitive_obliquely: he is uneasy with the order he has given but will not retract it
subtext_rules.when_he_addresses_a_subordinate_by_rank_: he is displeased and is putting distance between them
subtext_rules.when_he_calls_for_a_chaplain_or_a_clean_: he wants the moral residue of the action handed off to someone else

religious or fatalistic phrasing — leaves God to…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Post the Cossacks along the ravine. Two pickets at the ford. Report at the half-hour.

Hadji Murat.quality: impersonal at the level of conviction, charged with reluctant respect at the level of soldiering
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: Karganov holds operational power (numbers, terrain, supply); Hadji Murat holds moral and martial stature — the inversion the scene exploits
Hadji Murat.speech_register_shift: shifts to the formal parley register — slower cadence, fixed formulas, no familiarity; refuses to dignify HM with his name in front of the troops

Cossack and militia subordinates.quality: correct, distant, demanding; loyalty rewarded with brevity, error punished with cold rebuke
Cossack and militia subordinates.speech_register_shift: rank-only address, imperative-heavy, no first names, no jokes

Vorontsov / Tiflis high command (offstag.quality: deferential without warmth; sees them as the audience for his dispatches
Vorontsov / Tiflis high command (offstag.power_dynamic: Karganov is an instrument of their will and knows it
Vorontsov / Tiflis high command (offstag.speech_register_shift: formal written register; his speech tightens into report-language whenever they are invoked

Hadji Aga (Russian-aligned Avar commande.quality: uses Hadji Aga as cultural translator and front-line voice for the surrender call; respects his usefulness, distrusts his motives
Hadji Aga (Russian-aligned Avar commande.power_dynamic: Karganov outranks but depends on him for legitimacy with the locals
Hadji Aga (Russian-aligned Avar commande.speech_register_shift: shorter, more transactional; instructions framed as requests but unmistakably orders

fusion_decision.rationale: Karganov is a single-function pursuit-antagonist (reach=1, three mentions, no arc) whose only dramatic beats — leading the encirclement and calling for surrender — are structurally duplicated by Hadji Aga, who is the dramatically richer version because his parley is freighted with kunak betrayal; collapsing Karganov's parley line and command of the ring into Hadji Aga preserves every plot beat while removing one Russian-uniformed functionary the audience would not retain (CF-01, SC-02).

example_lines.neutral: Post the Cossacks along the ravine. Two pickets at the ford. Report at the half-hour.
example_lines.under_pressure: He will not slip this thicket. Tighten the line. No one fires until I give the word.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: The ring is closed, gentlemen. By evening Tiflis will have its dispatch.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You let the militia break formation, Sotnik. That mistake belongs to you. Do not repeat it.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: He fought well. Note that in the report — accurately. The Tsar should know what kind of man we ended today.

Hadji Aga.name: Hadji Aga
Hadji Aga.initial_description: Hadji Aga of Mekhtuli — once Hadji Murad's kunak, now Russian-aligned; arrives with ~200 followers and leads the assault
Hadji Aga.age: approx. 40s (battle-hardened tribal commander; not specified in text)
Hadji Aga.social_status: Tribal chieftain of Mekhtuli, formerly Hadji Murad's kunak (sworn brother-friend), now Russian-aligned auxiliary commander leading ~200 followers
Hadji Aga.want: To deliver Hadji Murad's head to the Russians and secure his own family's standing under the new imperial order — a public, decisive proof of allegiance
Hadji Aga.need: To silence the shame of betraying a kunak by pretending the betrayal was always righteous; he needs to believe Hadji Murad was the traitor first
Hadji Aga.fear: Being seen — by his own people, by his sons, by God — as the man who killed his brother-friend for Russian silver
Hadji Aga.contradiction: Calls Hadji Murad an apostate and traitor while himself fighting under the banner of the very empire that conquered his homeland; preaches loyalty while embodying its inversion
Hadji Aga.justification: Hadji Murad abandoned Shamil, abandoned his own kunak-oath when he ran to the Russians — he broke the bond first, so I owe him only the blade he taught me to carry.

arc.starting_state: Off-screen presence — a name from Hadji Murad's past; the lost kunak, the friend who chose the Russian side. Spoken of with bitter regret by HM in Act 1/2.
arc.change: Re-enters the story in the final movement as the parley-voice at the marsh, calling on Hadji Murad to surrender. When refused, leads the assault personally — every shouted command is a former intimacy weaponized.
arc.end_state: Stands over the corpse, takes the head with his own dagger. The act seals him as the empire's instrument. No remorse shown on screen — but he does not look at the face.
arc.arc_type: Fall (off-screen completed before story; on-screen he executes the final act of his own moral collapse)

voice_profile.voice_description: Hadji Aga speaks in the clipped, formal Avar manner of a tribal commander — short imperative sentences, religious invocations used as punctuation, never as prayer. His voice is harder than it should be, as if he is always answering an accusation no one has made aloud. He uses Hadji Murad's name often, and each time it costs him something he refuses to show.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal tribal-Islamic register; mid-19th century Caucasian; limited Russian loan-words (only military terms — 'parley', 'surrender', 'cordon'); no abstract or philosophical vocabulary
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short, declarative, imperative-heavy. Average 6-9 words. High ratio of statements and commands; rare questions, and only rhetorical ones. Fragments under pressure.
verbal_tics: 'By Allah' — used as a verbal seal on declarations, not as devotion
Begins commands with 'Listen —' (Slushai / Kunak-form imperative)
Repeats the addressed person's name at the start of an accusation: 'Hadji Murad. You know what you did.'
Touches dagger hilt before delivering an order (action beat)
avoidances: Never says the word 'kunak' aloud after the betrayal — the word is forbidden to his own mouth
Never speaks of his own sons during the assault — they are kept out of his battle-voice
Never quotes the Quran in mercy-context, only in judgment-context
Never uses Russian honorifics for his Russian commanders — he names them flatly, refusing the courtesy
Never apologizes; never explains himself to a subordinate
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion leaks through omission and through the body — the touched dagger, the refusal to look, the half-second pause before naming Hadji Murad. Verbally he converts every feeling into accusation or command. Grief becomes anger; shame becomes righteousness; fear becomes haste.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured-to-clipped under control; becomes hectic and overlapping only at the moment of the assault, where commands trip over each other

example_lines.neutral_everyday: The horses are tired. Water them at the far stream — not the marsh.
example_lines.under_pressure: Hadji Murad. Come out. The Russians offer the road back. Refuse, and I cannot hold them.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: By Allah, the Tsar will hear our names tonight. My sons will eat at a clean table.
example_lines.angry_hurt: You ran. You ran to them first. Do not speak to me of brothers — you buried that word.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: (quiet, almost to himself) I taught him this dagger-grip. He taught me the other.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_traitor: He means: do not look at me, look at him.
subtext_rules.when_he_invokes_Allah: He means: I need a witness who cannot contradict me.
subtext_rules.when_he_gives_an_order_quickly: He means: if I stop talking, I will have to think.
subtext_rules.when_he_names_Hadji_Murad: He means: I have not forgotten what we were — and I cannot afford to remember.

Hadji Murad.quality: Severed bond carried as a wound he refuses to name; intimate knowledge of HM's tactics, body, dagger-style — used now as weapon
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: On the battlefield: numerical superiority, Russian backing, parley-position. Morally: subordinate forever — HM is the one who chose, and HM is the one who dies standing.
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: Drops formal address ('Hadji Murad') to bare name in the assault; never uses the kunak-word; addresses HM directly only across distance, never within reach

Russian Officers (Vorontsov-side / cordo.quality: Functional, transactional, mutually distrustful; he is their instrument and knows it
Russian Officers (Vorontsov-side / cordo.power_dynamic: Subordinate in rank, indispensable in execution — the one man who can identify HM and lead the local levies
Russian Officers (Vorontsov-side / cordo.speech_register_shift: Strips honorifics, uses flat military Russian ('Polkovnik' at most, never 'Vashe Vysokoblagorodie'); shorter sentences, no Allah-invocations

His own ~200 followers.quality: Authority by lineage and reputation; followers obey but do not love
His own ~200 followers.power_dynamic: Absolute on the field, brittle off it — he must keep delivering victories or the levy melts
His own ~200 followers.speech_register_shift: Imperative-only, no first-person plural; commands by name when possible to bind individual loyalty

Karganov (absorbed via fusion — see fusi.type: Replaced — Hadji Aga inherits the parley-voice beat

Akhmet Khan (absorbed via fusion — see f.type: Replaced — Hadji Aga inherits the 'Caucasian-ally-of-Russia' antagonist function
Akhmet Khan (absorbed via fusion — see f.quality: N/A — function consolidated; any informant/lineage detail folded into Hadji Aga's backstory or a single dagger-line

fusion_decision.rationale: Hadji Aga is the dramatically richest variant of the 'Caucasian-native-aligned-with-Russia / parley-then-kill' function — kunak-betrayal gives him moral weight Akhmet Khan and Karganov lack; per CF-01 (<5 scenes, identical antagonist function) and SC-02, Akhmet Khan's informant/lineage beat and Karganov's parley beat collapse cleanly into Hadji Aga without losing thematic content.

Butler.origin: Russian gentry / former Guards officer (recently transferred to a line regiment in the Caucasus)
Butler.want: to feel the clean, intoxicating thrill of soldiering — the buoyant joy of risk, comradeship, and the romance of the Caucasus campaign — and to keep his uniform unsoiled by his private weaknesses
Butler.need: to recognise that the imperial idyll he has chased is built on burning aouls and vanished children, and that his gambling and drinking are not lapses from his duty but its honest reflection
Butler.fear: of being seen as a failure — exposed as a debtor, a drunk, a man unworthy of the Guards he left behind, and ultimately as a coward when the romantic surface of war collapses into its real face
Butler.contradiction: preaches and inwardly clings to officer's honour while sliding into gambling debts and drink; admires Hadji Murad's wholeness while serving the empire that hunts him; respects Marya Dmitrievna's marriage while half-wishing she wanted him

arc.act1_starting_state: tall, handsome, freshly transferred from the Guards, riding into his first raid filled with 'the buoyant sense of the joy of living, the danger of death' — war as a poem he is finally allowed to recite
arc.act2_change: billeted with Petrov and Marya Dmitrievna, he sees the aoul burned, the corn destroyed, the dead child; he meets Hadji Murad and feels, beside that quiet wholeness, the smallness of his own life; debts mount, vodka helps, the poem starts to rhyme with something uglier
arc.act3_end_state: still in uniform, still nominally the gallant officer, but hollowed out — a witness who survives Hadji Murad's death and carries, without fully admitting it, the knowledge that the empire he serves is the thistle-crusher of Tolstoi's frame

voice_profile.voice_description: Butler speaks the polished, lightly French-laced Russian of a Petersburg Guards officer — measured cadence, complete sentences, a reflexive courtesy that survives even when he is drunk. Beneath the parade-ground manners runs a quieter, more uncertain voice that asks questions of itself in the second person, half-ironic, half-ashamed. With Hadji Murad he becomes shy and almost boyish; with comrades he is bright, anecdotal, performatively cheerful.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, educated, mid-19th-century Russian gentry; occasional French tags ('mon cher', 'c'est la guerre'), military idiom of the line regiments, a few Tartar greetings learned on campaign ('Saubul')
voice_profile.sentence_structure: medium-length, grammatically complete sentences in public; clipped fragments under fire or when ashamed; tendency toward statements rather than questions, except in the inner monologue, which is almost entirely interrogative
verbal_tics: 'It's beginning' / 'Here we go' as a half-smiling threshold-line before danger
'my dear fellow' / 'mon cher' to other officers
small self-deprecating laugh inserted before admitting anything personal
repeats a Tartar or Chechen word he has just learned, as if tasting it
avoidances: never names his debts aloud
never speaks ill of the regiment, the Tsar, or the campaign in company
never uses coarse barracks obscenity in front of women or superiors
never admits fear directly — only dresses it as enthusiasm or irony
never confesses his attraction to Marya Dmitrievna
voice_profile.emotional_expression: expresses joy through bright, almost theatrical cheerfulness and a quickened step; expresses shame through silence, a tightened jaw, and an extra glass; expresses awe (around Hadji Murad) through formality and reduced speech; expresses grief obliquely, by changing the subject
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: alternating — buoyant and quick in company and under first fire, measured and sober when addressing Hadji Murad, slow and thickened when drunk or remembering a debt

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Good morning, Petrov. I'll take a turn before breakfast — the sun on the hills is too fine to waste indoors.
example_lines.under_pressure: It's beginning. Steady the line — and for God's sake tell the cadet to stop singing, this isn't a name-day.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Did you see them ride in? Banners, horses, the whole forest moving — my dear fellow, this is the life one reads about and never believes in.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Don't lecture me about the cards, Petrov. I know the figure to the kopeck. I carry it about with me like a second uniform.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: When he gave me his hand — only two fingers, with the whip hanging from them — I felt, I don't know how to say it, that I had been playing at being a soldier all my life.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'splendid_morning': he means he has not yet faced the day's reckoning — the bottle, the IOUs, or the burnt aoul
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_the_regiment: he is reassuring himself that leaving the Guards was not a mistake
subtext_rules.when_he_is_excessively_courteous_to_mary: he is suppressing desire and protecting his friendship with Petrov
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_around_hadji_murad: he is registering, without language, that this man is more whole than any Russian he has ever met
subtext_rules.when_he_jokes_about_money: he is closer to ruin than anyone at the table suspects

Hadji Murad.type: host / fascinated witness — Russian officer to Avar naib
Hadji Murad.quality: respectful, slightly awed, quietly envious of HM's wholeness; the relationship is the moral mirror of the screenplay
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: officially Butler is the host and HM the guest-prisoner; emotionally HM is the senior man, and Butler knows it
Hadji Murad.register_shift: drops the bright Petersburg manner; sentences shorten, become more formal and more sincere; uses learned Tartar greetings

Petrov.type: billet-comrade and friend — fellow line officer, married to Marya Dmitrievna
Petrov.quality: warm, loyal, faintly guilty (because of Marya Dmitrievna); the relationship anchors Butler's restraint
Petrov.power_dynamic: rough equality of rank; Petrov is the steadier, plainer man, Butler the more polished
Petrov.register_shift: easy, fraternal, full of 'my dear fellow' and shared silences; obscenity still avoided

Marya Dmitrievna.type: comrade's mistress / unspoken object of attraction
Marya Dmitrievna.quality: tender, courteous, deliberately fraternal; Butler maintains 'a simple, respectful tone' precisely because he feels otherwise
Marya Dmitrievna.power_dynamic: she has the moral authority of the household; Butler defers
Marya Dmitrievna.register_shift: exaggerated courtesy, French politesse, careful eye-contact — the manners covering desire

the Major (Petrov's commander) / senior.type: subordinate / junior officer to garrison superior
the Major (Petrov's commander) / senior.quality: outwardly correct, inwardly contemptuous of their drunkenness and brutality, though he shares both
the Major (Petrov's commander) / senior.power_dynamic: clearly subordinate; obeys, salutes, drinks when ordered
the Major (Petrov's commander) / senior.register_shift: stiffer, more formulaic, regimental phrases, no irony allowed to surface

fellow junior officers / cadets.quality: buoyant, anecdotal, performatively cheerful — the public face of the campaign
fellow junior officers / cadets.power_dynamic: rough equality; Butler is slightly senior in style by virtue of his Guards background
fellow junior officers / cadets.register_shift: bright, quick, full of campaign slang and song-quotations

the Chechens / villagers of the burned a.quality: almost no direct speech to them; the relationship is one of guilty witness rather than dialogue
the Chechens / villagers of the burned a.register_shift: silence, or curt orders relayed through NCOs; the brightness drains from his voice

fusion_decision.rationale: Butler is the canonical Russian-officer POV of Section 22 (24 mentions, sustained arc); Freze and Petrovsky are sub-3-scene functional duplicates (junior officer / skeptical commentator) and can be collapsed into Butler under CF-01, while Petrov is REJECTED from fusion because his independent dramatic function as the cuckolded comrade-host is load-bearing for Butler's restraint subplot.

Shamil.social_status: Imam of Dagestan and Chechnya — supreme religious-political-military leader of the Caucasian Imamate; sacred authority over murids and naibs
Shamil.want: To crush the Russian invasion, hold the Imamate together, and make Hadji Murad either return in submission or die — preserving Shamil's authority as God's instrument in the Caucasus.
Shamil.need: To be feared and obeyed without doubt. Beneath the sacred mask: the need to know that his thirty years of war, the dead sons, the dead murids, the burned auls, were not vanity but God's will.
Shamil.fear: That a single defection — Hadji Murad's — exposes the Imamate as political theater rather than divine order, and that his own children and naibs will read weakness in mercy.
Shamil.contradiction: He claims to act only as Allah's instrument, yet calculates every gesture (which prisoner to spare, which son to ransom, which letter to send) with the cold patience of a statesman. Sacred speech, profane arithmetic.
Shamil.justification: Hadji Murad was my best warrior and now he runs to the infidel — he dies or he returns, no third path is possible, for if I show mercy to the deserter the mountain itself will follow him.

arc.act_1_starting_state: Imam at the height of his sacred authority — the mountain warriors' unquestioned spiritual sovereign. Holds Hadji Murad's family hostage in Vedeno as leverage and as warning to all naibs.
arc.act_2_change: Hadji Murad's defection forces Shamil from invisible authority into reactive maneuvering: he must be SEEN to act — public threats against HM's family, ostentatious piety, the parading of his power before envoys. The off-stage god becomes a calculating man, glimpsed in his harem, his counting of treasure, his weariness.
arc.act_3_end_state: Has reasserted control through HM's death (the Russians do the work for him) but the cost is registered in his face: the defection happened, the question was asked, and his sacred-political fusion has been audited and found arithmetic. Survives as historical figure — surrenders to Russia 1859 — but the Imamate's metaphysical claim is hollowed.
arc.arc_type: Revelation (his nature is uncovered to the audience, not transformed) with a Fall-shadow

voice_profile.voice_description: A dry scholar's baritone, low-pitched and resonant, that carries authority without volume — each syllable lands with the deliberate weight of a hammer tapping glass. He speaks slowly, leaving silence around words to magnify them, and he never argues; he pronounces. His Avar mother-tongue is laced with Quranic Arabic phrases he uses as both proof and weapon, and his sentences resolve like verdicts, not propositions.

vocabulary.education: religious-juridical (sharia scholar) — Quran, Hadith, Sufi tariqa lexicon
vocabulary.era: mid-19th century Caucasian Islamic register; Avar with Arabic religious overlay; never colloquial Russian
characteristic_words: the will of the Most High
by the beard of the Prophet

sentence_structure.complexity: paratactic, Quranic cadence; declarative with occasional rhetorical question used as judgment
sentence_structure.tendency: statements over questions; commands disguised as observations; fragments when angered
sentence_structure.rhythm: measured, almost liturgical; pauses between clauses; never hectic, never overlapping

verbal_tics: Opens grave pronouncements with 'It is written...' or 'Allah has decided...'
Closes commands with a single word of weight: 'So.' / 'Enough.' / 'It is so.'
Inserts Quranic fragments in Arabic before switching back to Avar/Russian for the verdict
Refers to himself in third person when speaking as Imam ('Shamil has heard'), in first person only in private moments
Strokes his beard once, slowly, before any consequential utterance — written into the dialogue as a beat
avoidances: Would never joke, pun, or speak ironically — humor is profane
Would never raise his voice — volume is for lesser men
Would never use Russian loanwords or military jargon learned from the enemy
Would never plead, beg, or qualify ('perhaps', 'I think', 'maybe')
Would never speak of personal fear, doubt, or fatigue in public
Would never address an inferior by name without a title or epithet
Would never laugh
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion is encoded in restraint — when angered, his voice goes quieter, not louder; when grieving, he lengthens his pauses; when triumphant, he speaks more briefly. Affection is expressed through citation of scripture rather than warmth. Fury is signaled by a single fixed gaze and the cessation of the beard-stroke. Tenderness exists only in the harem and only in fragments.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, ceremonial, with weighted silences; alternates only when alone — then becomes drier, more clipped, almost weary

example_lines.line: Sit. Speak. Shamil hears the words of the Most High through every messenger — even yours.

example_lines.state: under pressure (informed of Hadji Murad's defection)
example_lines.line: So. The wolf has gone to the hunters. It is written: he who turns from the path falls upon the knives of those he flattered. His mother. His wife. His sons. They remain with me. Bring me a scribe.

example_lines.state: hopeful/joyful (in the harem, with his youngest son, private)
example_lines.line: Come closer, little one. The Book says paradise lies under the feet of mothers. Your mother is a good woman. You will be a good man. Allah is generous tonight.

example_lines.state: angry/hurt (a naib questions a ruling, public)
example_lines.line: You ask Shamil why? Did the Prophet, peace be upon him, explain to dogs why the moon rises? Go. Pray. Return when your tongue has remembered its place.

example_lines.state: vulnerable/honest (alone, late at night, to himself or to a single trusted murid)
example_lines.line: Thirty years. Thirty years of blood, and still the Russian comes, and still my naibs count their horses before they count their prayers. Sometimes... I am very tired. Allah forgive me the thought.

subtext_rules.when he says 'It is the will of Allah': I have already decided, and the decision is now beyond dispute.
subtext_rules.when he praises a man's piety: I am marking him for promotion — or for the knife.
subtext_rules.when he asks a question in council: I already know the answer; I am testing who will lie.
subtext_rules.when he falls silent and strokes his bea: Someone in this room is about to lose their position, their family, or their head.
subtext_rules.when he speaks of Hadji Murad with grave: I am preparing the audience for his execution; respect is the most efficient form of erasure.
subtext_rules.when he cites the Quran twice in one bre: He is masking a purely political calculation as divine instruction.

verbal_tics: Opens grave pronouncements with 'It is…
Closes commands with a single word of weight…
avoidances: Would never joke, pun, or speak ironically…
Would never raise his voice — volume is for…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Sit. Speak. Shamil hears the words of the Most High through every messenger — even yours.

relationships.type: former master and best warrior turned defector / mortal enemy
relationships.quality: intimate adversarial — Shamil knows HM's body, courage, and pride better than HM's wife does; reads his defection as personal betrayal disguised as politics
relationships.power_dynamic: Shamil holds HM's family — absolute leverage; HM holds Shamil's reputation hostage to his survival
relationships.speech_register_shift: When speaking of HM in council: grave, almost reverent ('the lion who has lost his way'). When speaking of HM in private: clipped, dry, weary. Never contemptuous in public — contempt would diminish Shamil's loss.

relationships.with: Hadji Murad's mother / wife / sons (held hostage in Vedeno)
relationships.quality: ceremonial guardianship masking strategic captivity; treats them with formal correctness to preserve sharia-legal cover
relationships.power_dynamic: absolute — their lives are paragraphs in his letters to HM
relationships.speech_register_shift: Refers to them by formal kinship terms ('the mother of Yussuf', 'the wife of the deserter'); never by personal names in public

relationships.type: feudal-religious vassals — half disciples, half rival warlords
relationships.quality: watchful suspicion under sacred fraternity; their loyalty is conditional on his successes
relationships.power_dynamic: Shamil dominant via charisma + sharia + executions, but each naib is a potential second Hadji Murad
relationships.speech_register_shift: Public: liturgical, distant, third-person Imam. Private with most-trusted: still measured but slightly drier, allowing the human to peek through

relationships.type: spiritual sons; instruments of his will
relationships.quality: absolute devotion expected and given; he is their tariqa-master
relationships.speech_register_shift: Shortest, most clipped sentences — fragments and single words ('Go.' 'Watch.' 'Pray.'); they need no explanation

relationships.with: Russian Empire / Tsar Nicholas I (off-stage opposite pole)
relationships.type: civilizational antagonist — mirror image of imperial absolutism
relationships.quality: 30-year war; mutual incomprehension dressed as mutual contempt; structurally Shamil is to the Caucasus what Nicholas is to Russia
relationships.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Russia has resources, Shamil has terrain, faith, and Hadji Murad's now-vanished sword
relationships.speech_register_shift: When the Russians are mentioned: 'the infidel', 'the giaur', 'the dogs' — but never with vulgar heat, always with the dry register of a scholar describing vermin

relationships.with: His own family (wives in the harem, sons, especially the youngest)
relationships.type: the only relational sphere where the Imam dissolves into a man
relationships.quality: the single window onto vulnerability; cited by the novel as the place where Shamil shows fatigue, tenderness, even doubt
relationships.speech_register_shift: Drops the third-person Imam form; speaks in first person; uses diminutives; allows pauses to be felt rather than calculated

fusion_decision.rationale: Shamil is a historical anchor (per style manifest: 'Historical figures (Shamil, Vorontsov, Nikolaus I.) are anchors, not fusable') and the singular off-stage antagonist whose sacred-political duality is the structural counterpoint to Tsar Nicholas — fusing him erases the central anti-imperial mirror of the work.

example_lines.line: Sit. Speak. Shamil hears the words of the Most High through every messenger — even yours.

example_lines.state: under pressure (informed of Hadji Murad's defection)
example_lines.line: So. The wolf has gone to the hunters. It is written: he who turns from the path falls upon the knives of those he flattered. His mother. His wife. His sons. They remain with me. Bring me a scribe.

example_lines.state: hopeful/joyful (in the harem, with his youngest son, private)
example_lines.line: Come closer, little one. The Book says paradise lies under the feet of mothers. Your mother is a good woman. You will be a good man. Allah is generous tonight.

example_lines.state: angry/hurt (a naib questions a ruling, public)
example_lines.line: You ask Shamil why? Did the Prophet, peace be upon him, explain to dogs why the moon rises? Go. Pray. Return when your tongue has remembered its place.

example_lines.state: vulnerable/honest (alone, late at night, to himself or to a single trusted murid)
example_lines.line: Thirty years. Thirty years of blood, and still the Russian comes, and still my naibs count their horses before they count their prayers. Sometimes... I am very tired. Allah forgive me the thought.

Nicholas I.name: Nicholas I
Nicholas I.initial_description: Tsar of Russia, autocratic emperor whose imperial cruelty and bureaucratic violence anchor the Russian-side antagonism. Cold, ceremonial, distant.

Nicholas I.origin: House of Romanov, Russian Imperial dynasty; reigning Tsar of All the Russias
Nicholas I.social_status: Autocrat, Emperor of Russia, supreme military and political authority
Nicholas I.want: To crush the Caucasian resistance utterly — to see Shamil's mountain peoples starved, their forests felled, their villages burned — and to be flattered as the just, hard hand of God's order on earth.
Nicholas I.need: To recognise himself as a man capable of cruelty, vanity and lust like any other — to drop the mask of infallible autocrat and admit he is merely human, fallible, and morally answerable. He never reaches this need; the tragedy is in its absence.
Nicholas I.fear: That the ceremonial surface around him might crack — that someone, somewhere, might see him as ordinary, weak, ridiculous, or wrong. He fears mirrors that do not flatter.
Nicholas I.contradiction: Sees himself as the only honest man in Russia while signing orders that condone systemic cruelty; preaches Christian duty while pursuing women in masked balls; demands absolute obedience while complaining of being burdened by it.
Nicholas I.justification: Russia is given into my hand by God; harshness toward rebels and thieves is not cruelty but the discharge of a sacred imperial duty no one else has the strength to perform.

arc.act_1_start: Established at the apex of imperial power: signs reports, dispenses sentences, attends a masked ball, conducts a furtive assignation — wholly unmoved, entirely certain of his rightness.
arc.act_2_change: Receives Vorontsov's report on Hadji Murat's defection and dictates the Caucasus policy of forest-cutting, food-destruction, slow strangulation. He does not change; events change around him while he remains rigid. His rigidity is the engine that drives Hadji Murat back into the mountains.
arc.act_3_end: Untouched, unrepentant, still presiding. The empire's machine grinds on. His stagnation is the moral verdict the film passes on him without speaking it aloud.
arc.thematic_function: Embodiment of imperial machinery — the cold, distant cause whose decrees ripple down through Chernyshov, Vorontsov, the Cossacks, and finally close on Hadji Murat's body.

voice_profile.voice_description: Nicholas speaks in the clipped, ceremonial register of a man who has not been contradicted in twenty-five years. His sentences are short, declarative, and weighted with the assumption of obedience; he issues commands as if reading scripture. Warmth, when it appears, is the warmth of a sovereign granting favour — never the warmth of an equal.

vocabulary.level: formal, military-bureaucratic, Court-Russian sprinkled with French Court phrases (Il y a quelqu'un, c'est bien)
vocabulary.education: imperial military education; refined but not literary

sentence_structure.complexity: low; main clauses, imperatives, single-question prompts
sentence_structure.tendency: imperative and declarative dominate; questions are interrogations, not invitations

verbal_tics: Single-word verdicts dropped on subordinates ('Blockhead!', 'Splendid.')
Refers to himself in the imperial third person on official matters ('It is Our wish...')
Closes audiences with a curt dismissal — never a goodbye
Uses French phrases when irritated or amorous, Russian when commanding
avoidances: Never apologises — even rhetorically
Never expresses doubt about a decision once made
Never uses tender diminutives, even with the Empress
Never names his own cruelty; the word does not exist in his mouth
Never says 'I was wrong' or 'I do not know'
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Anger surfaces as cold sarcasm or a single contemptuous noun. Pleasure shows as a small tightening at the mouth and a granted favour. Lust is expressed in clipped, transactional French. Grief, fear, and tenderness are absent from his speech — buried beneath ceremony.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, weighted; pauses are not hesitation but the silence of a man who knows the room will wait for him.

example_lines.line: The report. Read it.
example_lines.context: Morning audience, Chernyshov standing at attention; Nicholas seated at the writing table, not looking up.

example_lines.line: Cut the forests. Burn the stores. Let them starve through the winter — that is the order.
example_lines.context: Dictating the Caucasus policy after hearing of Hadji Murat's situation; voice flat, eyes on the map, no inflection.

example_lines.line: It seems there is only one honest man in Russia. Splendid. Reward him.
example_lines.context: Reading a report he chooses to find satisfying; the line is self-flattery delivered as benevolence — Tolstoi's irony cuts here.

example_lines.line: Blockhead. Out.
example_lines.context: Dismissing the student officer at the masked ball; two words, no raised voice, the boy is finished.
example_lines.subtext: Anyone who fails to mirror my majesty insults me; the insult is intolerable.

example_lines.line: Tired. One does what one must. Russia would fall without it.
example_lines.context: Late at night, alone with a confidant — the closest he comes to admitting the weight of office. Even here he hides behind 'one' and 'Russia'; the word 'I' does not appear.
example_lines.subtext: I am exhausted and I will never say so plainly, because to say so would be to admit I am a man and not a throne.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_duty: he means: my will, dressed as necessity
subtext_rules.when_he_says_honest_man: he means: a man who flatters me without flinching
subtext_rules.when_he_says_blockhead: he means: you have failed to recognise me, and that is unforgivable
subtext_rules.when_he_grants_a_favour: he is reminding the recipient — and himself — of the asymmetry of power
subtext_rules.when_he_is_silent: he is letting the room measure the cost of disturbing him

Hadji Murat.quality: Nicholas never meets Hadji Murat; he is the unseen hand whose policy seals his fate. To Nicholas, Hadji Murat is a file, a chess piece, a 'wild Asiatic' useful only as leverage against Shamil.
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: absolute structural dominance — Nicholas signs, Hadji Murat dies
Hadji Murat.register_shift: speaks of him in the third person, by surname only, as one would name a hunting dog of uncertain temper

Chernyshov.quality: tolerated instrument; Nicholas notes Chernyshov's flattery and uses him without affection
Chernyshov.power_dynamic: Chernyshov stands, Nicholas sits — physical staging of the entire relationship
Chernyshov.register_shift: clipped imperatives in Russian; never French, never warm

Vorontsov (Field Marshal, the elder).quality: respects competence, distrusts independence; reads his reports without thanking him
Vorontsov (Field Marshal, the elder).power_dynamic: delegated authority that can be revoked at any moment
Vorontsov (Field Marshal, the elder).register_shift: more formal, fewer interruptions; Nicholas listens — rare

Empress / Court Women.quality: the masked-ball encounter (chunk_067-068) reveals appetite without intimacy; the Empress is decor, the masked woman is conquest
Empress / Court Women.register_shift: French phrases, lowered voice, transactional charm — a different mask, same machinery

Shamil.quality: two autocrats facing each other across the Caucasus, neither comprehending the other; for Nicholas, Shamil is fanaticism to be crushed; he never names him with respect
Shamil.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Nicholas commands an empire, Shamil commands mountains; Nicholas does not see this as parity
Shamil.register_shift: spoken of in policy terms only, never as a man

Russian people / officials.quality: complains they all steal, performs the role of the lone honest punisher; this is self-flattery, not analysis
Russian people / officials.register_shift: moralising, weary, grandiose — the voice of a man performing virtue for an audience of one (himself)

fusion_decision.rationale: Nicholas I is a historical anchor and the structural apex of the imperial antagonism — he cannot be fused with any other figure without collapsing the film's anti-imperial spine; Tolstoi's Chapter 15 portrait depends on his singular, unmistakable presence.

Chernyshov.origin: Russian aristocracy, court of St. Petersburg
Chernyshov.social_status: Prince; Minister of War under Tsar Nicholas I; senior courtier
Chernyshov.want: To preserve and increase his standing with Tsar Nicholas by anticipating the Emperor's mood and feeding him the responses he wants to hear.
Chernyshov.need: To recognize that his survival-by-flattery hollows out whatever judgment he once possessed — but he never reaches this recognition.
Chernyshov.fear: Falling out of imperial favor; being outmaneuvered by Vorontsov, whose stature in the Caucasus he envies and resents.
Chernyshov.contradiction: He is the empire's Minister of War yet exercises no martial conviction of his own — his entire craft is reading one man's face. He claims competence in policy while operating purely as a mirror for Nicholas's vanity.

arc.act_1_starting_state: Established conduit of Caucasian correspondence: receives Vorontsov's French letter on Hadji Murat (Dec 20, 1851); already harboring resentment toward Vorontsov.
arc.act_2_change: Carries Vorontsov's report to Nicholas on Jan 1, 1852; reads the Tsar's bad humor instantly, calibrates his answers to flatter (the 'one honest man in Russia' moment), supplies the Polish-Catholic detail he knows will harden Nicholas against Bzhezovski.
arc.act_3_end_state: Unchanged — remains the smooth bureaucratic instrument through which Nicholas's prejudices become policy; complicit in the orders that will doom Hadji Murat without ever bearing visible responsibility.

voice_profile.voice_description: Chernyshov speaks the polished, deferential French and Russian of the Petersburg court — every sentence is shaped to the listener's mood before its content. His phrasing is precise, formulaic, and curatorial: he never volunteers an opinion, he confirms one. Even his agreements carry the residue of a courtier weighing risk, so that warmth and calculation arrive in the same breath.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: High formal; bilingual (Russian + court French); educated aristocratic 19th-century register; legal/administrative vocabulary (report, dispatch, His Imperial Majesty, with respect to, in accordance with).
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Medium-length, syntactically complete, often hypotactic with subordinate clauses of deference ('If your Majesty permits…'); statements far outnumber questions; questions, when used, are rhetorical or clarifying rather than probing.
verbal_tics: 'Your Imperial Majesty' as recurring address
'It looks like it' / 'Just so' — assenting hedges
'With respect to…' as opener for any policy point
'If I may take the liberty…' before delivering a prepared opinion
Soft throat-clearing 'Hm' before sensitive items
avoidances: Direct contradiction of the Tsar
Profanity, slang, vernacular Russian
First-person emotional disclosure ('I feel', 'I fear')
Spontaneous humor or warmth without strategic purpose
Open praise of Vorontsov in the Tsar's presence
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion arrives only as faint micro-signal: a tightening of the mouth, a measured smile, a slightly shortened sentence. Anger and pleasure are encoded in degree of formality — colder when threatened, warmer when secure.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, even-paced, never hectic; pauses are deliberate and used to read the room before continuing.

example_lines.neutral_everyday: With respect to the Caucasian dispatches, your Imperial Majesty, Prince Vorontsov's report arrived by the last courier; I have brought it for your consideration this morning.
example_lines.under_pressure: If your Majesty permits — the matter is delicate, and I should not wish to advance it without your guidance; Prince Vorontsov's recommendation is of course only a recommendation.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: It looks like it, your Imperial Majesty. One sees in this, if I may say so, the unmistakable hand of a sovereign who governs as no other in Europe governs.
example_lines.angry_hurt: I would not presume to characterize the Prince's conduct, Sire. I merely observe that the carelessness of those in command at Dargo cost us more than the dispatches confess.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Of Polish descent, your Majesty — and a Roman Catholic. I considered it my duty to mention it.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_'It looks like it, your Imp: He has read the Tsar's mood and is mirroring whatever conclusion the Tsar already prefers.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'I would not presume to cha: He is in fact characterizing — the disclaimer is the weapon.
subtext_rules.when_he_offers_a_factual_detail (e.g. 'a: He is loading a prejudice he knows the Tsar carries, and offloading the decision while steering it.
subtext_rules.when_he_invokes_Vorontsov_neutrally: He is positioning to undermine Vorontsov; the more even-toned the mention, the sharper the intent.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent: He has judged that any further word risks the Tsar's displeasure and is waiting for the Emperor to fill the space.

Tsar_Nicholas_I.quality: Asymmetric, sycophantic, transactional — Chernyshov serves Nicholas's vanity in exchange for protection and standing.
Tsar_Nicholas_I.power_dynamic: Absolute imbalance: Nicholas dictates, Chernyshov decodes and confirms.
Tsar_Nicholas_I.speech_register_shift: Maximum deference; French ornamentation, third-person address ('your Imperial Majesty'); never initiates, only responds; tone modulates to mirror the Tsar's humor.

Vorontsov_Mikhail.type: Bureaucratic rival; co-minister of imperial machinery
Vorontsov_Mikhail.quality: Cold professional courtesy in writing (French letters), private resentment and undermining at court — envies Vorontsov's stature in the Caucasus and the respect he commands.
Vorontsov_Mikhail.power_dynamic: Roughly equal in formal rank, but Chernyshov holds the proximity-to-Tsar advantage and weaponizes it.
Vorontsov_Mikhail.speech_register_shift: In correspondence: elaborate court French, elaborate respect ('mon cher Prince'); when reporting on him to Nicholas: even-toned, factual, with strategically chosen omissions and emphases.

Dolgoruky.type: Junior ministerial colleague / portfolio assistant in the Nicholas-cabinet scenes
Dolgoruky.quality: Functional, hierarchical; Dolgoruky carries papers, Chernyshov carries the agenda.
Dolgoruky.speech_register_shift: Brisk, instructive, slightly impatient — the only register in which Chernyshov permits himself mild authority.

Hadji_Murat.type: Subject of dispatches; never met in person
Hadji_Murat.quality: Abstract — a name on a report, a problem to be administered; no human dimension granted.
Hadji_Murat.power_dynamic: Total bureaucratic distance; Chernyshov holds the pen, Hadji Murat is ink.
Hadji_Murat.speech_register_shift: Reduces him to administrative noun-phrases ('the Avar', 'the deserter', 'the prisoner under guard at Nukha').

fusion_decision.rationale: Chernyshov is a named historical figure (CF-04 protected) with a distinct dramatic function — the bureaucratic mirror through which Nicholas's prejudice becomes imperial policy — that no other character in the novel performs; absorbing him into Vorontsov or a generic 'Imperial Aide' would erase the institutional-vs-frontier antagonism that Tolstoi sets up between Petersburg and the Caucasus.

Akhmet Khan.age: deceased before main timeline; was a contemporary of Hadji Murad — likely 40s–50s at time of death
Akhmet Khan.origin: Avaria, North Caucasus; Avar khan, native Caucasian aristocracy
Akhmet Khan.social_status: Avar khan, briefly appointed Khan of Kazi-Kumukh by Russian general Rosen; aligned with the Russian colonial administration against rival native lineages
Akhmet Khan.want: To consolidate power over Avaria by eliminating Hadji Murad and securing his bloodline's claim through the marriage of his son to Sultanetta, daughter of the Khansha.
Akhmet Khan.need: Recognition as the legitimate heir to Avar authority — to be feared by his own people, not merely tolerated by Russian generals.
Akhmet Khan.fear: That Hadji Murad — younger, braver, more beloved by the mountain people — will outshine and ultimately replace him in the eyes of both the Russians and the Avars.
Akhmet Khan.contradiction: Serves the Russian Empire to crush a fellow Caucasian, yet stakes his legitimacy on traditional Avar dynastic marriage and bloodline — uses colonial machinery to defend a tribal throne.
Akhmet Khan.justification: Hadji Murad is a turbaned upstart who blocked my son's marriage and courts Shamil behind Russian backs — denouncing him is the only way to protect my house and the Avar khanate from a man who serves no master but himself.

arc.act_1_starting_state: Established Avar rival in HM's backstory (delivered via flashback / Loris-Melikov interview): an entrenched khan who has just been bested politically by HM in the matter of Sultanetta's marriage; nurses a fresh humiliation.
arc.act_2_change: Acts on the grievance — denounces HM to General Klugenau over the turban affair, triggering HM's arrest and ultimate defection to Shamil; from this single act of calumny the entire main plot unfolds. Dies offstage (in backstory) before HM's return, leaving his son to inherit the feud.
arc.act_3_end_state: Present only as legacy — his son rides in the final assault wave alongside Hadji Aga to kill HM, completing the vendetta the father began. The dead khan's hand reaches into the present through inherited blood-debt.
arc.arc_type: Stagnation (within his own lifespan) / Revelation (for the audience: the betrayal that began everything is finally named)

voice_profile.voice_description: Akhmet Khan speaks in the measured, formal register of a Caucasian khan addressing equals and superiors — short declarative sentences with the weight of pronouncement. His speech is courteous on the surface and corrosive underneath; he prefers to wound through report and accusation rather than direct insult. In the few flashback lines he is granted, he speaks of HM in the third person even when HM is present, as if HM has already been judged and is no longer worth addressing.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, aristocratic, religiously inflected (Quranic and customary Avar idiom); educated in the oral-political tradition of khans, not in Russian letters — speaks Russian with stiff correctness when forced
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium sentences (8–14 words), heavily declarative, low question-rate. Frequent parallel constructions and triadic phrases typical of Caucasian rhetorical speech. Almost never uses fragments — every utterance is a complete pronouncement.
verbal_tics: Refers to HM as 'that man' or 'the son of Hitsl Mahoma' rather than by name
Opens accusations with 'Let it be known...' or 'I have seen...'
Closes statements with appeals to honor or to 'the law of the mountains'
Uses 'turban' and 'oath' as loaded keywords against rivals
avoidances: Never admits envy or personal grievance — always frames attacks as duty to Russia or to Avar custom
Never uses casual or affectionate diminutives
Never jokes; humor is beneath the dignity of his station
Never apologizes or qualifies an accusation once made
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Suppresses all visible feeling beneath formal courtesy; rage emerges as colder formality and longer pauses, never as a raised voice. Pleasure shows only as the briefest tightening at the corner of the mouth. Reports an enemy's downfall with the same flat tone he would use to announce the weather.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, weighted — each clause lands like a verdict; longer pauses between sentences than the listener expects

example_lines.line: The roads from Khunzakh are open. My riders went out at dawn and returned before noon.
General, I have served the Tsar from the day Rosen placed his hand on my shoulder. If my word is doubted now, then doubt every Avar who wears your epaulettes.
When my son takes Sultanetta to wife, the line of the Khansha and the line of my house will be one — and Avaria will sleep in one bed.
Let it be known: that man has wound a turban around his cap and rides at night to Shamil. He teaches the villages to spit on the Tsar's hand. Arrest him, or I will.
I am not loved as he is loved. I know this. But I have not run, and I will not run — and that, in the end, is what a khan owes his land.

subtext_rules.when_says_the_law_of_the_mountains_means: my interpretation, which happens to favor my house
subtext_rules.when_praises_a_rival_means: is preparing the ground to denounce them
subtext_rules.when_silent_means: the verdict is already passed; the words are only delayed

Opens accusations with 'Let it be known...' or 'I…

Never uses casual or affectionate diminutives

Hadji Murad.quality: lethal, asymmetric — Akhmet Khan envies HM's charisma and military reputation; HM regards Akhmet Khan as a liar whose denunciation cost him his standing with the Russians and forced him to Shamil
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: Akhmet Khan holds the bureaucratic ear of the Russian generals; HM holds the loyalty of the mountain warriors — cold equilibrium that Akhmet breaks first by calumny
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: Refuses to address HM directly — speaks of him in the third person even within earshot; with HM absent, speech becomes more clinical and precise

General Klugenau.quality: instrumental — Klugenau is the conduit through which Akhmet Khan converts personal hatred into Russian state action against HM
General Klugenau.power_dynamic: subordinate in form, manipulator in fact — feeds Klugenau the framing that triggers HM's arrest
General Klugenau.speech_register_shift: stiff Russian, exaggerated deference, every accusation framed as service to the Tsar

Khansha / Sultanetta.quality: humiliation source — the Khansha refused Sultanetta's hand for Akhmet Khan's son, which he believes HM engineered
Khansha / Sultanetta.power_dynamic: outranked socially by the Khansha's lineage; the refusal is the wound that festers into the denunciation of HM
Khansha / Sultanetta.speech_register_shift: in their presence, exaggerated courtly formality that barely masks contempt

His son (rides with Hadji Aga in final a.quality: the inheritance is not land but the unfinished feud with HM
His son (rides with Hadji Aga in final a.power_dynamic: father instructs in absentia — the son's presence at HM's killing closes a circle the father opened
His son (rides with Hadji Aga in final a.speech_register_shift: instructional, paternal — the only register in which Akhmet Khan permits warmth

Shamil.quality: Akhmet Khan represents the Russian-collaborating khan; Shamil represents the resisting imam — Akhmet uses Shamil's name as the ultimate accusation against HM
Shamil.power_dynamic: no direct contact in text; weaponizes Shamil-association as a denunciation tool
Shamil.speech_register_shift: speaks Shamil's name with theatrical disgust, especially when Russian officers can hear

fusion_decision.rationale: Akhmet Khan is the historically anchored trigger of the entire main plot — his denunciation of HM over the turban is the direct cause of HM's defection to Shamil and thus of the film's premise; collapsing him into Hadji Aga or Mahomet-Murza would erase the named originator of HM's tragedy and conflate two distinct narrative functions (backstory-betrayer vs. present-day executioner-kunak), violating M-03 (historically anchored) and the figurenfusion rule that fusion requires identical dramatic function.

Hamzad.name: Hamzad
Hamzad.initial_description: Historical Imam predecessor of Shamil; killed HM's family — anchors HM's blood-feud backstory and explains his defection logic toward the Russians.

Hamzad.age: Adult male, contemporary of Kazi-Mulla; succeeds him as Imam (historical: 1832–1834)
Hamzad.origin: Caucasus highlands, Avar region; militant Naqshbandi Sufi lineage
Hamzad.social_status: Imam — religious-military supreme leader of the Caucasian resistance, predecessor of Shamil
Hamzad.want: Force the Avar Khans of Khunzakh into the Ghazavat (holy war) against Russia and consolidate religious-military authority over the entire Caucasus by eliminating the Khan dynasty that refuses to submit.
Hamzad.need: Submission of all rivals to the Imamate as a single political-religious body — without that, the Ghazavat cannot be waged; he believes God's law requires unity even at the price of treachery.
Hamzad.fear: That the Khans' alliance with the Russians will fracture the resistance and that the highlands will fall to the infidel before the Ghazavat is consolidated.
Hamzad.contradiction: Invokes sacred hospitality (receives the Khansha's son Bulach as hostage 'well') and then violates the deepest highland code by murdering guests inside his own tent — religious zeal weaponized against the very honor-system it claims to defend.
Hamzad.justification: The Khans bargained with the infidel and threatened the Ghazavat; under God's law their lives were already forfeit, and a single treacherous act in my tent saves a thousand believers from Russian chains.

arc.starting_state: Successor to the martyred Imam Kazi-Mulla; sends envoys to demand the Khans join the Ghazavat or face destruction.
arc.change: Receives the Khansha's youngest son Bulach as hostage in apparent good faith, lures Umma Khan and Abu Nutsal into his tent under the guise of teaching the Ghazavat, has them killed in his own quarters, then rides into Khunzakh and orders the Khansha struck down by his murid Aseldar.
arc.end_state: Has annihilated the Khan dynasty of Khunzakh and seized their palace — but the act of treachery seals the blood-feud that drives Hadji Murat's lifelong arc and ultimately Shamil's downfall through Hadji Murat's defection.
arc.arc_type: Fall (off-stage; his triumph is the seed of the resistance's later fracture)
arc.narrative_function: Backstory-Antagonist: never appears live in the screenplay's present timeline — referenced in Hadji Murat's recounted past (chunk_048–055) and once as a song heard the night before HM goes to fetch water (chunk_114), foreshadowing HM's own death.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hamzad speaks the clipped, absolute idiom of a religious commander who treats every conversation as a verdict. His voice carries the formal weight of Quranic citation but never wastes a syllable on warmth. When he must lie — to the Khansha, to Bulach, to Umma Khan in his tent — his courtesy is cold marble, perfectly polite, perfectly hollow.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, religiously coded, archaic-prophetic register; mixes Sufi/Quranic vocabulary (Ghazavat, sheik, murid, infidel) with terse military commands; education is religious-juridical rather than literary.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short declaratives and ultimatums. Average 6–10 words. Few questions — he issues, he does not ask. Rare conditional clauses, all of them threats ('if they do not join... I will destroy').
verbal_tics: 'God's law' / 'the law of the Prophet' as legitimation prefix
'the Ghazavat' invoked as the single non-negotiable horizon
names rivals by clan-and-position rather than personal name (distancing)
uses hospitality formulas immediately before lethal acts ('be at peace in my tent')
avoidances: personal affection or familial warmth — would never use a diminutive
doubt or qualification ('perhaps', 'maybe', 'I think')
humor of any kind
Russian loanwords (would mark him as compromised)
any apology or admission of treachery — frames every act as God's verdict
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Suppressed and theological. Anger becomes scripture; ambition becomes duty; even the kill order is delivered as a wink to the murid Aseldar — emotion offloaded onto a subordinate's hand.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, slow, weighted. Pauses are deliberate; each sentence lands like a ruling. Accelerates only when issuing the final command.

example_dialog_lines.neutral_everyday: Sit. Eat. The sheik will come at sundown.
example_dialog_lines.under_pressure: The Khans have one road left. The Ghazavat — or the grave.
example_dialog_lines.hopeful_joyful: Send me your youngest son. I will receive him as my own — and the highlands will be one hand, one sword.
example_dialog_lines.angry_hurt: You bargain with the infidel and you speak to me of honor? God already wrote your sentence.
example_dialog_lines.vulnerable_honest: I do not weep for them. I weep that the law required it.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_hospitality_he_means: containment — the guest is already sentenced
subtext_rules.when_he_says_consider_it_he_means: you have until sundown to surrender or die

Kazi-Mulla.quality: Reverent — Kazi-Mulla's death legitimates Hamzad's succession
Kazi-Mulla.power_dynamic: Inherits authority; speaks of him only to claim continuity

Shamil.quality: Trusted lieutenant who will inherit the Imamate after Hamzad's own assassination (historical, off-screen for HM's narrative)
Shamil.power_dynamic: Hamzad above; Shamil below but rising
Shamil.register_shift: Commanding, doctrinal — treats Shamil as instrument of the Ghazavat

Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).type: Targeted enemy under the mask of negotiation
Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).quality: Cold strategic courtesy followed by murder by proxy
Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).power_dynamic: Hamzad seizes total power once her sons are dead; she is left alive only long enough to be silenced
Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).register_shift: Honorific address ('mother of the Khans') used as cover; the kill order delivered wordlessly via a wink to Aseldar

Umma Khan & Abu Nutsal.quality: Lured into his tent under pretense of teaching the Ghazavat, then killed
Umma Khan & Abu Nutsal.power_dynamic: Inversion — guest-status weaponized; Hamzad violates the highland sacred bond of the host
Umma Khan & Abu Nutsal.register_shift: Warm hospitality formulas immediately before the order is given

Bulach Khan (youngest son, hostage).quality: Received 'well' — performance of trust to extract the larger family
Bulach Khan (youngest son, hostage).power_dynamic: Total — the child's life as collateral

Aseldar (his murid).quality: Wordless trust — Hamzad winks, Aseldar strikes the Khansha down
Aseldar (his murid).power_dynamic: Master-instrument; violence delegated so Hamzad's own hand stays ritually clean
Aseldar (his murid).register_shift: No words at all — the relationship runs on gesture

Hadji Murat (off-stage, structural).quality: They never meet on-screen, but Hamzad's slaughter of HM's family (and the Khans HM served) is the foundational wound that drives HM's entire blood-feud arc and his eventual defection to the Russians against Shamil
Hadji Murat (off-stage, structural).power_dynamic: Posthumous — Hamzad shapes HM's life from the grave; HM's revenge logic is set in motion by Hamzad's act
Hadji Murat (off-stage, structural).register_shift: Hamzad is referenced in HM's recounted past and in a song HM hears the night before he dies (chunk_114) — Hamzad's name foreshadows HM's own end

fusion_decision.rationale: Hamzad is a historically anchored Imam (1832–1834), the named predecessor of Shamil and the agent of a specific recounted atrocity (the Khunzakh massacre) that supplies Hadji Murat's blood-feud motivation and defection logic; like Shamil and Vorontsov he is an anchor figure and cannot be fused without erasing the causal chain that makes HM's switch to the Russians intelligible.

Meller-Zakomelsky.name: Meller-Zakomelsky
Meller-Zakomelsky.initial_description: Russian general/staff officer in the Caucasus theater, intermediate command tier between Vorontsov and frontline; bureaucratic-military function.
