"It's all about the same matter. ... Il a eu quelques desagrements avec le commandant de la place.  Simon a eu tort. ... But 'All's well...

Chunk chunk_042: ...from his son, who described Hadji Murad's surrender and his own encounter with Meller-Zakomelsky.  The princess came up and inquired what their son had written.  "It's all about the same matter. ... Il a eu quelques desagrements avec le commandant de la place.  Simon a eu tort. ... But 'All's well...
Meller-Zakomelsky.origin: Russian Imperial military aristocracy (Baltic-German surname pattern)
Meller-Zakomelsky.social_status: General, regional commanding officer in the Caucasus theater; outranks Vorontsov nominally but holds less real influence
Meller-Zakomelsky.want: To be properly recognized and reported to as the ranking commander — to have protocol observed in his jurisdiction.
Meller-Zakomelsky.need: To accept that formal rank without political weight is hollow; that the empire respects influence, not titles.
Meller-Zakomelsky.fear: Being publicly outranked or bypassed by a subordinate (Vorontsov) whose court connections eclipse his own command authority.
Meller-Zakomelsky.contradiction: Insists on the prerogatives of command while privately knowing — and being known to know — that his commands carry less weight than Vorontsov's suggestions.

arc.act_1: Off-screen authority figure; learns of Hadji Murat's surrender after the fact and is publicly displeased that protocol was breached.
arc.act_2: Summons Hadji Murat through his aide-de-camp to assert jurisdiction; the encounter exposes that real power flows around him to Vorontsov.
arc.act_3: Recedes into bureaucratic background — referenced in correspondence as a formality already smoothed over ('Simon a eu tort... All's well that ends well').

voice_profile.voice_description: Bureaucratic-military register: clipped, formal, weighted with rank-language and regulatory phrasing. Speaks in declarative orders and procedural reproaches rather than questions; uses passive constructions to depersonalize friction ('it has not been reported'). Tone is cold-correct, never warm — the voice of a man who confuses protocol with presence.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, educated, military-administrative; mid-19th-century Russian officer-class register with occasional French interjections of the court ('desagrements', 'Simon a eu tort')
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Medium-to-long declarative sentences; subordinate clauses for protocol citation; questions are rare and rhetorical; fragments only as terse commands
verbal_tics: 'It has been brought to my attention' / 'I have been informed'
Reflexive citation of rank ('as commanding officer')
'I require...' rather than 'I would like...'
Closes orders with a clipped 'That will be all.'
avoidances: Personal warmth or humor
Admitting Vorontsov's superior influence aloud
Casual or first-name address with subordinates
Showing curiosity about Hadji Murat as a man rather than a file
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Displeasure shown through procedural language and silence — never raised voice. A cleared throat, a fixed stare, a slow signature on a document. Approval is granted by absence of reproof.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, deliberate, with short pauses where a less rank-conscious man would soften the line

example_lines.neutral: The aide-de-camp will read the dispatch. I will sign it after.
example_lines.under_pressure: It has not been reported to me. I am the commanding officer in this district. I require the prisoner brought before me by morning.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: The matter is closed. Protocol has been observed. That will be all, gentlemen.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Prince Vorontsov has chosen to inform Tiflis before he informs his own commander. I will note it in the record.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: A general's signature is not always a general's command. One learns this. One does not say it aloud.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_protocol: He means: my rank is being ignored.
subtext_rules.when_he_cites_regulations: He means: I have no other lever.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'that_will_be_all': He means: I will not let you see I have already lost this.
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_a_junior_officer: He means: I am marking you as compliant for future use.

Vorontsov.type: Nominal subordinate / actual rival in influence
Vorontsov.quality: Cold-correct, surface-cordial, undercut by jurisdictional resentment
Vorontsov.power_dynamic: Meller-Zakomelsky outranks formally; Vorontsov outweighs him in court influence — a humiliation Meller-Zakomelsky must absorb in protocol-language
Vorontsov.speech_register_shift: Formal third-person address, French phrases borrowed from court register; never the warm familiarity Vorontsov uses with peers

Hadji Murat.quality: Procedural, depersonalized; treats him as a jurisdictional question, not a man

Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).type: Junior officer who 'erred' by not reporting first
Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).quality: Reproachful but ultimately deferential to the family name
Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).power_dynamic: Formal superior who must let the matter pass once the father intervenes
Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).speech_register_shift: Reproof framed as procedural correction, not personal anger

fusion_decision.rationale: Both are mid-tier Russian generals functioning as bureaucratic chain-of-custody figures over Hadji Murat with no independent dramatic action; collapsing them into a single Composite-Russian-Frontline-Officer (canonical: Kozlovsky) preserves the jurisdictional-friction beat (offended by not being informed first) while eliminating a redundant rank-tier per CF-01 and CF-09.

Major Petrov.name: Major Petrov
Major Petrov.initial_description: Russian fort major in the Chechen frontier sectors; embodies routine Russian command at the boundary of empire.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_079: ...agers are, Fagers are!"  Butler rode beside the officer next in rank above him, Major Petrov, with whom he lived, and he felt he could not be thankful enough to have exchanged from the Guards and come to the Caucasus.  His chief reason for exchanging was that he had lost all he had at cards and was...

Chunk chunk_107: ...hich the regimental singers performed and which ended up with a drinking bout.  Major Petrov, very drunk and no longer red but ash pale, sat astride a chair and, drawing his sword, hacked at imaginary foes, alternately swearing and laughi...

Chunk chunk_108: ...Major Petrov, very drunk and no longer red but ash pale, sat astride a chair and, drawing his sword, hacked at imaginary foes, alternately swearing and laughing, now embracing someone and now dancing to the tune of his f...
Major Petrov.age: mid-40s (estimated from rank and bearing; not stated explicitly)
Major Petrov.origin: Russian provincial gentry; long service on the Caucasian frontier
Major Petrov.social_status: Russian Imperial Army Major, fort commander on the Chechen line; married to Marya Dmitrievna; lives in spartan officer quarters within the fort
Major Petrov.want: To keep his small frontier sector running by routine — patrols, ambushes, evening drink, regimental songs — and to be seen as a competent commander by the men who live around his table.
Major Petrov.need: To feel that his life on the frontier still has shape and meaning; underneath the routine he needs the dignity that years of war and provincial obscurity have eroded.
Major Petrov.fear: Public collapse — that the next drinking bout reveals he is no longer in command of himself, his marriage, or his post.
Major Petrov.contradiction: He gives orders by day and unravels by night; the same authority that holds the fort together dissolves into sword-waving farce at his own table.

arc.act1_start: Steady fort-major: Butler rides beside him on patrols, lives under his roof, accepts his hospitality as the natural order of frontier life.
arc.act2_change: The evenings curdle — songs, vodka, the famous drinking bout where Petrov hacks at imaginary foes with his sword; the routine that defined him begins to corrode the household and his wife Marya Dmitrievna.
arc.act3_end: Stagnation in defeat: he remains in command, unchanged in rank, but the fort scenes have shown that the Russian frontier order is held together by men who are themselves coming apart.

voice_profile.voice_description: Major Petrov speaks in short, blunt military sentences with a provincial Russian flavour, the cadence of an officer who has given the same orders for fifteen years. Sober, his voice is flat, declarative, slightly nasal; drunk, it slides into singsong, repetition, and sudden shouts that crack the room. He prefers commands and toasts to conversation and almost never finishes a sentimental thought.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal-formal hybrid: military-formal in duty contexts (orders, reports), provincial-informal at table; modest education, frontier slang, no French ornament; era-appropriate 1850s Russian officer register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 6–10 words sober; under drink, fragments and exclamations of 2–4 words; heavy on imperatives and statements, almost no questions except rhetorical ones
verbal_tics: Brothers — at the start of toasts and rebukes
Well then — as a transition before any decision
By God — as filler oath, not blasphemy
Pour — repeated as shorthand for the whole evening's program
Enough — to cut off both subordinates and his own wife
avoidances: Never speaks of his marriage in front of other officers
Never admits fear of the Chechens or of his own men
Never quotes literature, French, or court manners
Never apologises after a drunken scene — pretends it did not happen
Never speaks tenderly to Marya Dmitrievna in public
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion comes out sideways: as a louder order, a harder drink, a sudden embrace of whoever is nearest, or a sword drawn against an imaginary enemy. Affection is gruff and physical — a hand on the shoulder, a refilled glass — never named.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and clipped on duty; alternating to hectic and looping under drink, with long silences between bursts

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Butler. Saddle up. We ride the line at first light — same as yesterday, same as tomorrow.
example_lines.under_pressure: Hold the gate. Two men on the wall, the rest with me. No firing until I say. By God, no firing until I say.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Brothers — the song! Pour, pour, fill them properly. Tonight the fort drinks like men, not like clerks.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Enough. Sit down. You think you know this country better than I do? I've buried better officers than you on this line.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Marya. Don't look at me like that. The frontier eats a man slowly. I'm still here, aren't I. I'm still here.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_pour_more: He means: do not let me notice what this evening is becoming.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_routine_patrol: He means: I am still in command of something, even if only of a road.
subtext_rules.when_he_shouts_at_imaginary_foes: He means: the real enemies — boredom, his marriage, his own decline — cannot be cut down with a sword.
subtext_rules.when_he_calls_Butler_brother: He means: stay close, you are the only witness who still sees me as a soldier and not as a drunk.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_with_Marya_Dmitriev: He means: I know what I am doing to this house, and I have no language to stop it.

Butler.quality: Warm, gruff, paternal; Butler lives under his roof and rides beside him
Butler.power_dynamic: Petrov is clearly above in rank and in domestic authority, but Butler's youth and Guards background give him a moral leverage Petrov half-resents and half-relies on
Butler.speech_register_shift: With Butler alone: more relaxed, near-confessional in fragments; in front of other officers: clipped, commanding, performs the senior role

Marya Dmitrievna.quality: Long-eroded affection encased in routine; she is the caretaker of his dissolution
Marya Dmitrievna.power_dynamic: Public: he commands; private: she manages him, his drinking, the household, and increasingly the fort's social fabric — a quiet inversion
Marya Dmitrievna.speech_register_shift: In public: terse, almost formal, as if she were a fellow officer; in private: shorter still, often only a name, a sigh, a single imperative

Regimental officers / soldiers.quality: Bonded by ritual — patrol, song, vodka — not by intimacy
Regimental officers / soldiers.power_dynamic: Unquestioned authority on duty; on drinking nights the hierarchy dissolves into shared chaos that he leads from the front
Regimental officers / soldiers.speech_register_shift: Duty: imperatives, surnames, no warmth; drink: 'brothers,' first names, toasts, embraces

Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.type: Distant counterpart — the famed Avar guest in the Russian sector
Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.quality: Curiosity tempered by professional wariness; Petrov registers HM as a phenomenon, not a peer
Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.power_dynamic: Formally Petrov is host and Russian authority; in human stature HM dwarfs him, which Petrov senses and covers with extra brusqueness
Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.speech_register_shift: Stiffer, more formal, fewer tics; the drunken Petrov never appears in HM's presence

fusion_decision.rationale: Petrov is the load-bearing commandant figure of the Butler / fort-life vignette (Section 22 strand) and the husband whose dissolution gives Marya Dmitrievna her dramatic ground; fusing him with Poltoratsky, Bogdanovich, or Serogin would either flatten the frontier-rot theme into one generic officer or destroy the only domestic Russian household in the script.

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Butler. Saddle up. We ride the line at first light — same as yesterday, same as tomorrow.
example_lines.under_pressure: Hold the gate. Two men on the wall, the rest with me. No firing until I say. By God, no firing until I say.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Brothers — the song! Pour, pour, fill them properly. Tonight the fort drinks like men, not like clerks.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Enough. Sit down. You think you know this country better than I do? I've buried better officers than you on this line.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Marya. Don't look at me like that. The frontier eats a man slowly. I'm still here, aren't I. I'm still here.

data.type: former imam and patron, now mortal enemy and captor of his family
data.quality: reverence corroded into hatred; bond of oath turned to bond of vendetta
data.power_dynamic: Shamil holds his family — absolute leverage; HM holds his own legend, which Shamil cannot replace
data.register_shift: speaks of him only with controlled formality, never by curse; the restraint is itself the measure of the rage

data.quality: mutual respect between two professionals of power; warmth without trust
data.power_dynamic: Vorontsov has armies, treasure, and time; HM has a name Vorontsov wants to display
data.register_shift: most ceremonious register — full titles, slow tempo, careful translation; HM gives him the warrior's courtesy due a worthy adversary

data.quality: quiet courtesy; she sees the man, not the trophy; he registers it
data.power_dynamic: she has no political power but moral perception; he is briefly disarmed
data.register_shift: softer, shorter sentences; gifts instead of arguments; the only register where he comes close to gentleness

data.quality: the only Russian to whom he gives the chronological truth of his past
data.power_dynamic: near-equal in the room of memory; Loris listens, HM speaks
data.register_shift: longer answers, more first-person narrative, occasional Avar phrases left untranslated — the closest he comes to confession

data.quality: absolute trust; minimal words required
data.power_dynamic: patron and disciple; HM commands by glance
data.register_shift: fewest words of any relationship — orders in two-word fragments, often only a nod

data.type: son and central love-object of the want-line
data.quality: absolute, mostly unspoken; the engine of every decision
data.power_dynamic: the boy is the lever every other character uses against him
data.register_shift: Yusuf's name is rationed; when spoken, the voice drops — the rarest and heaviest register

data.quality: lifelong tension between her warning ('do not be Khan, do not be killed') and his life as both
data.power_dynamic: she has only memory and song; that is enough to haunt him
data.register_shift: invoked only in private or near death; the one register that admits failure

data.quality: courteous distance; HM treats him kindly but knows he is a guard
data.power_dynamic: HM is older, more dangerous, formally lower in rank — a Caucasian inversion
data.register_shift: patient, almost paternal tone; teaches by example rather than instruction

data.type: distant sovereign / abstract guarantor of the bargain
data.quality: never met; an idea HM invokes for leverage and discards in private
data.power_dynamic: absolute on paper, useless in practice; HM's family is not in St Petersburg
data.register_shift: the most formal title-laden phrasing in public, complete silence about him in private

data.quality: Outwardly warm, genuinely curious, fundamentally instrumental; admires Hadji Murad as one admires a rare animal one intends to keep
data.power_dynamic: Total — Vorontsov controls movement, communication, and ransom; Hadji Murad's freedom is a fiction Vorontsov maintains for both their dignities
data.speech_register_shift: Slows further, softens further, French recedes; he speaks almost intimately, as if to a brother-in-arms — which makes the betrayal subtler and deeper

data.quality: Respectful partnership; she is one of the few people to whom he reveals genuine fatigue or doubt
data.power_dynamic: Equal in private, public asymmetry observed in form only
data.speech_register_shift: Drops court formality; uses her name and patronymic, allows fragments and sighs, may admit moral ambivalence

data.quality: Loyal, deferential, strategically careful; Vorontsov knows the Tsar distrusts independent prestige and writes accordingly
data.power_dynamic: Total subordination upward; Vorontsov bows even on paper
data.speech_register_shift: Letters become stately and impersonal; the lyrical Vorontsov disappears into court formula

data.quality: Cordial, encouraging, occasionally testing them with a soft question that has a sharp answer hidden inside
data.power_dynamic: Absolute, but exercised through suggestion, not command
data.speech_register_shift: Adds 'mon cher' and first names; becomes slightly playful when satisfied, fractionally cooler when displeased

data.quality: Performed hospitality; he ensures their comfort to the centimetre while his officers watch every door
data.power_dynamic: Total but hidden behind ceremony
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks through interpreters with elaborate courtesy, choosing nouns that flatter their honor while binding their movement

data.type: former master and best warrior turned defector / mortal enemy
data.quality: intimate adversarial — Shamil knows HM's body, courage, and pride better than HM's wife does; reads his defection as personal betrayal disguised as politics
data.power_dynamic: Shamil holds HM's family — absolute leverage; HM holds Shamil's reputation hostage to his survival
data.speech_register_shift: When speaking of HM in council: grave, almost reverent ('the lion who has lost his way'). When speaking of HM in private: clipped, dry, weary. Never contemptuous in public — contempt would diminish Shamil's loss.

character_relationships.to: Hadji Murad's mother / wife / sons (held hostage in Vedeno)

data.with: Hadji Murad's mother / wife / sons (held hostage in Vedeno)
data.quality: ceremonial guardianship masking strategic captivity; treats them with formal correctness to preserve sharia-legal cover
data.power_dynamic: absolute — their lives are paragraphs in his letters to HM
data.speech_register_shift: Refers to them by formal kinship terms ('the mother of Yussuf', 'the wife of the deserter'); never by personal names in public

data.type: feudal-religious vassals — half disciples, half rival warlords
data.quality: watchful suspicion under sacred fraternity; their loyalty is conditional on his successes
data.power_dynamic: Shamil dominant via charisma + sharia + executions, but each naib is a potential second Hadji Murad
data.speech_register_shift: Public: liturgical, distant, third-person Imam. Private with most-trusted: still measured but slightly drier, allowing the human to peek through

data.type: spiritual sons; instruments of his will
data.quality: absolute devotion expected and given; he is their tariqa-master
data.power_dynamic: absolute and uncontested
data.speech_register_shift: Shortest, most clipped sentences — fragments and single words ('Go.' 'Watch.' 'Pray.'); they need no explanation

character_relationships.to: Russian Empire / Tsar Nicholas I (off-stage opposite pole)

data.with: Russian Empire / Tsar Nicholas I (off-stage opposite pole)
data.type: civilizational antagonist — mirror image of imperial absolutism
data.quality: 30-year war; mutual incomprehension dressed as mutual contempt; structurally Shamil is to the Caucasus what Nicholas is to Russia
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Russia has resources, Shamil has terrain, faith, and Hadji Murad's now-vanished sword
data.speech_register_shift: When the Russians are mentioned: 'the infidel', 'the giaur', 'the dogs' — but never with vulgar heat, always with the dry register of a scholar describing vermin

character_relationships.to: His own family (wives in the harem, sons, especially the youngest)

data.with: His own family (wives in the harem, sons, especially the youngest)
data.type: the only relational sphere where the Imam dissolves into a man
data.quality: the single window onto vulnerability; cited by the novel as the place where Shamil shows fatigue, tenderness, even doubt
data.power_dynamic: patriarchal-absolute, but emotionally porous
data.speech_register_shift: Drops the third-person Imam form; speaks in first person; uses diminutives; allows pauses to be felt rather than calculated

data.quality: Genuine respect tinged with romantic orientalism; pledges protection in good faith and watches that pledge erode upward through the chain of command.
data.power_dynamic: Simon has formal power (rank, freedom, language); Hadji Murat has moral power (he is the man who trusted him). The dynamic inverts the longer they sit at the same table.
data.speech_register_shift: Drops French entirely with Hadji Murat. Slows his Russian, simplifies his syntax, uses Hadji Murat's name often. The voice becomes plainer, almost reverent, when they are alone.

data.quality: Affectionate, slightly indulgent; he relies on her as the household's emotional translator while he keeps his official mask on.
data.power_dynamic: Public deference to him, private equality; she sees the costs on his face that he will not put into words.
data.speech_register_shift: Most relaxed register; French phrases return; sentences shorten; the only person to whom he will admit, in fragments, that something is wrong.

data.quality: Filial respect intertwined with professional ambition and a quiet anxiety about adequacy.
data.power_dynamic: Heavily asymmetric upward — his father is both parent and commander-in-chief; almost everything Simon does is, in some sense, addressed to him.
data.speech_register_shift: Most formal of all; rank-titles ('Your Excellency', 'father' only in private letters); reports rather than converses.

data.quality: Courteous authority; runs his staff by example rather than fear; quietly disliked by careerists who find him too soft on the Avar.
data.power_dynamic: Clear hierarchy, gentled by manners.
data.speech_register_shift: Crisper, shorter sentences; military register; the warmth retracts but the courtesy does not.

data.quality: His tragedy lives here. He believes — and continues to believe — that the system he serves is honourable enough to honour his word for him. It is not.
data.power_dynamic: He is small. The institution is total.
data.speech_register_shift: Written-dispatch register: passive constructions, precedent-citations, no first person.

data.quality: Nicholas never meets Hadji Murat; he is the unseen hand whose policy seals his fate. To Nicholas, Hadji Murat is a file, a chess piece, a 'wild Asiatic' useful only as leverage against Shamil.
data.power_dynamic: absolute structural dominance — Nicholas signs, Hadji Murat dies
data.register_shift: speaks of him in the third person, by surname only, as one would name a hunting dog of uncertain temper

data.quality: tolerated instrument; Nicholas notes Chernyshov's flattery and uses him without affection
data.power_dynamic: Chernyshov stands, Nicholas sits — physical staging of the entire relationship
data.register_shift: clipped imperatives in Russian; never French, never warm

data.quality: respects competence, distrusts independence; reads his reports without thanking him
data.power_dynamic: delegated authority that can be revoked at any moment
data.register_shift: more formal, fewer interruptions; Nicholas listens — rare

data.quality: the masked-ball encounter (chunk_067-068) reveals appetite without intimacy; the Empress is decor, the masked woman is conquest
data.power_dynamic: imperial entitlement; consent is assumed
data.register_shift: French phrases, lowered voice, transactional charm — a different mask, same machinery

data.quality: two autocrats facing each other across the Caucasus, neither comprehending the other; for Nicholas, Shamil is fanaticism to be crushed; he never names him with respect
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Nicholas commands an empire, Shamil commands mountains; Nicholas does not see this as parity
data.register_shift: spoken of in policy terms only, never as a man

data.quality: complains they all steal, performs the role of the lone honest punisher; this is self-flattery, not analysis
data.power_dynamic: total
data.register_shift: moralising, weary, grandiose — the voice of a man performing virtue for an audience of one (himself)

data.quality: Reverent — Kazi-Mulla's death legitimates Hamzad's succession
data.power_dynamic: Inherits authority; speaks of him only to claim continuity
data.register_shift: Sacred-elegiac when invoked

data.quality: Trusted lieutenant who will inherit the Imamate after Hamzad's own assassination (historical, off-screen for HM's narrative)
data.power_dynamic: Hamzad above; Shamil below but rising
data.register_shift: Commanding, doctrinal — treats Shamil as instrument of the Ghazavat

data.type: Targeted enemy under the mask of negotiation
data.quality: Cold strategic courtesy followed by murder by proxy
data.power_dynamic: Hamzad seizes total power once her sons are dead; she is left alive only long enough to be silenced
data.register_shift: Honorific address ('mother of the Khans') used as cover; the kill order delivered wordlessly via a wink to Aseldar

data.quality: Lured into his tent under pretense of teaching the Ghazavat, then killed
data.power_dynamic: Inversion — guest-status weaponized; Hamzad violates the highland sacred bond of the host
data.register_shift: Warm hospitality formulas immediately before the order is given

data.quality: Received 'well' — performance of trust to extract the larger family
data.power_dynamic: Total — the child's life as collateral
data.register_shift: Paternal warmth used as bait

data.quality: Wordless trust — Hamzad winks, Aseldar strikes the Khansha down
data.power_dynamic: Master-instrument; violence delegated so Hamzad's own hand stays ritually clean
data.register_shift: No words at all — the relationship runs on gesture

data.quality: They never meet on-screen, but Hamzad's slaughter of HM's family (and the Khans HM served) is the foundational wound that drives HM's entire blood-feud arc and his eventual defection to the Russians against Shamil
data.power_dynamic: Posthumous — Hamzad shapes HM's life from the grave; HM's revenge logic is set in motion by Hamzad's act
data.register_shift: Hamzad is referenced in HM's recounted past and in a song HM hears the night before he dies (chunk_114) — Hamzad's name foreshadows HM's own end

data.type: Old friend, political patron and military superior
data.quality: Warm in private, deferential in form, mutually instrumentalizing in substance
data.power_dynamic: Vorontsov outranks him as Viceroy, but Baryatinsky carries the Tsar's direct mandate on the left flank — a quiet equilibrium
data.speech_register_shift: French phrases, first-name intimacy, longer sentences with rhetorical flourish

data.quality: Genuine attraction wrapped in social danger; never named publicly
data.power_dynamic: Asymmetric — his rank protects him, her reputation does not
data.speech_register_shift: Lowered voice, dropped formality, single-word sentences carrying weight

data.type: Strategic asset / colonial subject (no direct intimacy)
data.quality: Distant, instrumental — HM is a card to be played, not a man to be known
data.power_dynamic: Total in Baryatinsky's framing; he never grasps that HM has his own logic
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks about HM, almost never to him; uses third person and military designators

data.quality: Reverent, performative loyalty
data.power_dynamic: Absolute downward
data.speech_register_shift: Formal third-person reference ('His Majesty'), no irony, no qualification

data.quality: Cool, evaluating, occasionally cutting
data.power_dynamic: Total downward
data.speech_register_shift: Clipped imperatives, no French, no warmth

data.quality: impersonal at the level of conviction, charged with reluctant respect at the level of soldiering
data.power_dynamic: Karganov holds operational power (numbers, terrain, supply); Hadji Murat holds moral and martial stature — the inversion the scene exploits
data.speech_register_shift: shifts to the formal parley register — slower cadence, fixed formulas, no familiarity; refuses to dignify HM with his name in front of the troops

data.quality: correct, distant, demanding; loyalty rewarded with brevity, error punished with cold rebuke
data.power_dynamic: absolute within the district
data.speech_register_shift: rank-only address, imperative-heavy, no first names, no jokes

data.quality: deferential without warmth; sees them as the audience for his dispatches
data.power_dynamic: Karganov is an instrument of their will and knows it
data.speech_register_shift: formal written register; his speech tightens into report-language whenever they are invoked

character_relationships.to: Hadji Aga (Russian-aligned Avar commander, parley counterpart)

data.quality: uses Hadji Aga as cultural translator and front-line voice for the surrender call; respects his usefulness, distrusts his motives
data.power_dynamic: Karganov outranks but depends on him for legitimacy with the locals
data.speech_register_shift: shorter, more transactional; instructions framed as requests but unmistakably orders

data.quality: lethal, inherited, impersonal hatred dressed as duty
data.power_dynamic: subordinate while HM lives free; dominant the moment HM is unhorsed and exposed
data.speech_register_shift: addresses HM by full name only, never directly in dialogue if avoidable; in confrontation, drops to single-clause threat-register

data.quality: cold cooperation; mutual use without trust
data.power_dynamic: Karganov holds imperial authority; Arslan Khan holds the moral claim and the local knowledge
data.speech_register_shift: switches to broken, formal Russian; clipped military nouns; refuses any familiar register

data.quality: reverent, silent, non-negotiable
data.power_dynamic: the dead command him absolutely
data.speech_register_shift: softens only when naming them; uses possessives ('my father', 'my brother') as the warmest words he speaks in the script

data.quality: Severed bond carried as a wound he refuses to name; intimate knowledge of HM's tactics, body, dagger-style — used now as weapon
data.power_dynamic: On the battlefield: numerical superiority, Russian backing, parley-position. Morally: subordinate forever — HM is the one who chose, and HM is the one who dies standing.
data.speech_register_shift: Drops formal address ('Hadji Murad') to bare name in the assault; never uses the kunak-word; addresses HM directly only across distance, never within reach

data.quality: Functional, transactional, mutually distrustful; he is their instrument and knows it
data.power_dynamic: Subordinate in rank, indispensable in execution — the one man who can identify HM and lead the local levies
data.speech_register_shift: Strips honorifics, uses flat military Russian ('Polkovnik' at most, never 'Vashe Vysokoblagorodie'); shorter sentences, no Allah-invocations

data.quality: Authority by lineage and reputation; followers obey but do not love
data.power_dynamic: Absolute on the field, brittle off it — he must keep delivering victories or the levy melts
data.speech_register_shift: Imperative-only, no first-person plural; commands by name when possible to bind individual loyalty

data.type: Replaced — Hadji Aga inherits the parley-voice beat
data.quality: N/A — function consolidated
data.power_dynamic: N/A

data.quality: N/A — function consolidated; any informant/lineage detail folded into Hadji Aga's backstory or a single dagger-line

data.speech_register_shift: N/A

data.quality: indifferent
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Rosen is gatekeeper
data.register_shift: identical formal register, no individuation

data.type: predecessor / successor relationship in the Caucasus command (Vorontsov supersedes Rosen's failed approach)

data.register_shift: n/a (no direct scene)

data.quality: absolute submission

data.register_shift: lowers eyes, reduces speech to confirmations and reports

data.quality: rivalrous loyalty under same imam, then mortal enemy after the Khansha killing
